4. SYLLABUS
========================================================================
Faculty: this Canvas page is a sample. If your campus provides you with a syllabus template, you are encouraged to use it to modify the relevant information highlighted in yellow.

Course Name

College

Course Number: XXXX

My Contact Information

Name:

Preferred Contact Method:
email:

Campus Office:
Campus phone:

Describe your preferred method of contact. [for example: I prefer you to contact me via Canvas Inbox. I will respond to your note Monday through Friday within 24 hours. If you don’t hear from me within 24 hours, please resend your message. Weekend response times may be longer. I am not available during campus holiday periods.]

I have a virtual Student Hour on Thursday evenings from 4-5pm. I encourage you to make an appointment with me, if that hour does not work for you.

Our Required Text

Our textbook chapters and materials are Open Educational Resource (OER) materials (i.e. "free" materials) integrated into each module. Here is a PDF version of the texts we will be using:

- It's About Them: Public Speaking in the 21st Century

- The Public Speaking Project: Chapter 6

Course Description

Theory and techniques of public speaking in democratic society. Discovery, development, and criticism of ideas in public discourse through research, reasoning, organization, composition, presentation, and evaluation of various types of speeches including informative and persuasive speeches.

Course Learning Outcomes

At the conclusion of this course, the student should be able to:

- Explain the basic principles of human communication;

- Analyze their communication situation, audience, occasion, and purpose; and selection of subject matter;

- Formulate through research, analysis, and organization of material; presentation of the message including management of communication apprehension; and evaluation of the effectiveness of their communication;

- Demonstrate that they are careful and critical thinkers and communicators, both as speakers and as listeners;

- Explain their relationship and ethical responsibilities to others involved in the communication transaction.

- Articulate the connection between communication and culture and respect diverse perspectives and the ways they influence communication.

Your Activities and Assessments

Here are the specific activities and assessments you will participate in throughout the course to achieve the above course outcomes:

- Weekly discussions, 15 pts each

- Weekly Reflections or Unit Assignments, 10 pts each

- Practice Quizzes, points variable

- Exam 1: 40 points (required)

- Exam 2: 40 points (required)

- Final Portfolio

Course Grading

Add your course grading specifics here.

I encourage you to access your scores on individual assignments and/or your grade to date at any time via the Canvas Gradebook to monitor your progress and review my strategies for improving your future work.

Course Participation

Plan to log in to the course a minimum of 3 times a week:

- when the module opens (Wednesdays) , to check out the Unit Overview;

- by Saturday evening to post your initial reply to the discussion that week;

- as often as necessary to complete the required unit activities and discussion replies before the end of the work week (Tuesday evenings) .

Expect me to check in with you if you are not logging in regularly. I will drop you from the course if you have missed more than one unit (one week) AND you have not contacted me regarding your absence.

Our Learning Environment

Add your specific campus or course policy details, netiquette advice, etc. .

Our Work Policy

- You are responsible for keeping up with all course work, readings, and assignments.

- All of your work should be formatted according to 9th edition MLA style guidelines (unless I let you know otherwise) and this policy will inform the grading criteria for work you submit.

Submission Policy

Add your specific submission policy details.

Please communicate with me as soon as possible to coordinate submitting your work late due to illness or other unforeseen circumstances. Working together, we can create a plan to get you back on track!

Discussion Participation Guidelines

Discussions are the heart of this course. They are places to share your ideas and consider the ideas of others. You should plan to visit the weekly discussions at least twice during the week for posts, replies, and follow up. Discussions become "Read Only” after they officially close, which means you should still be able to review the discussion even if you were unable to participate. Though the discussion area is a bit more informal than a traditional essay, it is best to practice good writing habits. All discussion posts should pay attention to the basic writing rules and should be proofread and double-checked for accuracy.

- Discussion Initial Posts generally present a task for sharing or a question for consideration. I often require specific support from the reading(s) for your observations or interpretations in the form of direct quotes or paraphrases. These quotes and paraphrases should always be appropriately integrated and cited, applying 9th edition MLA style wherever appropriate. Discussions are a great place to practice your documentation! Initial posts after the due date are acceptable.

- You may notice lower rubric scores if you not follow the initial post instructions.

- Discussion Replies to classmates also have content requirements. Always address your classmates by their names. A meaningful response follows all reply instructions which usually ask you to make additional observations or ask questions that your classmate can use to consider the topic further or dig deeper into the discussion.

- You may notice lower rubric scores if you not follow the reply guidelines.

Academic Integrity in COMM C1000

Plagiarism may broadly be defined as trying to pass someone else’s work off as your own and can end up being more trouble than it's worth.

Plagiarism and unauthorized collaboration (cheating) will require a conference with me to determine the intent of the offense, and you will not receive a grade for the assignment until we resolve the issue.

- Unintentional plagiarism will result in a grade reduction on the assignment and will require a rewrite.

- Deliberate or intentional plagiarism and unauthorized collaboration will result in a zero for the assignment.

If intentional plagiarism is repeated, extensive, or particularly flagrant, I may also refer the matter to the Student Conduct Board, since plagiarism is a violation of the Students’ Code of Conduct.

Academic Integrity at Your Campus

Add any information with your specific campus policies on Academic Honesty.

Academic Accommodations via DSPS

Our college is committed to providing all students with equal access to learning opportunities, and I am equally committed to your success in my course. If you are unable to access any of my course materials please contact me, and I will work with you to solve the problem. Should you need additional accommodations, insert DSPS office name is the official campus office that works to arrange reasonable accommodations for students with an identified physical, psychological or cognitive disability (learning, ADD/ADHD, psychological, visual, hearing, physical, cognitive, medical condition, etc.).

Please apply online for student access services and select “New Application” and follow the prompts. If you need assistance or accommodations to complete the application, please contact DSPS on the XXX Campus by calling XXX or emailing XXX.

If you do not wish to register with DSPS , please contact the ADA Coordinator at XXX , to arrange for a confidential meeting to discuss services and accommodations. Be sure to contact DSPS as early as possible to allow adequate time for accommodations to be arranged. Please know that accommodations are not retroactive, so documentation of disability must be reviewed and eligibility for DSPS approved prior to approval of accommodations.

5. COURSE PAGES AND INSTRUCTIONAL CONTENT
========================================================================
## Read First: Explanation of Plug and Play Course
Resource ID: g5ac6da562eac8c0e166c60ca06d47518
Source: wiki_content/read-first-explanation-of-plug-and-play-course.html
Module placement(s): How to use this course (DO NOT PUBLISH THIS MODULE)

What is CVC@ONE Plug and Play

CVC@ONE Plug and Play Courses are courses that are ready to be used. These courses have been designed to align all CVC Course Design Rubric elements. The instructor simply needs to add a few college-specific elements to the course (explained in this unpublished module).

About this Course

This is a template course for a 16-week asynchronous Public Speaking course. The course has been created using the course outline of record for the C-ID course, COMM 110 which corresponds to COMM C1000. The course uses textbook chapters and materials are Open Educational Resource (OER) materials (i.e. "free" materials) integrated into each module. Here is a PDF version of the texts we will be using:

- It's About Them: Public Speaking in the 21st Century It's About Them: Public Speaking in the 21st Century

- The Public Speaking Project: Chapter 6

The course was developed a CCC faculty member who is a subject-matter expert, in conjunction with the CVC @ONE team.

Every Plug and Play course includes an instructor guide; we highly encourage you to read it since it is specific to the course you are adopting.

What This Course Contains

You'll find the following in this course:

- Weekly modules complete with learning objectives which could include the following:

- Activities

- Assignments

- Discussions

- Quizzes

- OER resources

Course Customization

While this course is ready for you to use, we recommend that you add a few details including:

- Your own, personalized instructional videos. A great deal of content has been curated for you, but specific, instructor-led narratives, instructions, or lectures are not present.

- Your own personalized text with lessons and other instructional materials: We've done our best to make this course free of personalized anecdotes and narratives, but you'll want to adapt a number of the content pages and assignment guidelines to fit your unique tone as an instructor.

- Your own rubrics or scoring guides if the included rubrics don't meet your needs.

Use and adapt to your heart's content!

Publishing the Course

Before you publish this course , please complete the following:

- Work through Module 0 (orientation module) and update all pages with your course and campus information

- Work through each page, assignment, discussion, etc and customize/edit with your specific course and campus information

- Some pages include highlighted areas that must be updated

- Review each assignment and assessment and adjust due dates and point values to fit your needs

The following pages in the module will provide more details about using this course.

Links:
- COMM 110 -> $IMS-CC-FILEBASE$/COMM%20110%20(C-ID)%20Descriptor.doc?canvas_=1&canvas_qs_wrap=1

- The Public Speaking Project: Chapter 6 -> $IMS-CC-FILEBASE$/OER%20Text%20Files/Chapter6_Critical%20thinking%20and%20reasoning_PSP.pdf?canvas_=1&canvas_qs_wrap=1

## Instructor Guide for COMM C1000
Resource ID: ge9d7c37615a3e63606838f5d9cde03b5
Source: wiki_content/instructor-guide-for-comm-c1000.html
Module placement(s): How to use this course (DO NOT PUBLISH THIS MODULE)

Weekly Overview

The course begins each week with a page titled “Week X Overview.” Please see below to learn more about this page.

Weekly Overview Notes

Preparation: Each week, the "preparation" section of the overview will describe the steps to set you up for success in the week, as well as any special considerations (such as coordinating speech times).

Key Deliverables: Each assignment due is listed on your To-do list, noted on the syllabus and placed in our modules. The "Key Deliverables" section of each week is a space where I'll highlight some points of considerations of specific tips. Going to the respective assignment space will give you a more comprehensive look at the guidelines. Think of it like this: The "Preparation" is more general, while "Key Deliverables" is more specific.

Why it Matters: The last item under our weekly overviews is the "Why It Matters" section. As instructors, it's obvious to us why the course concepts are important, but we don't always do the best job of articulating that to you all. This is the section where I'll highlight the relevance of the week's lesson and the value of us even spending time on it.

Speech Guidelines

The course includes a module title “Speech Guidelines.” The information in this module provides “Resources & Guides” and “Presentation Criteria” that students will use throughout the course to help them complete the course assignments.

Important Note About COMM Courses

Many colleges have different approaches to handling the speech requirement for an asynchronous online oral communication course. This course was designed with specific attention to the language that states the following:

Create and deliver summative faculty-supervised, faculty-evaluated, oral presentations in front of a live audience (one to many), including an Informative speech of at least five minutes and a Persuasive speech of at least six minutes in length

If your department or college guidelines have interpreted this differently, then adjust accordingly.

## Finding Grades and Feedback

Finding Grades

The Grades link displays your grades for all course assignments. Please view the following video to see how to find your Grades.

Grades and Assignment Feedback (Text-based Instructions)

- Log into the course and click on the 'Grades' link in the Course Navigation.

- Find the assignment and click the assignment name.

- Click on 'View Feedback' to view all feedback including comments and any additional annotated comments made on the submission. You can download a pdf with the comments by selecting the download icon.

- Click on "Show Rubric" beneath the score to view the rubric and my comment there as well.

Using My Feedback and Suggestions

When I evaluate your work, I always provide lots of comments in the rubric to provide more context for the score, so it is important to view this feedback to help you improve your work going forward. My expectation is that students will use this feedback to improve their learning and performance.

- I will provide feedback to you for all assignments and graded discussions within 3-5 days .

- Research papers, longer written works, and major projects may take longer, but I will generally provide feedback within 7 days .

- I will be using a rubric to grade your assignments and discussions.

- I will add comments and annotations on your submitted files.

- I may also provide video feedback on your assignment, discussion, or exam.

## Our Communication Plan
Resource ID: g5f80860baa31607de609aadb3b541a1f
Source: wiki_content/our-communication-plan.html
Module placement(s): Module 0 Orientation: Welcome to Your Course

[Customize this information to your specific communication style and include course specific links where appropriate]

I hope you will take advantage of the many opportunities to meet or communicate with me about any aspect of our course. I value an interactive and personal learning environment. I want to make sure you know that you're not alone in the course. I care about you, and I care about supporting your learning.

I respond to messages within 24 hours, Monday through Friday. If you don't hear from me within 24 hours, please resend your message. I am not generally available on campus holidays. If you message me on Friday evening or on the weekend, responses may take a bit longer.

Best Ways to Contact Me

You can communicate with me via phone, your school email account, or the Canvas Inbox. Feel free to leave a message at any time and I will get back to you within 24- 48 hours.

E-mail: [ your e-mail here]
Phone: [Office extension or phone here]

Here are some of the ways I'll connect with you throughout the semester:

General Announcements

I use general announcements to let you know when a new module opens, to send assignment reminders, or to share information I think will be valuable for you. I also use announcements to feature useful resources that will help you as a student or as a human being. If we need to make any changes to our semester schedule, I'll also post these in the announcements area.

Weekly Videos

Every week, I'll record a brand new video for you where I briefly preview the module content, assigned readings, and provide context for your work.

Gradebook Feedback

Not only will you find your grades and rubric scores in our gradebook, but I also use the comments area in the assignment to ask questions and spark further conversations about your ideas and contributions. I also use the messaging feature in the gradebook to make additional comments and observations to groups of students.

I do my best to grade discussion and reflection assignments and offer feedback within 3-5 days. I will let you know via an announcement if grading is delayed, and provide a new timeline for feedback.

- How do I view grades and instructor comments?

- How do I view annotation feedback in an assignment?

Canvas Inbox

If I need to message you this semester, I will use all of the tools within Canvas, so you can expect me to also contact you through Canvas Inbox if necessary. You can access this feature by clicking the "Inbox" in the left-hand global navigation bar.

Weekly Student Hour

Each week, I host drop-in student hours. Everyone is welcome to ask a question, get assignment clarification, or just discuss something from the reading or from the module. Please review the syllabus, welcome letter, and welcome announcement for our scheduled Student Hours. If the scheduled times and days do not work for you, message me to schedule a a different time and day to meet.

Discussion

I monitor our weekly discussions daily and will chime in with opinions and clarifications as it seems appropriate. The Course Related Questions forum is where I encourage you to post general questions about the class - and feel free to answer someone's question when you can!

There is also a Student Lounge forum for you to converse with classmates about appropriate topics other than those related to the course material.

## Participating in Discussions
Resource ID: gd801958c82b64ce4f272a2def9b52f4b
Source: wiki_content/participating-in-discussions.html
Module placement(s): Module 0 Orientation: Welcome to Your Course

[Customize this information to your course; ensure it matches information in your official syllabus]

Participating in Discussions

Discussions allow you to apply, work with, and discuss the ideas and concepts presented in each unit. You should expect to participate at least twice during the week: once to contribute your initial post, and then again to reply to your classmates' ideas.

If you miss a discussion opportunity, you won't be able to "make it up," as per our work policy. But all Discussions become "Read Only” after the due date, so you should still be able to review the conversations even if you were unable to participate.

Initial Posts

Discussion Initial Posts have specific word requirements and generally present a specific task or question related to the unit content. I always require.... Late initial posts are ...

If your posts don't follow post instructions, you may notice lower overall rubric scores, and I will usually provide you with some suggestions or tips for how to improve your work.

Tip: Though the discussion area is a bit more informal than a traditional essay, it is best to practice good writing habits. All writing for the course should pay attention to the basic rules, and should be proofread and double-checked for accuracy.

Replies

Discussion Replies to classmates also have content requirements, so read instructions carefully. Even though it is tempting, please do not simply tell someone how wonderful their post is or that you “could not have said it better yourself” and leave it at that! Be sure to follow all instructions.

A meaningful response:

- follows the reply instructions;

- advances the discussion;

- makes additional observations;

- asks questions and digs deeper.

If your replies don't follow reply instructions, you may notice lower overall rubric scores, and I will usually provide you with some suggestions or tips for how to improve.

Discussion Etiquette

- Respectful Communication : Always address classmates and instructors with respect. Avoid using offensive language and be mindful of tone, as written words can sometimes be misinterpreted.

- Constructive Feedback : Provide feedback that is constructive and supportive. Aim to contribute to the learning experience by offering thoughtful insights and questions.

- Stay On Topic : Ensure that your comments and questions are relevant to the discussion topic. This helps maintain a focused and productive conversation.

- Proper Grammar and Spelling : Use proper grammar and spelling to ensure clarity. This shows respect for your audience and helps convey your ideas more effectively.

Grading

All discussions are graded by a rubric. To view the rubric for any discussion, use the "more options" menu in the upper right corner to "show" the rubric. Use the rubric to make sure you have included or considered all necessary elements for that discussion.

Please select "NEXT" to continue in the module.

What are the basic computer specifications for Canvas?

My Support Role

I can assist students with assignment submission and general troubleshooting, such as not being able to open a link or file. I can also help troubleshoot technical difficulties during exams. However, I'm not your best bet when you run into technical challenges. In addition to the Student Help Desk options mentioned above, the suggestions that follow should be helpful.

Basic Computer Requirements

Having regular access to a computer with a reliable Internet connection is required.

Check out the technical requirements for your computer and browser to make sure that Canvas can function well on your system: Test your computer's readiness.

NOTE: It is also important to check your campus student email account regularly.

Browser Support

While Chrome is the preferred browser for most Canvas interactions, Canvas supports the last two versions of every browser release. It's highly recommended that you update to the newest version of whatever browser you are using as well as the most up-to-date plug-ins. Check out the Canvas Browser Support page for the most recent information.

Troubleshooting

Many computer issues can be resolved by following the steps below.

- Check to see if your browser is up to date.

- Completely shut down your computer and reboot.

- Clear the cache in your browser.

- How to clear cache on a Mac and PC .

- Try allowing pop-ups from Canvas.

- Try using another computer and/or browser to see if you have similar problems.

- If using a mobile device, try again using a computer.

- If using public wifi, then try again using a better Internet connection

## College Policies, Services & Campus Community Links
Resource ID: ge35d52ba9f75a7b4f694539993386bda

Institutional Policy: Academic Integrity

Include your college's Academic Integrity; it is likely AP 5500:
Cheating, plagiarism (including plagiarism in a student publication), or engaging in other academic dishonesty. For purposes of this provision, the term “cheating” includes, but is not limited to:

- Use of any unauthorized assistance in taking quizzes, tests, or exams;

- Use of sources beyond those authorized by the instructor in writing papers, preparing reports, solving problems, or carrying out other assignments;

- The acquisition, without permission, of tests or other academic material belonging to the College.

The term “plagiarism” includes, but is not limited to, the use, by paraphrase or direct quotation, of the published or unpublished work of another person without full and clear acknowledgment. It also includes the unacknowledged use of materials prepared by another person or entity engaged in the writing and/or selling of term papers or other academic materials.

## Guide: Presenting in an Online Environment
Resource ID: gda5eea56b70f89c1c9693eb694eee507
Source: wiki_content/guide-presenting-in-an-online-environment.html
Module placement(s): Speech Guidelines

- Overview

- Tech Check

- Presenting Online

- Camera & Lighting

- Visual Aids

Overview - Effective Presentations in an Online Environment

When we present online, it can be all too easy for speakers and the audience to take for granted many of the conveniences that presenting face-to-face gives us: We don’t have to worry if we’re muted on Zoom; someone’s ringtone going off is more likely than a leafblower; we don’t have to worry about our pets thwarting our attempts to present (ok, perhaps it’s not all bad).

On this page, we’ll be highlighting some expectations of our online presentations as well as tips and strategies to ensure your presentations go smoothly.

Speaking & Professionalism

Never forget that your audience is graciously volunteering their time and attention. While some anxiety is normal, it’s not an excuse for a lack of (or zero) preparation, which may lead to an audience feeling like their time has been wasted. As such:

- Practice! I suggest practicing your speech out loud a minimum of two times before your speech round.

- Time yourself during practice sessions: If you’re within 30 seconds of the designated time limit for the speech, then chances are you will risk going over on time.

- I will cut off speeches that go over on time: This usually means that any components that are missing (such as the conclusion) won’t receive a score, because they’re effectively not present

- Have FUN. A speaker’s enthusiasm for a topic is often infectious, and can serve to motivate and engage an audience

Being an Engaged Audience Member

Just as the speaker has a responsibility to be prepared, being a good audience member is also important: A speaker may already be anxious – please don’t exacerbate it further with rude behavior. Specifically:

- Log on to the Zoom Speech Round by the time our session begins, even if you are not presenting first. Additionally, I expect audience members to stay through the entire round. Leaving early without notice will result in not receiving credit for peer evaluations.

- Please try to have your camera on throughout the speeches. While I can deal with presenting to black boxes, your peers deserve the ability to practice in an environment that lets them gauge and respond to nonverbal feedback.

- Avoid the siren call of social media, your phone, or completing other class assignments (beyond taking notes or completing a peer evaluation): We’ll notice.

- Take notes! If there’s a question that pops up in your head, write it down and save it for the Q&A session. One of the best ways to show your engagement is that you paid enough attention to ask a question!

Tech-Check

If you've heard of Murphy's Law, then you know that if something can go wrong, it will. When it comes to presenting, technology is usually the first casualty of said law. Here, you'll see a few tips that will hopefully minimize the chances of this happening.

Zoom Client

One of the first things you should do is download Zoom conferencing to any device you might use to access this class. You can download the Zoom client at the following link: Zoom Client Download

Before joining as a presenter or audience member, be sure to test for functionality. You can find the instructions here: Testing a Zoom Meeting

Additionally, you can always set up your own meeting and invite a few classmates to test. Either way, be sure you can answer “yes” to the questions in the list below:

- Can you log on?

- Can you see your group mates and yourself in the meeting?

- Can they hear you?

- Can you hear them?

Device Access

You have a variety of tools at your disposal to serve as a recording device: Your cell phone, a webcam (stand-alone or built into your laptop), and a tablet. A few things to remember during presentation day:

- Make sure your device is fully charged, or at least able to be plugged in if necessary

- Make sure that your device has the latest updates installed (Windows, in particular, has a nasty habit of sneaking in an update when you’re not looking)

- If possible, have a backup device handy and ready to log in to Zoom just in case

Presenting Online

Many of the skills that you have learned from presenting in other classes will serve you in this one. However, there are a few additional considerations, as well as things people might forget when presenting from the comfort of their own home.

Attire

Be mindful of what you are wearing. Part of what I am looking for is a professional image. There are many ways in which you can achieve this, but at a bare minimum having a shirt that is clean and pressed will serve you well in having your audience view you as a credible authority for your topic.

Environment:

We do not always have control over the place we’re presenting at in a virtual environment. If you’re presenting at home, the reality is that many of us do not have an optimally configured place dedicated solely to presenting or remote work. The guidelines below, however, can give you some control over your environment:

- Perform in a space that’s free of clutter. To the extent possible, avoid open closet doors, a dirty room, a kitchen (unless it’s pertinent to your presentation), other family members, or pets (as adorable as they might be).

- Using a partition (a sheet or room divider) can help you create a clean performance space if necessary

- You are not required to use a space that allows you to stand and move, but we need to be able to see you from the waist up, at least.

- Once again, I emphasize the importance of professionalism: You should be comfortable, but avoid being too casual : This means sitting on a bed huddled in a blanket, on the floor, in the bathroom, etc. (yes, I've seen all of these. . unfortunately)

- As best you can, minimize any outside noise: We will need to be able to hear you (hence presenting outdoors, while scenic, may not be helpful).

Please note that not following guidelines may result in a reduced delivery score during your major presentations: Not as a punitive measure! It’s simply that all of the above are factors that tend to distract the speaker.

Regarding Virtual Backgrounds: You’re allowed to use virtual backgrounds, but be thoughtful regarding the positives and negatives. For example, an article in the Harvard Business reviews reported perceiving speakers who used a virtual background as less sincere and trustworthy in some contexts.

Camera & Lighting

Lights, camera, action! While I’m not expecting Hollywood level production quality, but how you position your camera, as well as your lighting will make a tremendous difference in how successful your presentation is

Lighting

You may not have a professional studio set-up in your speaking space, but follow the tips below to minimize the negative impact of poor lighting:

- Make sure there is no glare from the sun or other external lights so we can see your face and facial expressions

- Make sure you have enough light to be clearly visible on camera

- Built-in webcams, particularly on laptops, may not have the best quality. Techwalla provides some tips on how to adjust the quality of your camera

If you’re serious about doing presentations over video outside of this class or producing better quality media, consider doing some reading on “ 3-point lighting” . LifeHacker gives some tips to do it on a comparatively modest budget, though the article is from 2015: Improving Your Photos and Videos with Affordable Lighting. If you’re looking for something more up-to-date, check out WebEx’s blog .

Camera

When presenting and being an audience member, you will want to make sure that your camera is at a fixed angle. Consider the following tips:

- Camera angle: Position your camera so that you are facing it straight on

- Your camera angle should not shoot from behind or offstage.

- Camera distance: Your camera should be far enough back so that we can see you at least from the waist up, and also allow enough space that you aren’t cut out of frame when you gesture.

- Speak directly TO the camera, rather than at the screen (so it won’t appear as if you’re constantly looking down)

- If using a laptop, make sure that it’s placed on an even surface

If you are using a webcam or mobile device to record and plan on recording videos outside of this class, then you may want to consider investing in a tripod, or at least borrowing one from a friend for this class. An inexpensive one will run you around $12-$15, such as this one on Amazon: TriPod for Mobile Devices

Visual Aids

Certain presentations in this class may call for the use of visual aids. This may be an object, a video, a handout, or even a slide show. Keep the following in mind when incorporating visual aids

- Objects : Make sure that your aid is nearby and can be included naturally in the presentation. We should be able to clearly see your object. Conversely, once you’re done using it, make sure to set it aside, off camera (one advantage of it being off screen is that we don’t HAVE to see it)

- Handouts : Zoom allows you to copy and paste links into the chat before or during the presentation. Consider utilizing this to your advantage by linking a Google doc or weblink, but make sure it doesn’t distract from your presentation. Alternatively, you can post it on Canvas in the space dedicated to our presentation supplemental material.

Working with Technology

If you are using PowerPoint or Prezi, there are a range of options you have available:

- Mobile Devices & Laptop Combo : Use your mobile device to join our Zoom meeting. While you perform, use your laptop to display your PowerPoint while you deliver your presentation. *Avoid* holding your laptop in your hands as you perform: Place it on a solid surface so that you can reference back to it as you perform.

- HDMI Connection : Use one device to present your speech (laptop or mobile device) while connecting another to a TV or other external monitor to display your PowerPoint or Prezi.

- ShareScreen : Zoom allows you to share your screen with the audience. However, if you are opting to go this route, I need advance notice, as well as assurance that you’ve practiced with this: ShareScreen will take you off-camera while you share the slides, so use this sparingly : We are trying to avoid a voice-over-text presentation.

Please review all five tabs before selecting Next.

## Guide: Creating & Uploading Virtual Presentations
Resource ID: gd9893c8a1d9cec0f2c9038ea2d785204
Source: wiki_content/guide-creating-and-uploading-virtual-presentations.html
Module placement(s): Speech Guidelines

- Overview

- YouTube

- Zoom

- Canvas Studio

- Google Meet

Overview - Uploading Virtual Presentations

Periodically in this course, there may be times where you are required to upload a pre-recorded presentation for an assignment. Above all else, my focus is for you to get something that is audible and viewable to me and your classmates. That said, there are some technological considerations to bear in mind. This page is intended as a guide to a few different ways you can go about recording.

One of the most direct ways to do this without leaving the Canvas environment is to use the Record/Upload Media function in the Rich Content Editor's toolbar. Using the following guides can aid you here:

- Using the RCE to record and upload media : This is intended for instructors, but the toolbar should be similar to what you'd see in when it requires an upload in a discussion thread.

- Submitting a Media File as an assignment submission : If it's an assignment that requires a direct upload

If you want to be more involved, then you can opt to utilize a conferencing tool (e.g. Zoom, which allows a free version), which allows for a more seamless presentation (since you're all presenting together), but requires a few more steps to get finalized, then click on the corresponding tabs to learn more !

Whichever route you go, please keep the following in mind:

- Waiting until the day of or night before to attempt to record and/or edit, especially if you're not used to technology, is a recipe for trouble. To the extent possible, try to plan ahead.

- Watch your video to ensure audio and visual quality. I empathize with the cringe that comes with seeing ourselves on camera (I don't like it either), but you can catch avoidable issues if you do this.

- If you're feeling especially anxious or having trouble, again remember that my office hours are an option: We can do testing and troubleshooting there.

On the positive side, if you're able to get this down then you'll have built some skills in digital communication, which is always a plus in our tech-focused day and age.

YouTube

YouTube is one of the more ubiquitous ways to upload your videos that most people are familiar with. Here's a brief video explaining the process.

If you're a person that prefers a text explanation and doesn't want to watch a video, then check out the following resource created by Communications instructor Norma Jones : Uploading Your Video to YouTube (PDF)

If you go the YouTube route, there are a few considerations I want you to remember:

- Enable auto-captioning for your videos and edit them for accuracy. Your classmates will be watching these after all, so make things easier on them -- particularly for people who are multilingual and those who might need other accommodations (if nothing else, this gives you an incentive to stay within the time limits -- less to caption!)

- Set your video to private if you do not want anyone else to see it. That's my default expectation, but if you want to put it out into the world so be it, but I want that to be your choice -- not an unintended consequence.

Zoom

Free Zoom accounts allow up to 40 minutes of recording before automatically ending the meeting, which should be more than enough to meet your needs for this presentation. The Zoom blog gives some instructions on the following post: So You've Made a Zoom Recording: Now What?

The upside to using Zoom (or any other conferencing software) is that you don't necessarily need to edit the videos together.

Once more, keep in mind that the Cloud Recording option is only available if you have a paid account: The majority of you will likely need to use a local recording, then share the file through cloud storage (e.g. Google Drive or DropBox. . or YouTube). Here's some instructions on that extra step of getting it to YouTube

If you're truly struggling, then in a pinch, you can record your session to the cloud if you join one of my office hours (I have a paid account; a perk of being an employee in the education sector). That said, my office hours may not work with your schedule.

Canvas Studio

Canvas Studio is another option that's available to us: Many of the course videos that I record are actually done using that platform. Now, depending on what institution you're at, you may not have access to Studio (many, but not all community colleges have it). The following guides can provide some help in using this tool:

- Embedding Canvas Studio media in discussion replies

- Recording Webcam Videos with Studio: Chrome/Edge | Safari/Firefox.

- Recording Screen Capture Videos: Chrome/Edge | Safari/Firefox

Google Meet

Another option you have is to record a Google Meet Presentation. Free versions do not inherently allow this, but it can be possible with an extension. One such example is found in this video.

There are other plugins (as well as tutorials) available for this, so please pool knowledge with your peers if you've come up with something better than I have here.

Please review all five tabs before selecting Next.

## Speech Sign Ups
Resource ID: ge7161875728f34b057ceb9e150eef96e
Source: wiki_content/speech-sign-ups.html
Module placement(s): Speech Guidelines

Note: It is advisable that you include instructions for what your speech sign-up process will look like. Some instructors use Google Docs for simplicity, while others use the scheduling tool directly in the Canvas calendar. The former tends to be simpler while the latter has the benefit of keeping students within the LMS environment. The instructions provided here are a sample -- not the ones you need to stick with.

This page provides information and links regarding our speech sign-ups. As noted on the syllabus and our course correspondence, we have 5 presentations which will require us to meet over Zoom. We'll be using a shared Google doc to sign up for which session you plan to attend, as well as which peer you're evaluating (for Speeches 2, 3, and 5). Utilizing a shared doc will also allow us to more easily coordinate information amongst each other: For instance, if there's a survey or link you'd like your peers to see prior to the presentation, the document will allow a space for this.

Sign-up sheets typically open up 2 weeks before the presentation date. This provides a balance between giving you enough advance notice to plan, but not being so far in advance that you forget. The sheets close for editing on the Monday of the presentation week, meaning you're locked into the slot at that point.

## Speech #1 Guidelines: Your Dream Job
Resource ID: gf8fd1cd2f99388866abdbcc538e61cef
Source: wiki_content/speech-number-1-guidelines-your-dream-job.html
Module placement(s): Speech Guidelines

- Overview

- Directions

- Preparation

- Tips

Your Dream Job

Borrowed with permission from Barry Regan - Central Arizona College

For many people, acing a job interview is one of the most intimidating yet rewarding experiences in their lives. Additionally, while I hope that a large part of the reason you're enrolled in a public speaking course is to better yourselves and learn, let's also be honest here: Most of you are enrolled at De Anza to brighten your career prospects. To that end, have you ever considered that job interviews and presenting a speech actually share many qualities? For instance:

- In both, you're often discussing your beliefs and values with others

- You have to prioritize important information, rather than dump it all on the audience/interviewers

- It's a high-pressure situation that requires you to stay clear-minded.

There are of course, more parallels, but you get the idea. Therefore, in order for you to get the most out of your time in COMM 1, your first graded speech will be a practice job interview. For the purposes of this presentation, consider me and your classmates as if we were an interview panel for this job.

Directions

Reflect on a job that you are interested in obtaining. It should be one that you will be qualified for once you've completed your degree or certificate program here at De Anza (or a 4-year institution, if you're taking this class while concurrently enrolled at a CSU or UC).

Search for a job posting online (such as on LinkedIn or Indeed.com). You'll be referring to this job description throughout your presentation.

Your presentation should follow this structure:

Introduction

State your name, the title of the job, and the name of the organization it is with

Include a brief transitional statement (e.g. "let me explain why I am an ideal candidate for this position," "here is why I will be an exceptional worker for your organization")

Main Point #1: Work Experience

Briefly explain 1-3 of your past or current jobs. What did you do? Briefly describe some of your tasks and (if applicable) accolades you received while at these jobs.

Be sure to tailor what you say to THIS specific job. Even if your work experience doesn't seem directly applicable, it is your task to explain how it prepared you for this position.

If, for some reason you do not have job experience, you can reference community work, volunteer experience, or even coursework that aligns with the job title.

Main Point 2: Life Experience

Briefly explain 1-2 important or "life-changing" moments you've had that have given you the proper perspective necessary to do this job. You may want to consider volunteer work, travel experience, or a personal relationship with a family, friend, or mentor.

Conclusion

Restate the title and name of the organization (i.e. "Once again, I think I'm the best candidate for the position at

End with a proper conclusion ("Thank you for your time, and I look forward to hearing back from you soon").

Preparation

Prepare to speak for 2-3 minutes. I will cut off speeches at 3 and a half minutes to ensure everyone has the ability to speak. Prior to speaking, you will need to submit to me an outline with the following:

- The title of the job and the company

- A link to the job description

- A full-sentence outline (not script) for your presentation using the standard outline format: We'll review outlining later in the term; what you submit doesn't have to be perfect or extensive.

During the presentation, I advise that you stick to minimal speaking notes: Resist the urge to read your notes directly (whether it's physical notes or another screen).

Lastly, while it's not required, you can certainly dress the part for an interview (especially if it makes you feel more confident)!

Tips

This presentation is being assessed on completion: Meaning that I'm not using the same detailed rubric that I will be for other speeches. As long as you submit the required outline, show up, and complete your presentation, you'll receive full credit. Still, even knowing that, it's understandable that there still might be some nerves surrounding this speech. Here are some quick tips for this presentation.

- Don't hyperfixate: Remember, this is a dream job. While I'm not saying you should fabricate anything, don't get hung up on whether you currently have the exact qualifications. At a minimum, you can present as if you've already met the degree qualifications of your particular program or major.

- Practice: You're going to hear (and read) me saying this a lot, but the first time you meet with us shouldn't be the first time you're verbalizing the presentation. Try to do at least one dry run solo. If you can lean on your classmates to be an audience before the presentation, strive for that. I'm also willing to be an audience for a practice run if you come by office hours.

- Timing: A good general rule of thumb is that if you land right at the 3-minute mark in a practice run, then chances are you're more likely to run over, rather than under in the actual presentation. This means you might need to cut some content.

Please read through each tabbed section before clicking Next

Links:
- Overview -> #tab-1
- Directions -> #tab-2
- Preparation -> #tab-3
- Tips -> #tab-4

## Speech #2 Guidelines: Core Values Presentation
Resource ID: g0209134573dc48e22736d49d26d67ac5
Source: wiki_content/speech-number-2-guidelines-core-values-presentation.html
Module placement(s): Speech Guidelines

- Presentation Guidelines

- Outline Guidelines

- Samples

- Tips

Core Values Presentation

The purpose of this presentation is to explore, identify, and express at least three of your core values as they relate to your life and your community. Clearly illustrate said values and discuss how each of these shapes your life and your world in a socially significant way. Ideally, this speech should speak to issues of social, community, and/or cultural relevance and be something that your audience can connect with, even if they don't share the exact same value.

Each value will form a main point of your speech. To guide your speech, choose an object to symbolize each of these values, while addressing the following questions:

- What object have you chosen to symbolize this value? Why did you choose it?

- Why is this value important to you? Where does it come from (friends, family, your own life experience)?.

- How does this value shape your life and community (be specific)?

You may find it easier to construct this speech of each of these values follows a theme tied to a singular culture (e.g. military culture; gamer culture; racial or ethnic culture; spirituality). However, this is not explicitly required.

Requirements

The assignment page for this speech will include a rubric with the specific areas of evaluation. Beyond the rubric, though, here are some general expectations to follow.

- Speech Length: A maximum of five minutes. If you go under, that's fine, but going under 3 and a half minutes means it's likely too short.

- Delivery: Incorporate proper tenets of extemporaneous delivery: Natural and conversational vocal delivery, proper eye contact, pacing, etc.

- Posture: I'm not requiring to to be standing for this presentation (though some people find it helpful), but at a minimum, please be seated upright.

- Space: Please be well lit, stationary area. Please no recording while walking, or in a moving vehicle.

- A physical representation of your value is preferred, rather than a photo of it (unless the picture itself represents the value).

If you need a quiet space to set up your presentations, De Anza's library offers quiet study rooms if you are close to campus.

Outline Guidelines

Unlike your first presentation, which utilized a simpler keyword outline, a full sentence outline is required for this presentation. If you don't have much practice in constructing an outline, then you'll want to download the Outline Template as well as review some of the samples over on the next tab.

In addition, during our third week of class, you will be participating in an outlining workshop that will help you in getting feedback for your Speech Outline. Part of this workshop will require you to distribute surveys to your classmates to help in the development of both your outline and presentation. An additional requirement for this assignment is when submitting your outline, you are to submit a short write-up (150-200 words) detailing how the feedback from the surveys helped in adapting your speech to your audience.

Sample Presentations & Outlines

I am including a few samples from past students in the class. Not all of them are perfect , but they are posted here to give you an idea of how to lay out your presentation. Each of these uses the outlining template that you will also find in the assignment area for each speech outline.

- Sample Outline #1

- Sample Outline #2

- Sample Outline #3

Tips for Success

Here are a few tips for success with this presentation:

- Tech-Check : Make sure your technology is working beforehand

- Practice : Speaking out loud will help you work out some of the awkward wording and kinks. If possible, gather an audience to practice in front of (avoid practicing in front of the mirror).

- Objects: Don't let finding the perfect object as an aid consume all of your time -- at the end of the day, it is an aid and nothing more. It needn't be anything grandiose.

Good luck!

## Speech #3 Guidelines: Social Issues Speech
Resource ID: g46216569c0456e29ff0dbbb158ef64d2
Source: wiki_content/speech-number-3-guidelines-social-issues-speech.html
Module placement(s): Speech Guidelines

- Presentation Guidelines

- Outline Guidelines

- Samples

- Tips

Speech #3 – Social Issues Speech

The primary purpose of informative speaking is to ensure an audience’s clear understanding of the ideas presented. In this speech, you will be selecting a nonprofit organization, cause, or campaign (The Red Cross, Mothers Against Drunk Driving, and the Humane Society are a few examples) that you believe strongly in and inform your audience about it. Again, your goal with this presentation is to inform us about your organization/cause/campaign, not persuade (we’ll get to that later in the term!), so while I am asking you to pick something to care about, be sure to rein in the desire to convince your audience to take action. The body of your presentation should cover the following:

- Background information (the history; how it was formed; mission statement, etc.)

- The purpose of the organization, social cause, or campaign

- Recent work the organization/campaign has done, or things done to advance the cause

- Recent news (on the local, national, or global level) surrounding you’re topic

To have a successful presentation, drawing upon the lessons from our previous presentations will help you. In addition, I’ll be looking for you to incorporate a variety of supporting materials, as well as outside research and evidence. Where relevant, you may wish to incorporate a presentation aid (Chapter 13).

Requirements

- Your speech is to be no longer than 5 minutes in length.

- Adhere to principles of clear explanation (organizers such as signposts, enumerations, etc.), emphasis cues, and definitions were appropriate

- Include at least 3 valid source citations in the body of the speech

- Incorporate a range of supporting materials (clear examples, authority/layperson testimonial, statistics, definition)

- Utilize strong vocal and physical delivery (utilization of the space, gestures, eye contact / proper pacing, volume, enunciation, and pitch)

Outline Guidelines

This speech will utilize the same full-sentence outline format as the second presentation. Be sure to utilize the outlining template found on the assignment page, as well as reviewing some sample presentation outlines as they become available. As always, a detailed rubric will be made available on the assignment page.

Additionally, as you develop your presentation, you may find it helpful to incorporate a survey as you did with Speech #2 to better tailor your speech to your audience, though this is not a specific requirement.

Samples

This page includes some sample outlines for your use and review.

Sample Outline #1

Sample Outline #2

Sample Outline #3

Tips for Success

The same tips from Speech #2 presentation apply to here as well. Check your technology, do a dry run of the presentation, and practice in front of an actual audience if you're able.

In addition, however, factor in the following:

- Remember that your intent is to inform , not persuade (that's for later presentations). Refrain from making any strong persuasive appeals (beyond emphasizing the believability of your facts).

- Visual aids can be helpful, but use them sparingly . Ask yourself if it helps illustrate a point more effectively than your words, serves a specific function to grab the audience's attention, or saves an explanation time.

- Distinguish between what's "nice to know" and what's "necessary to know". 5 minutes won't be enough time to tell your audience everything about your issue. Your task is to let them know something they didn't know about it (if they have some knowledge of it), know that it exists (if they know nothing), or pique their interest in learning more (if you're hoping they get involved or care down the line).

- Pick something that's relevant and interesting to you . Remember: A speaker's enthusiasm can often be contagious (in a good way).

Good luck! If you've made it to this presentation, then you've got this.

## Speech #4 Guidelines: Your Personal Soapbox
Resource ID: g036bc100c642aae936eadc7a5db6e167
Source: wiki_content/speech-number-4-guidelines-your-personal-soapbox.html
Module placement(s): Speech Guidelines

Soapbox Speech

Adapted with Permission from Alex Kramer: De Anza College

(SLO 1, 2, & 4; CLO 3, 4, & 5)

This speech is an opportunity for you to speak out about something that either bothers you in society, or that you are passionate about. Think of this as an opportunity to get up on your 'soapbox' and speak to an issue in our world that you find lacking and needs change. You may also connect this to any of the previous speeches you have done, or use it to test-drive ideas for Speech #5.

I am not requiring any outside research for this presentation, but avoid making this into a rant.

Requirements

- Your speech should be 2-3 minutes in length. I will have to cut you off if you exceed 3 minutes

- Structure your presentation utilizing the steps of Monroe's Motivated Sequence (Attention, Need, Satisfaction, Visualization, & Action). You will submit a short outline detailing these steps.

- Your delivery should be extemporaneous; Please avoid reading directly from your notes

- Visual aids are banned for this presentation: Rely solely on your ability as a speaker and the power of your words

Like your first presentation, I am assessing this speech purely on completion, so think of this as a light reprieve before you get into your next speech. As long as you make the effort to present your ideas and don't go too far under or over on your time, then you will receive credit for this assignment.

Outline Format

Your outline should be structured around Monroe's Motivated Sequence (Chapter 16 of the Public Speaking Project discusses this). I don't need the extensive outlines that Speeches #2, #3, and #5 require, so simply follow the template below:

- I: Attention Step ( Develop 1-2 points on how you will grab the audience's attention)

- II: Need Step: ( Develop 1-2 points describing your issue/problem and why something should be done about it)

- III: Satisfaction Step: (Develop 1-2 points describing what you think should be done about the issue)

- IV: Visualization Step: (Develop 1-2 points that help your audience visualize either what your solution looks like, or what NOT implementing your solution would result in)

- V: Action Step: ( Conclude with having a specific call to action).

## Speech #5 Guidelines: Advocacy Speech
Resource ID: ge2b8ddab4f619e262b3cc625e691722f
Source: wiki_content/speech-number-5-guidelines-advocacy-speech.html
Module placement(s): Speech Guidelines

- Presentation Guidelines

- Outline Guidelines

- Samples

- Tips

Speech #5 – Advocacy Speech

(Course Learning Objectives 2 -6 / Student Learning Outcomes 1-4)

This speech is designed to apply all of the concepts you have learned thus far in this course. In this persuasive presentation, you are to select a cause, social issue, or nonprofit organization important to you and attempt to convince your audience to support it. You are welcome to, but not required to use the same topic you did for Speech #3. Be specific about your course of action: Do you want people to donate, volunteer their time, or, like you, become an advocate and spread the word? Your speech should be based on sound reasoning and evidence, while also providing motivational appeals relevant to the audience's needs and values. Your speech should include:

- Background information (history, purpose, recent things they've done)

- Why this organization, cause, or campaign is worth supporting

- What the audience can do to become involved or support them

Requirements

- The speech should be no longer than 6 minutes in length.

- Clearly incorporate at least one type of valid reasoning pattern.

- Utilize at least two different methods to establish your credibility throughout the presentation.

- You must use at least one clear appeal to audience needs and one clear appeal to audience values

- Your delivery skills are to be the best so far: Extemporaneous, conversational, energetic. (in short, persuasive)

- Cite at least four sources in your speech (remember, the rest of your audience doesn’t see your outline)

Outline Guidelines

This speech will utilize the same full-sentence outline format as the second and third speeches. Be sure to utilize the outlining template, which will be available on the assignment page, as well as reviewing some sample presentation outlines as they become available. You will find the specific rubric details on the assignment page. One of the main changes is that I will be asking you to highlight, within the outline, where your targeted appeals to audience needs and values are.

Additionally, as you develop your presentation, you may find it helpful to incorporate a survey: It's not required, but with a persuasive presentation, this may give you some insight into audience attitudes, which will help you better tailor your message.

Sample Presentations & Outlines

This time around, we're fortunate enough to have a few sample presentations! Now, these are from a few years ago, but the general topics still remain relevant.

[Note: These samples have been removed as the original instructor only had permission to share them with future classes, not system-wide]

In addition, here are a few sample outlines.

- Sample Outline #1

- Sample Outline #2

- Sample Outline #3

Tips for Success

Much of what you've learned to this point still applies: Check your technology, do a dry run of the presentation, and practice in front of an actual audience if you're able.

In addition, however, factor in the following:

- Your presentation should have a clear appeal for the audience to act or believe a particular position on something. Framing your thesis as a proposition of policy ("you should/should not support this cause") is often the easiest way to accomplish this.

- As always, be strategic with your visual aids. While they can help, the main power lies in you, the speaker, and how you convey your message.

- While your audience needs to have some background on the topic you are speaking about, don't get so caught up in explaining the history that this ends up being an informative presentation.

- Pick something that's relevant and interesting to you . Many people can pick up on authenticity, and if you're genuinely invested i the topic, then you will be more persuasive.

Good luck! This presentation is the true measure of what you've learned this quarter. Make it count!

## Speech #6 Guidelines
Resource ID: gab9242a2fdd319e31ed951278c752c8e
Source: wiki_content/speech-number-6-guidelines.html
Module placement(s): Speech Guidelines

Speech #6?! If you're reading these guidelines, then likely you missed out on one of the major presentations for some unforeseeable circumstance and weren't able to get into one of the other slots. If you've completed all of your speeches, then congratulations! You're not required to do this presentation and, before it gets asked, no, this isn't an extra credit opportunity.

Rather, this is a make-up opportunity for people who have missed a presentation and met the other criteria.

You have two options for this presentation: A tribute speech, or a life advice speech.

Guidelines

The first option is a special occasion presentation, while the second is more akin to a persuasive speech. You may find it helpful to review the textbook reading that corresponds with this assignment. For this presentation, you have two options

You have two options for the content of your presentation, noted below

Option #1 – Tribute Speech

All of us (hopefully) have someone in our life who, at one point, played a significant role in helping us become the best version of who we are today. This might be a close relative, such as a grandparent, parent, or sibling who always supported you in your endeavors. It might have been a co-worker or supervisor who gave you an opportunity to advance. It could be a friend, spouse or significant other who’s supported you through the best and worst times. It might even be a teacher who pushed you and was a positive influence in your life (so clearly, I’m exempt from this).

Whoever this person is, for this presentation, you will be giving them a tribute (or a “toast”) to honor them.

- Describe the person: Their name and their relation to you. If you’d like, you can also give a physical description of them as well

- Describe at least twokey ways in which this person influenced your life: Link this to a short narrative or story (this should be the bulk of your presentation)

- What would you say to this person if they were standing in front of you in the moment?

This presentation should be no more than 5 minutes in length. One can speak for hours on their loved one (Anyone who has heard me gush about my grandparents can attest..), but a short narrative can often be more potent than a longer one.

Option #2 – The Best (or worst) Life Advice

Whether it’s a close family member, friend, or a complete stranger you happen to meet, chances are that you’ve received your share of solicited (and unsolicited) advice. For this presentation, you will be sharing the best or worst piece of advice you have ever received with the class, and persuading them to follow (or not follow) the advice. Specifically, this presentation should:

- State the advice clearly in the introduction.

- Explain from where, or whom, you received the advice (Was it a person? A TV? A line in a video game? A fortune cookie?)

- Describe why you believe the advice is great, or why you think it’s a terrible piece of advice

- Mention why you think we should or should not follow the advice

You will have up to 3-5 minutes to complete this presentation. In addition, adhere to the following expectations:

- No use of multimedia (slides, video, etc.)

- Arrange your speech around a clear organizational framework

- Incorporate at least 2 outside sources (excluding the person who gave you the advice)

Grading Criteria

Regardless of the option, I'll be grading on the following criteria:

- Introduction: Does the presentation contain all of the elements of an effective introduction that we've covered during the term?

- Content: Does the presentation hit the content requirements outlined earlier in this page?

- Organization: Is there a clear structure and use of transitions in the presentation? Does it stay within the time limit?

- Citations: Are outside sources incorporated successfully in the presentation (for option #2).

- Conclusion: Does the presentation contain all of the elements of an effective introduction that we've covered during the term?

- Delivery: Does the presentation incorporate elements of effective extemporaneous delivery?

The criteria might seem less specific than previous major presentations, but make no mistake: Given that this speech is happening during finals week and you've had an entire quarter of both watching and performing speeches, I'm holding it to a higher standard.

Successful completion of this presentation will earn you up to 80% of the missed presentation. Better than not having the option at all, yeah?

Time, Place, & Audience

I will set aside time during finals week to hold a Zoom session where we will hold these presentations. I'll solicit times where we can get enough of an audience to make it similar to our usual speech sessions and post the time by no later than Monday of finals week. I'll do my best to come up with a mutually agreeable time that works for the most people, but please understand that I'm also going to pick a time where I know I'll be at my best to focus on the presentations with the attention they deserve.

In the event that we cannot gain enough people for a sufficiently sized audience, you'll need to find a live audience of your own (8 adults or more) to present in front of, then submit a recording of the presentation to me, as well as a response to the following reflection questions:

- Describe how doing this presentation was either more or less challenging than your previous speeches

- In what ways do you feel that our previous online presentations helped prepare you to present in front of an audience located in the same room as you?

Hopefully it won't come to that! But better to be prepared in the event that it does.

## Week 1 Overview
Resource ID: g868faf0bbe5d4c9a638f7fa426b40eae
Source: wiki_content/week-1-overview.html
Module placement(s): Week 1

- Objectives

- Preparation

- Key Deliverables

- Why It Matters

Objectives

Welcome to the first week of our Public Speaking class! This week is split between our course orientation and learning about some core foundations regarding the concept of public speaking, its evolution, and how that dynamic looks different in a virtual space as opposed to your standard face-to-face environment. Our specific objectives for this week include the following:

- Identify and describe key models of communication (transactional & interactional)

- Distinguish between public speaking best practices in a virtual versus on site environment

- Identify and describe rhetorical proofs, as well as rhetorical canons

- Compare diverse global perspectives on Public Speaking.

To the last point, I'd like you to view this video from the Stanford School of Business regarding cross-cultural communication.

Now, while the video did not explicitly go into public speaking, it did bring up a number of interesting points. Before you move forward, I'd like you to reflect on the following questions:

Is there a universal agreement on what good public speaking looks like? How might that definition shift, depending on where you are in the world?

Preparation

The first week is primarily about getting acclimated to the course. If you have not already, then I strongly encourage you to review our course orientation and review our key policies. Specifically:

- Ensure that you know how to navigate our course and use Canvas's basic functions, as well as setting up your notifications.

- Do a technology check! Make sure that your device of choice can record video and audio to your liking (this will come back by the end of the week)

- Reread our course syllabus: If something's unclear, then booking an office hour appointment or reaching out can help clarify.

- If applicable, get any special accommodations addressed by Disability Support Services.

Key Deliverables

- Discussion: Our discussion this week is a "mini speech" of sorts. You won't be presenting in front of an audience in real time, but it will be viewed by your peers. Think of this as a test run before we meet up next week.

- Applied Exercise : This week's applied exercise will give you an opportunity to examine your speaking space with intentionality: While it's easy to present from the space that's most comfortable (public speaking's nerve-wrecking enough for some people), sometimes that comfort inhibits how well our message lands.

Why It Matters

For this week? The rhetorical traditions lay a foundation for how many people in the U.S. approach communication, public speaking and persuasion. If you have a solid grasp on how these work, then you have the building blocks to make a great presentation, and heck, even a written argument no matter what context you're in. On a more practical level, learning tips and tricks about how to set up your room and lighting can elevate your presentation: Even if you aren't aiming to be a professional streamer or internet personality, just having a well-lighted, well arranged space can make you stand out when it matters (think: interviews, or other presentations where the stakes are higher).

Of course, just don't take it from me: Take it from the speaker (an apparent world champion of Public Speaking -- yes that's a thing) who speaks to this:

In particular, his point stands out about how much one has to spend in the "Public Speaking industry"; as someone who has friends that consult in this area (and on rare occasion in the past, has been a consultant), his claim of people spending hundreds, or thousands of dollars is accurate. So if nothing else, consider that you're getting quality from this class (..or so I hope) at a fraction of the cost, so long as you put in the time!
