## Textbook Reading- Chapter 11: Language (Section 11.3)
Resource ID: g05dd7c0cee11752c8cb636d02585a856
Source: wiki_content/textbook-reading-chapter-11-language-section-11-dot-3.html
Module placement(s): Week 12

Instructions: Read through the following lecture material, and consider taking notes. This week we'll be reading Chapter 11. Since it's a fairly straightforward and shorter chapter, I won't highlight the specific sections for you to focus on -- take it all in. We'll have additional material under the instructional content this week which will further highlight the concepts from the text.

11.3 Using Effective Language in Public Speaking

At this point, we will make some applications and suggestions about using language as you grow as a public speaker.

First, get in the habit of using “stipulated definitions” with concrete examples (defining operationally). In other words, define your terms for the audience. If you are using jargon, a technical term, a word that has multiple meanings in different contexts, or an often-misunderstood word, you can say at the beginning of the body of your speech, “In this speech I am going to be using the word ‘X,’ and what I mean by it is…” And then the best way to define a word is with a picture or example of what you mean, and perhaps also an example of what you don’t mean (visual aids can help here). Don’t worry; this is not insulting to most audiences if the word is technical or unfamiliar to them. On the other hand, providing dictionary definitions of common words such as “love” or “loyalty” would be insulting to an audience and pretty boring.

Second, develop specific language. The general semantics movement suggested ways to develop more specific language that reflects the imperfection of our perceptions and the fact that reality changes. You can develop specific language by the following:

- Distinguishing between individuals and the group (that is, avoid stereotyping). Person 1 is not Person 2 is not Person 3, etc., and none of them are all the People in the world.

- Specifying time and place of behavior instead of making broad statements. What was true of a person in 1999 is not necessarily true of the person now.

- Using names for jobs or roles (“accountants,” “administrative assistants,” “instructors”) instead of “people” or “workers.”

- Avoid “always/never” language. “Always” and “never” usually do not reflect reality and tend to make listeners defensive.

- Avoid confusing opinion for fact. If I say, “Frozen is a stupid movie,” I am stating an opinion in the language of fact. If you preface opinions with “I believe,” or “It is my opinion” you will be truthful and gain the appearance of being fair-minded and non-dogmatic. What should be said is “The first time I saw Frozen, I didn’t realize the underlying message of the film, but after I saw it a second time, I understood it better.” This sentence is much more specific and clarifying than “Frozen is a stupid movie.” Using this kind of language also helps make the speaker seem less dogmatic and closed-minded.

Third, personalize your language. In a speech, it’s fine to use personal pronouns as opposed to the third person. That means “I,” “me,” “we,” “us,” “you,” etc. are often helpful in a speech. It gives more immediacy to the speech. Be careful of using “you” for examples that might be embarrassing. “Let’s say you are arrested for possession of a concealed weapon,” sounds like the audience members are potential criminals.

Finally, develop your vocabulary, but not to show it off. One of the benefits of a college education is that your vocabulary will expand greatly, and it should. A larger vocabulary will give you access to more complicated reading material and allow you to understand the world better. But knowing the meaning of a more complicated word doesn’t mean you have to use it with every audience.

Conclusion

Although the placement of this chapter may seem to indicate that language choices, or what the ancient rhetoricians called “style,” are not as important as other parts of speaking, language choices are important from the very beginning of your speech preparation, even to your research and choice of search terms. Audience analysis will help you to develop language that is clear, vivid, appropriate, credible, and persuasive.

Reference

Anderson, Gregory, and David Harrison. (2007) “Global Language HotSpots.” Language Hotspots – Fast Facts, Living Tongues, https://langhotspots.swarthmore.edu/fastfacts.html.

## Language & Persuasion: Resonating with your audience
Resource ID: gbe88f053a0ae92337435e91b2552225f
Source: wiki_content/language-and-persuasion-resonating-with-your-audience.html
Module placement(s): Week 12

Throughout this course, we've focused on structuring arguments, recognizing logical reasoning, and supporting claims with credible evidence. While these elements are crucial, the language we use can significantly impact the persuasiveness of our speeches. Humans are emotional beings, and how we frame our language can resonate deeply with our audience.

Using these tools ethically is important. Think of it not as manipulating your audience, but rather aligning your language with their needs and values. Chances are, you already do some of these without realizing it!

Emotive Language

Language that expresses and arouses emotions: Words like "entrepreneur," (at least, if you're in major urban zone like the Bay Area) "income inequality," "family" and "comfort" evoke strong feelings and can make your message more impactful.

Connotations

Connotations are meanings attached to a word apart from its explicit definitions. This is a form of emotive language.

Consider the connotations of words like "educated," "home," "police brutality," "poor," "unsuccessful," and "woke." These words carry societal and personal weight that can influence how your audience perceives your message.

Euphemisms

Euphemisms are unpleasant or unmentionable things disguised by polite terms. Phrases like "hooking up," "in between jobs," and "discovering oneself" soften the impact of more direct language. We also use euphemistic terms to describe unfortunate events like the death of a person.

Slanting

Slanting involves interpreting or presenting information in line with a special interest, often suggesting a prejudice or judgment without all the facts. Logical fallacies, which we'll cover later in the term, often accompanies these types of statements. Fore example:

- "Incorporating gun control is an insult to all law-abiding supporters of the Second Amendment."

- "Anyone who values human communication should avoid being addicted to a smartphone."

- "Everyone knows the most successful graduates come from a UC, not a CSU."

Figurative Language

Figurative language uses words that produce images in the mind of the audience, heightening the impact of an argument. This involves using certain literary devices at times, such as simile, personification, or in the examples provided below, metaphor:

Examples using metaphor

- "Universal healthcare is a safety net for all citizens."

- "Free college tuition is a key that unlocks the door to opportunity."

- "Subsidized mental health care is a lifeline for struggling students."

An example using personification might be a statement in your intro or conclusion saying "the promise of free college tuition whispers hope. ." personifying a concept as human, while using a simile might show up as "Supporting universal healthcare is like building a bridge to a healther society".

Concrete Language

Words that point to real objects and experiences, often more descriptive. Examples include:

- "Universal healthcare provides regular check-ups, vaccinations, and emergency care for everyone."

- "Free college tuition covers tuition fees, reducing financial barriers for students."

- "Subsidized mental health care includes counseling sessions, support groups, and stress management workshops."

Abstract Words

Words that express qualities, characteristics, and values. Useful for making generalizations.. Terms like "Justice," "freedom," "equality," and "well-being" are abstract concepts that can be powerful in persuasive speeches. Like euphemisms, they can be used to soften language, but whereas a euphemism is meant to replace a specific word or term, abstract language is used more for intangible concepts.

By understanding and utilizing these types of language, you can craft speeches that not only present logical arguments (which we'll go into more in the coming weeks) but also connect emotionally with your audience. This combination of logic and emotion is key to effective persuasion in public speaking.

## Closing The Loop - Week 12
Resource ID: gb75b9888794a4aad356f7ffd20099153
Source: wiki_content/closing-the-loop-week-12.html
Module placement(s): Week 12

Congratulations! You've made it to the end of Week 12!

We've made the transition from discussing informative speaking to discussing persuasive speaking. While it might seem intimidating, the reality is that you've likely already engaged in some type of persuasive speaking. What I'm aiming to do now, as I have all quarter, is give you a framework for a set of skills that you're already incorporating, while hopefully refining them along the way.

Assignment Recap & Connection

This week's exercise and overall lessons connect back to the concept of pathos we discussed much earlier in the term, but holds more weight when it comes to trying to influence your audience to action. While facts and well supported evidence are important in your next presentations, we cannot ignore the emotional dimension of the audience.

Next Steps - Preparing for Week 13

Next week we'll be doing Speech #4. While I'm sure that for some of you, a presentation still conjures up feelings of anxiety, remember that this one is another low-stakes assignment that I'm grading on completion. As such, you might find it useful to utilize this upcoming assignment as a warm-up for speech #5.

Additional Resources and Practice

Using Language & Persuasion Without Being Pushy : This directs you to a 2023 post on LinkedIn which gives a succinct approach to incorporating rhetorical devices, alongside commentary from professionals.

- Using Language & Persuasion Without Being Pushy -> https://www.linkedin.com/advice/0/how-can-you-use-language-make-your-public-speaking-yr8jc

## Week 13 Overview
Resource ID: gb69a5d06712d973d5b7214bfea1e6d14

- Preparation

Objectives

Welcome to week 13! This week, we continue on the As we wind down towards the end of the quarter, this week is a bit of calm before the storm of finals. As such, our objectives are as follows:

- Identify strategies to evoke emotion within audience members

- Apply the Monroe's Motivated Sequence framework to a presentation delivered to peers.

- Identify the different layers of audience needs and values

Next week, we'll be touching on logical arguments and how they play a role in helping to strengthen your argument. But this week we're focusing more on the emotional side: For persuasion to truly be effective, one has to win the hearts of the audience, as well as their minds. To that end, take a look at this video: What emotions or values do you think it's trying to appeal to?

It's a succinct, but do you feel it communicated the message? Did it evoke any feelings in you?

Preparation

Most of your preparation should be focused on delivering the best presentation possible!

Key Deliverables

Our main deliverable this week centers around our presentation. With three speeches under your belt, this one should be a cinch! I'm grading primarily on completion, so as long as the necessary components are completed, then you'll do just fine!

Why It Matters

We've touched on the importance of audience-centered communication through the term and why it's crucial to do an audience analysis. This is the next step -- connecting the data from that analysis to real ways in which you can authentically tailor your message to the needs and values of your audience to maximize (without being manipulative) the emotional impact. Sometimes, audiences need a little help framing why they should care about something, or how it impacts their lives. When speakers take this for granted, they often lose their audience and their message misses the mark.

## Connecting to Audience Needs & Values
Resource ID: g923c32223fa587225a3978868e16ef04
Source: wiki_content/connecting-to-audience-needs-and-values.html
Module placement(s): Week 13

- Overview

- Audience Needs

- Audience Values

- Conclusion

Overview

When it comes to persuasion, it might be tempting to think that, as a speaker, if you just lay all of the facts out there and have a convincing argument, then audiences will naturally draw the rational conclusion. Fact alone aren't enough, and sometimes the audience doesn't see all of the same connections to a topic that you do: Particularly if they're far removed from it. Hence the importance of knowing how to align your topic with the needs and values of your audience. Sometimes, this is just a matter of clarifying something that's already there. Other times, it might not be readily evident since the person isn't directly affected by it: For instance, maternal health and mortality rates during pregnancy is something that impacts us all, but only a certain portion of the population is biologically capable of carrying a child to term.

In this section, we're going to review some common frameworks for understanding audience needs and values, as well as some strategies to connect with them. Let's begin with a deeper dive into pathos by Alex Lyon:

Lyon's point on language is something we'll cover later in the term: His point about storytelling is one avenue you can use to connect with audience needs and values. Let's take a look at audience needs first.

Audience Needs

When we discuss audience needs in this context, we're referring to specific information an audience requires to address concerns and gain a benefit from your presentation. One of the most well-known models describing needs is Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs. Your textbook mentions this hierarchy, but doesn't give a visual representation, so I'm providing one with the following:

Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons, CCBY 3.0 license

As we can see, the hierarchy is divided into five levels, with the idea being that an audience will not focus on needs at a higher level before satisfying needs at the lower level. From lowest to highest, these are:

- Physiological needs

- Safety needs

- Love and belonginess needs

- Esteem Needs

- Self Actualization

Using these as a general framework, and in conjunction with audience analysis, you might target your appeals to specific needs. For instance, you might offset the stigma someone perceives about attending community college by pointing to something the college is widely recognized for (thus highlighting esteem needs), or addressing the cost-benefit (safety/security needs). You could highlight the benefits of career paths that receive less prestige and recognition by highlighting that the salary will allow them to provide for their families (safety/security) or that the flexible nature of the work allows more time with friends and family (belonginess needs).

On the flipside of this, recognizing what needs are most important or pressing to your audience helps you avoid appeals that might fall short. I used to frequently have people from the campus's study abroad office come and encourage students to study in a variety of countries. At the end of each presentation, they'd send around a sign-up sheet for more information, but rarely got many signatures (usually two). It became a bit of a running joke until one year, someone changed the formula.

This time, the speaker led the presentation by acknowledging the elephant in the room: "Most of you are broke" she said, then proceeded to break down all the ways in which their costs could be covered, how much they saved in other areas, and ultimately that the cost was significantly lower than they were thinking. This speaker then proceeded with a similar presentation as speakers in the previous years.

When she passed around the interest form, I saw that more than half of my students had signed up. This speaker had a keen sense of the audience's needs.

Now, Maslow's hierarchy comes with a lot of caveats: First, it was developed in 1943, which was a very different time. Second, people are not video games with a linear progression. For instance, like me, if you've had family members who struggle with addiction, they will ignore a number of basic needs to secure substances. ..In a less dark example, consider investigative journalists or humanitarian aid workers who will eschew safety to go to war zones to help out the less fortunate. The following video lays out some of these nuances:

As you can see from the video, even Maslow himself acknowledged that context and culture impact human behavior: Meaning that even he knew these ideas weren't universal. So consider this hierarchy as a framework and to get you thinking about the general idea of tailoring your speech to audience needs, rather than gospel.

Next, we're going to focus on audience values.

Audience Values

In contrast to needs, audience values are the ethical and moral principles that guide someone's choices. In The Speaker's Handbook , the authors suggest that there are three types of audience values that speakers have

To further elaborate on these:

Peripheral Values

These loosely held values are the ones most easily changed. Think of it as a 'loose brand loyalty'. For instance, a person might have a strong desire to attend a CSU or UC, but they're agnostic about which UC or CSU it is, so long as it has the program they want or is located close to a city. In another, more personal example, I was a fan of New Balance running shoes for years after a friend's suggestion. I went into a store and told someone in no uncertain terms "I want a pair of new balances!" They didn't have them in the size I wanted, at which point the employee said "Hey, ever tried Asics?" Long story short, I came home with a different brand (if you're interested, I've cycled now between Hoka, Brooks, Asics, AND New Balances).

Authority Values

These are values that we hold due to our family, cultural group, close social groups, or even our professional workplaces. In the college example, someone might be fixated on a particular school (say, UCLA or UC Berkeley) because their family has put that pressure on them: either due to the prestige that comes with it OR because many families are alumni of the school. Our religion, views on politics, and even the types of people we associate may also be formed by these values.

Authority values are most open to change when people are away from the aforementioned groups for periods of time: This is often why college is such a transformative time for people, as it's the first time they're either away from their family (because they're living on campus or have moved away) or the close friend groups they spent time with in high school. Also consider when you've left a job you've been at; maybe the company culture you got so tied up in seems silly once you've left.

For me, as someone not from the Bay Area, I find that when I go back home to the east coast, I've snapped out of certain behaviors that I ended up adopting (mainly the hyperfocus on work).

Core Values

Core values are values that are so central to who a person is that to change them is to fundamentally change them as a person. Bluntly spoken, your chances of changing someone's core values in a presentation are pretty slim, unless that person has been in the process of questioning that said value for a while. These are often rooted in things like their views on family, their views on religion, money, health, or relationships. If you've ever known someone to change (or abandon their religion), they've likely seemed like an entirely different person.

Now, I don't bring this up to be a downer, but rather to advise you to find ways to align your presentation points with someone's core values, while also being aware when certain points might run up against those values and finding ways to temper your message.

Tab Three Content

Conclusion

By understanding audience needs and values, you'll be able to better tailor your message to the needs and values of your audience. If this is all feeling overwhelming, then remember these key takeaways:

- Connecting with the emotions of your audience (pathos) is one of the core elements of persuasion.

- Having a clear understanding of what your audience needs helps you better develop your main speech points to show how your information addresses said needs

- Audience values are more about aligning your message to those values than trying to change those values, which is tremendously difficult.

If you keep those in mind, then you'll be on the path to be a more persuasive, influential speaker.

## Week 14 Overview

Objectives

This week, we will be touching upon concepts of logic, reasoning, and fallacies. Bear in mind that our course only gives you an introduction to it: other courses in Communication Studies, like Argumentation & Debate, go into more depth. Our objectives for this week are as follows:

- Distinguish between inductive, deductive and causal arguments deductive arguments

- Identify and describe common fallacies in reasoning

A large focus of this week ties back to one of the first rhetorical appeals we discussed early in the term: Logos.

Now that you've spent two months in this course, how does your understanding of logos in week 1 compare to now? After this week's reading, are you seeing more of a connection?

Preparation

By this week you should be ironing out the final details of your presentation. The lessons this week will help in cementing some of the required elements of your outline as well.

Key Deliverables

We're winding down the requirements this week -- this time, there's only a discussion and quiz to take care of.

Why It Matters

As mentioned previously, you need to win over the hearts of the audience as well as the minds: This week we're focusing on the latter. While it is true that emotional appeals are extremely powerful (and, at times, can short circuit logical reasoning if used in a manipulative way), it helps to proceed from the assumption that there's someone in the audience who won't be moved by emotional appeals alone. In addition, recognizing all the ways in which logical reasoning can weaken an argument will not only help you create stronger presentations, but defend you against being misled by speaker who, whether by intention or not, employ these strategies in their fixation to win their audience over.

## Textbook Reading- Chapter 6 (Section 6.3)
Resource ID: g93d78c3815a9e8a0309ecca922def9f1
Source: wiki_content/textbook-reading-chapter-6-section-6-dot-3.html
Module placement(s): Week 14

Instructions: Read through the following lecture material, and consider taking notes. This section touches on induction and deduction. We'll cover this as well in a section of our instructional content this week.

6.3 Logic and the Role of Arguments

We use logic every day. Even if we have never formally studied logical reasoning and fallacies, we can often tell when a person’s statement doesn’t sound right. Think about the claims we see in many advertisements today – Buy product X, and you will be beautiful/thin/happy or have the carefree life depicted in the advertisement. With very little critical thought, we know intuitively that simply buying a product will not magically change our lives. Even if we can’t identify the specific fallacy at work in the argument (non causa in this case), we know there is some flaw in the argument.

By studying logic and fallacies we can learn to formulate stronger and more cohesive arguments, avoiding problems like that mentioned above. The study of logic has a long history. We can trace the roots of modern logical study back to Aristotle in ancient Greece. Aristotle’s simple definition of logic as the means by which we come to know anything still provides a concise understanding of logic (Aristotle, 1989). Of the classical pillars of a core liberal arts education of logic, grammar, and rhetoric, logic has developed as a fairly independent branch of philosophical studies. We use logic everyday when we construct statements, argue our point of view, and in myriad other ways. Understanding how logic is used will help us communicate more efficiently and effectively.

defining arguments

When we think and speak logically, we pull together statements that combine reasoning with evidence to support an assertion, arguments. A logical argument should not be confused with the type of argument you have with your sister or brother or any other person. When you argue with your sibling, you participate in a conflict in which you disagree about something. You may, however, use a logical argument in the midst of the argument with your sibling. Consider this example:

Brother and sister, Sydney and Harrison are arguing about whose turn it is to clean their bathroom. Harrison tells Sydney she should do it because she is a girl and girls are better at cleaning. Sydney responds that being a girl has nothing to do with whose turn it is. She reminds Harrison that according to their work chart, they are responsible for cleaning the bathroom on alternate weeks. She tells him she cleaned the bathroom last week; therefore, it is his turn this week. Harrison, still unconvinced, refuses to take responsibility for the chore. Sydney then points to the work chart and shows him where it specifically says it is his turn this week. Defeated, Harrison digs out the cleaning supplies.

Throughout their bathroom argument, both Harrison and Sydney use logical arguments to advance their point. You may ask why Sydney is successful and Harrison is not. This is a good question. Let’s critically think about each of their arguments to see why one fails and one succeeds.

Let’s start with Harrison’s argument. We can summarize it into three points:

- Girls are better at cleaning bathrooms than boys.

- Sydney is a girl.

- Therefore, Sydney should clean the bathroom.

Harrison’s argument here is a form of deductive reasoning, specifically a syllogism. We will consider syllogisms in a few minutes. For our purposes here, let’s just focus on why Harrison’s argument fails to persuade Sydney. Assuming for the moment that we agree with Harrison’s first two premises, then it would seem that his argument makes sense. We know that Sydney is a girl, so the second premise is true. This leaves the first premise that girls are better at cleaning bathrooms than boys. This is the exact point where Harrison’s argument goes astray. The only way his entire argument will work is if we agree with the assumption girls are better at cleaning bathrooms than boys.

Let’s now look at Sydney’s argument and why it works. Her argument can be summarized as follows:

- The bathroom responsibilities alternate weekly according to the work chart.

- Sydney cleaned the bathroom last week.

- The chart indicates it is Harrison’s turn to clean the bathroom this week.

- Therefore, Harrison should clean the bathroom.

Sydney’s argument here is a form of inductive reasoning. We will look at inductive reasoning in depth below. For now, let’s look at why Sydney’s argument succeeds where Harrison’s fails. Unlike Harrison’s argument, which rests on assumption for its truth claims, Sydney’s argument rests on evidence. We can define evidence as anything used to support the validity of an assertion. Evidence includes: testimony, scientific findings, statistics, physical objects, and many others. Sydney uses two primary pieces of evidence: the work chart and her statement that she cleaned the bathroom last week. Because Harrison has no contradictory evidence, he can’t logically refute Sydney’s assertion and is therefore stuck with scrubbing the toilet.

defining deduction

Deductive reasoning refers to an argument in which the truth of its premises guarantees the truth of its conclusions. Think back to Harrison’s argument for Sydney cleaning the bathroom. In order for his final claim to be valid, we must accept the truth of his claims that girls are better at cleaning bathrooms than boys. The key focus in deductive arguments is that it must be impossible for the premises to be true and the conclusion to be false. The classic example is:

All men are mortal.
Socrates is a man.
Therefore, Socrates is mortal.

We can look at each of these statements individually and see each is true in its own right. It is virtually impossible for the first two propositions to be true and the conclusion to be false. Any argument which fails to meet this standard commits a logical error or fallacy. Even if we might accept the arguments as good and the conclusion as possible, the argument fails as a form of deductive reasoning.

A few observations and much reasoning lead to error; many observations and a little reasoning to truth. ~ Alexis Carrel

Another way to think of deductive reasoning is to think of it as moving from a general premise to a specific premise. The basic line of reasoning looks like this:

This form of deductive reasoning is called a syllogism. A syllogism need not have only three components to its argument, but it must have at least three. We have Aristotle to thank for identifying the syllogism and making the study of logic much easier. The focus on syllogisms dominated the field of philosophy for thousands of years. In fact, it wasn't until the early nineteenth century that we began to see the discussion of other types of logic and other forms of logical reasoning. It is easy to fall prey to missteps in reasoning when we focus on syllogisms and deductive reasoning. Let's return to Harrison's argument and see what happens.

Logic: The art of thinking and reasoning in strict accordance with the limitations and incapacities of the human misunderstanding. ~ Ambrose Bierce

Considered in this manner, it should be clear how the strength of the conclusion depends upon us accepting as true the first two statements. This need for truth sets up deductive reasoning as a very rigid form of reasoning. If either one of the fast two premises isn't true, then the entire argument fails. Let's turn to recent world events for another example: In the debates over whether the United States should take military action in Iraq, this was the basic line of reasoning used to justify an invasion. This logic was sufficient for the United States to invade Iraq; however, as we have since learned, this line of reasoning also shows how quickly logic can go bad. We subsequently learned that the "experts" weren't quite so confident, and their "evidence" wasn't quite as concrete as originally represented.

defining induction

Inductive reasoning is often thought of as the opposite of deductive reasoning; however, this approach is not wholly accurate. Inductive reasoning does move from the specific to the general. However, this fact alone does not make it the opposite of deductive reasoning. An argument which fails in its deductive reasoning may still stand inductively. Unlike deductive reasoning, there is no standard format inductive arguments must take, making them more flexible. We can define an inductive argument as one in which the truth of its propositions lends support to the conclusion. The difference here in deduction is the truth of the propositions establishes with absolute certainty the truth of the conclusion. When we analyze an inductive argument, we do not focus on the truth of its premises. Instead we analyze inductive arguments for their strength or soundness.

Another significant difference between deduction and induction is inductive arguments do not have a standard format. Let’s return to Sydney’s argument to see how induction develops in action:

- Bathroom cleaning responsibilities alternate weekly according to the work chart.

- Sydney cleaned the bathroom last week.

- The chart indicates it is Harrison’s turn to clean the bathroom this week.

- Therefore, Harrison should clean the bathroom.

What Sydney does here is build to her conclusion that Harrison should clean the bathroom. She begins by stating the general house rule of alternate weeks for cleaning. She then adds in evidence before concluding her argument. While her argument is strong, we don’t know if it is true. There could be other factors Sydney has left out. Sydney may have agreed to take Harrison’s week of bathroom cleaning in exchange for him doing another one of her chores. Or there may be some extenuating circumstances preventing Harrison from bathroom cleaning this week.

You should carefully study the Art of Reasoning, as it is what most people are very deficient in, and I know few things more disagreeable than to argue, or even converse with a man who has no idea of inductive and deductive philosophy. ~ William John Wills

Let’s return to the world stage for another example. After the 9/11 attacks on the World Trade Center, we heard variations of the following arguments:

- The terrorists were Muslim (or Arab or Middle Eastern).

- The terrorists hated America.

- Therefore, all Muslims (or Arabs or Middle Easterners) hate America.

Clearly, we can see the problem in this line of reasoning. Beyond being a scary example of hyperbolic rhetoric, we can all probably think of at least one counter example to disprove the conclusion. However, individual passions and biases caused many otherwise rational people to say these things in the weeks following the attacks. This example also clearly illustrates how easy it is to get tripped up in your use of logic and the importance of practicing self-regulation.

## Textbook Reading- Chapter 6 (Section 6.5)
Resource ID: g4eb4b0791c599bd149493449b53afb03
Source: wiki_content/textbook-reading-chapter-6-section-6-dot-5.html
Module placement(s): Week 14

Instructions: Read through the following lecture material, and consider taking notes. This section covers informal fallacies. We'll also have another resource in Canvas that elaborates on this.

6.5: Formal Fallacies

A formal fallacy exists because of an error in the structure of the argument. In other words, the conclusion doesn’t follow from the premises. All formal fallacies are specific types of non sequiturs, or arguments in which the conclusions do not follow from the premises. Formal fallacies are identified by critically examining the structure of the argument exclusive of the individual statements. As you read through the following types of formal fallacies and examples, this definition will become more clear.

bad reasons fallacy (argumentum ad logicam)

In this fallacy, the conclusion is assumed to be bad because the arguments are bad. In practice, a premise of the argument is bad and therefore the conclusion is bad or invalid. This fallacy is seen often in debate or argumentation. We summarize the fallacy as: He gave bad reasons for his argument; therefore, his argument is bad. Consider the following claim:

The new employee is too quiet and has no sense of style. We should fire him.

The problem here should be obvious. To be a good employee does not require a certain look or the ability to put together interesting outfits. (Just look around your campus or workplace and you’ll probably see how true this is.) As such, the reasons for concluding the new faculty member should be fired are bad. We commit a fallacy if the conclusion to fire him is also bad or wrong. While the given reasons don’t necessarily support the conclusion, there may be others that do.

Bad reasoning as well as good reasoning is possible; and this fact is the foundation of the practical side of logic. ~ Charles Sanders Peirce

masked man fallacy (intensional fallacy)

The masked man fallacy involves a substitution of parties. If the two things we substitute are identical, then the argument is valid:

Rosamond Smith wrote the book .Nemesis

Rosamond Smith is an alias for Joyce Carol Oates.

Joyce Carol Oates wrote the book .Nemesis

This argument is valid because Rosamond Smith is in fact an alias for Joyce Carol Oates, so there is no flaw in the structure of the argument. Consider the following example:

Chris told police that a red- haired woman stole her car. Ginny is a red-haired woman. Therefore, Chris told police that Ginny stole her car.

The fallacy in this example occurs between the second premise and the conclusion. Looking at each premise individually, we can see that each is true. However, simply because each premise is true doesn’t mean the conclusion is necessarily true. Even if Ginny did steal Chris’s car, this fact doesn’t make the conclusion true. The existence of this fact cannot be presumed to change what Chris told the police.

fallacy of quantitative logic

Fallacies of quantitative logic revolve around the grammatical structure of the proposition. The focus is on the use of some sort of quantifying word such as “all” or “some.” Consider this example:

All philosophers are wise.

We can show the flaw in this statement by simply finding a counter- example. And since the fact of being wise is abstract, how do we truly know if one is wise or not? Consider how the statement changes with the use of a different quantifier:

Some philosophers are wise.

This statement is stronger because it allows for the possibility there are counter-examples. However, the error arises from the fact that it is not a known quantity. We must infer from the statement that some philosophers are not wise.

Let’s look at another example:

All conservatives are Republicans.
Therefore, all Republicans are conservatives.

Without thinking too hard you can probably think of one counter-example. Let’s try one more:

Some doctors are not MDs. Therefore, some MDs are not doctors.

While the first premise is true (there are other types of doctors), the second is clearly not true.

This page titled 6.5: Formal Fallacies is shared under a CC BY-NC-ND 3.0 license and was authored, remixed, and/or curated by Terri Russ@Saint Mary’s College (Public Speaking Project) via source content that was edited to the style and standards of the LibreTexts platform.

## Logical Reasoning
Resource ID: g8171a33c0fb2cbb33f3f927452979f71
Source: wiki_content/logical-reasoning.html
Module placement(s): Week 14

- Inductive Reasoning

- Deductive Reasoning

- Causal Reasoning

- Reasoning by Analogy

Inductive Reasoning

Example 1 – Universal Healthcare

- Specific Incidents :

- "In countries with universal healthcare, citizens report higher satisfaction with their healthcare services."

- "Studies show that universal healthcare systems reduce overall healthcare costs."

- "Countries with universal healthcare have better health outcomes, such as lower infant mortality rates."

Conclusion : Implementing universal healthcare in the United States would likely lead to higher satisfaction, lower costs, and better health outcomes.

Example 2 – Free College Tuition

- Specific Incidents :

- "In countries where college tuition is free, more students from low-income families attend college."

- "Free tuition programs in some states have led to higher college enrollment rates."

- "Graduates from countries with free tuition have lower student debt and more financial stability."

Conclusion : Making college tuition free at CSU and UC schools would increase access to higher education and reduce student debt.

Example 3 – Subsidized Mental Health Care

- Specific Incidents :

- "Colleges that subsidize mental health care see higher student retention rates."

- "Students who receive mental health support are more likely to succeed academically."

- "Subsidized mental health services reduce the stigma associated with seeking help."

Conclusion : "Subsidizing mental health care for college students would improve academic success and reduce stigma."

If you're comfortable with inductive reasoning, click on the next tab at the top of this page to move on to deductive reasoning.

Deductive Reasoning

In contrast to inductive reasoning, deductive reasoning moves from the general to the specific. The following terms are important in deductive reasoning:

- Major Premise: A generalization about a large group or class.

- Minor Premise: A specific statement about a specific member of the group or class

- Conclusion: The statement that links the major and minor premise (similar to a warrant)

When examining a deductive argument, it’s also important to make (and analyze) arguments that are both valid and sound. Easier said:

- Valid: Both premises are true (i.e. it follows the “formula” for a deductive argument)

- Sound: The premises and conclusions are closely related.

Keep in mind the following with deductive reasoning:

- If someone accepts both premises, then they have to accept the argument (or at least, look very silly backpedaling. .)

- Most “absolute” deductive arguments are trivial (“All men are mortal; Socrates is a man; Socrates is mortal)

- Many deductive arguments you encounter will not always have both premises clearly stated.

Next, let’s work with some examples:

Example 1 – Universal Healthcare

- Major Premise : "All countries with universal healthcare provide better health outcomes."

- Minor Premise : "The United States is considering implementing universal healthcare."

- Conclusion : "The United States will provide better health outcomes if it implements universal healthcare."

Example 2 – Free College Tuition

- Major Premise : "All states that offer free college tuition see increased enrollment rates."

- Minor Premise : "California is a state that could offer free college tuition at CSU and UC schools."

- Conclusion : "California will see increased enrollment rates if it offers free college tuition at CSU and UC schools."

Example 3 – Subsidized Mental Health Care

- Major Premise : "All colleges that subsidize mental health care improve student academic success."

- Minor Premise : "Our college is considering subsidizing mental health care."

- Conclusion : "Our college will improve student academic success if it subsidizes mental health care."

Hopefully these examples clarify deductive reasoning a little bit more. Next, we'll cover causal reasoning. Be sure to click on the tab at the top of the page. If you'd like a video explanation, check out the following video. In particular, pay attention to how the speaker describes "validity" and "soundness" in relation to the concept.

Causal Reasoning

Causal reasoning is a type of inductive reasoning where one event (a.k.a. antecedent ) contributes to or brings about the other event (a.k.a. the consequent ).

Examples

- Increased access to affordable healthcare ( antecedent) leads to better health outcomes for the citizens of a country ( consequent)

- Free tuition at 4-year institutions ( antecedent ) increases enrollment among lower socioeconomic and traditionally disadvantaged groups ( consequent)

- Free access to mental health care ( antecedent ) leads to fewer mental health emergencies among college students ( consequent ).

Now, the examples I've given here are simplified, but they should give you the general idea.

General Rules of Causal Reasoning

1: Causal Relationships are inferred, not directly observed

You can observe that a consequent follows an antecedent, but not that the consequent causes the event:

Example #1: You may witness a horrific car accident where a driver dies after a wild night of drinking, but a coroner would have to determine the exact cause of death.

Example #2: You may see your friend who attends every class achieve a higher grade than you, but you would need to examine the whole picture to ascertain what led to the good performance.

2: A Cause must precede its effect.

Is it the increased access to healthcare that leads to better health outcomes in a population? Or, in the process of implementing this system, did a society take other steps that lead to better health outcomes (making cities more walkable; stricter regulations around food; better education around health).

By clicking on the 4th tab of this page, we'll get to the last type of reasoning: Analogy

Reasoning by Analogy

When you reason by analogy, you are attempting to say that two separate cases are comparable in some relevant aspect. Analogies primarily serve to create a conceptual link between two things that are similar in some respect. In argumentation, analogies are employed as premises.

Typically, this will include three parts:

- The analogy, which usually contains two cases (hence "A & B contain a similar feature )

- Some type of statement that says from case A , some proposition ( P ) follows

- A conclusion, which says that since A and B are similar, if P follows from A, then it also follows from B.

Breaking it down a bit further, the formula looks like this:

- Case A is like case B

- Concerning A , P is true.

- Therefore, concerning B , P is also true.

If thinking in the abstract is making your head spin, let's look at some more specific examples

Example 1

- The skills that a teacher uses on a day to day basis (case A) mirror the skills a manager has to use daily (case B)

- As a teacher, you need to manage a range of varying personalities, regularly interface with stakeholders (parents), and design improvement plans for your students.. ( said skills represent "P" in this example)

- Therefore, if you you have those same skills you can be an effective manager.

Example 2

- Practicing a martial art (case A) is similar to practicing dance ( case B)

- In martial arts, you need to pay careful attention to your breathing so that you can maximize your movements effectively.

- Therefore, you probably need to pay careful attention to your breathing while practicing dance.

Determining an Analogical Argument's Strength

Consider these factors when determining the strength of an argument by analogy

Degree of similarity: How similar are the two cases being presented? In the examples above, being an MMO guild leader and a manager at a company are further apart than the second example: Dance and martial arts are both physical activities.

Relevance: How relevant are the similarities presented in the conclusion? In our above examples, how crucial is breathing in martial arts? In the first example, how relevant are the skills presented?

Hopefully that gives you some additional insight. Now that you've had an overview of four types of reasoning, the next page in the module will link out to some videos that expand upon things even further.

Please review all tabs before clicking "Next"

- Deductive Reasoning -> #tab-2
- Causal Reasoning -> #tab-3
- Reasoning by Analogy -> #tab-4

## Logical Fallacies
Resource ID: gd5d972427c44be299bf3e7f0c44f525e
Source: wiki_content/logical-fallacies.html
Module placement(s): Week 14

Avoiding Logical Fallacies

Broadly speaking, logical fallacies are errors in reasoning that weaken an argument. By recognizing and avoiding these fallacies as a speaker, you can build more credible arguments and avoid misleading your arguments. As a listener, you can better evaluate the viewpoints of your speaker.

What follows is a list of ten of the more common logical fallacies you may encounter as a speaker as they apply to a range of topics. By no means is this an exhaustive list. If you're interested in learning more about logical fallacies, then I highly suggest you enroll in a COMM 8 or COMM 9 here at De Anza.

What follows will be a brief description of each logical fallacy. For the purposes of this lesson, we're going to be working with three propositions of policy:

- Example #1 : Why the United States should move towards a universal healthcare system.

- Example #2 : Why college tuition at CSU and UC schools should be free for California residents.

- Example #3 : Why schools should subsidize mental health care for college students.

Ad Hominem (click to reveal)

This is a fallacy where the speaker attacks the person making the argument, rather than addressing the weaknesses in the argument itself.

- Universal Healthcare : "We shouldn't listen to his argument for universal healthcare; he's not even a doctor."

- Free College Tuition : "She argues for free tuition at CSU and UC schools, but she didn't even go to college herself."

- Subsidized Mental Health Care : "Why should we trust his opinion on subsidizing mental health care? He has no experience in the field."

Straw Man Fallacy (click to reveal)

In this fallacy, one side (often purposefully) builds up a weak argument and attributes it to their opposing side, making it easier to attack and dismantle.

- Universal Healthcare : "Supporting universal healthcare means you want the government to control every aspect of our lives."

- Free College Tuition : "If we make college tuition free, you're saying education should be completely free for everyone, including private schools."

- Subsidized Mental Health Care : "If we subsidize mental health care, you're suggesting we should pay for everyone's personal problems."

Appeal to Authority (click to reveal)

Claiming something is true simply because an authority figure endorses it, without evidence to support the claim. Often, this authority has little or no experience in the area of the endorsement.

Examples :

- Universal Healthcare : "Universal healthcare must be the best option because a supports it."

- Free College Tuition : "Free tuition at CSU and UC schools is a great idea because a well-known politician endorses it."

- Subsidized Mental Health Care : "We should subsidize mental health care because a says it's necessary."

False Dichotomy (click to reveal)

A false dichotomy fallacy, sometimes termed a "false dilemma" fallacy, presenting two options as the only possible choices when others exist. Often, these choices are presented as extremes on either end of the spectrum.

Examples :

- Universal Healthcare : "We either implement universal healthcare, or we let people die without medical care."

- Free College Tuition : "Either we make college tuition free, or only the wealthy will be able to afford higher education."

- Subsidized Mental Health Care : "We either subsidize mental health care for students, or we ignore their mental health needs entirely."

Slippery Slope (click to reveal)

This fallacy argues that one small action will lead to a chain of negative events culminating in the worst possible outcome without evidence to back it up. Likely, you've heard a well-meaning, concerned parent or other authority figure use this on you.

Examples :

- Universal Healthcare : "If we adopt universal healthcare, it will lead to socialism and eventually the collapse of our economy."

- Free College Tuition : "If we make college tuition free, soon all education will be free, and the quality of education will decline."

- Subsidized Mental Health Care : "If we subsidize mental health care for students, next we'll be paying for all their personal expenses."

Hasty Generalization (click to reveal)

A hasty generalization draws a conclusion based on insufficient evidence: Essentially flawed inductive reasoning.

Examples :

- Universal Healthcare : "I know someone who had a bad experience with universal healthcare, so it must be a terrible system."

- Free College Tuition : "A few students I know don't take their education seriously, so making tuition free will just lead to more slackers."

- Subsidized Mental Health Care : "One student abused the mental health services, so subsidizing it for all students is a bad idea."

Circular Reasoning (click to reveal)

With circular reasoning, the speaker uses the conclusion as evidence to support the argument.

Examples :

- Universal Healthcare : "Universal healthcare is necessary because everyone should have access to healthcare."

- Free College Tuition : "College tuition should be free because education shouldn't cost anything."

- Subsidized Mental Health Care : "We should subsidize mental health care because students need affordable mental health care."

Post Hoc Fallacy (click to reveal)

The post-hoc (a.k.a. false cause) fallacy assumes that because one event followed another, the first caused the second; a flaw in causal reasoning.

Examples :

- Universal Healthcare : "After implementing universal healthcare, the country's economy improved. Therefore, universal healthcare caused the economic improvement."

- Free College Tuition : "After a state made college tuition free, graduation rates increased. Therefore, free tuition caused the increase in graduation rates."

- Subsidized Mental Health Care : "After subsidizing mental health care, student grades improved. Therefore, the subsidies caused the improvement in grades."

Red Herring (click to reveal)

Introducing irrelevant information to distract from the main argument. It gets its name from a (supposed) practice of using the strong scent of a red herring to distract a hunting dog from its prey (though even that origin story is suspect).

Examples :

- Universal Healthcare : "Why worry about universal healthcare when we have so many other issues like unemployment?"

- Free College Tuition : "We shouldn't focus on free college tuition when the real problem is the high cost of living."

- Subsidized Mental Health Care : "Why discuss subsidizing mental health care when there are still issues with campus safety?"

Bandwagon Appeal (click to reveal)

Arguing that something is true or correct because it’s popular or widely accepted. The ever popular "everyone's doing it!" rationale.

Examples :

- Universal Healthcare : "We should support universal healthcare because most developed countries have it."

- Free College Tuition : "Free college tuition is a good idea because many students support it."

- Subsidized Mental Health Care : "We should subsidize mental health care because other universities are doing it."

Hopefully these examples give you a better idea of how these logical fallacies might play out. I want to end by stressing that committing a fallacy does not automatically invalidate your argument, but it can weaken it. In addition, as you begin to make longer, more sophisticated arguments, avoiding ALL fallacies (check out our master list in this week's resources) can be extremely challenging.

Still, you should strive to avoid them to the best of your ability and ensure that your argument is based on sound, rather than rushed or flawed logic.

## Week 15 Overview
Resource ID: g9e59bf33b83a7e21ec90763375d30de9
Source: wiki_content/week-15-overview.html
Module placement(s): Week 15

- Objectives

- Preparation

- Key Deliverables

- Why It Matters

Objectives

The end (of the term) is finally in sight! This week we'll be focusing on your advocacy speech. Our objectives include:

- Deliver an extemporaneous persuasive presentation to a group of your peers.

- Apply principles of logic, sound reasoning, audience engagement and motivational appeals.

- Evaluate the strength of persuasive arguments given during peer presentations.

- Compare and contrast communication apprehension levels between the start of the quarter and the current period

There's not much for me to add here: If you've made it to this point in the quarter, then I have every bit of confidence that you can succeed.

Preparation

Since you've made it this far, my expectations for your preparation should come as no surprise: It's standard for our major speech week.

Logistics

Confirm your presentation time. You know the drill by now. As always, you're welcome to attend sessions outside of your own as an audience member.

While you're not required to use presentation aids, if you are going with one of the cloud-based services, download a copy to your device just in case something's finicky.

During the Presentation Day

Not much has changed here, either, but a few reminders:

Presenters

As always, I'll have the feature that allows you to join before the host (me) enabled. If you're able, hop on beforehand to make sure your audio and video are working. I must be able to see and hear you in order to grade this presentations.

Audience Members

Be a good audience member! You'll need to have your camera on, but during the speech I'll have you muted. Try to minimize distractions from your environment as you listen to the presentations. As we draw closer to finals week, I know the pressure is on, but make every effort to show up on time for these presentations. We have presentations scheduled this week in order to avoid some of the schedule conflicts and mishaps that occur with finals week.

Key Deliverables

Three key deliverables with this week:

- Speech #5 outline: Review the guidelines for citing APA. As with the previous outline, I expect both in-text citations and a full reference page for your outline.

- Speech #5: The biggest one this week. While it's the speech that's weighted the highest, you've also been doing presentations all term by this point, so I'm confident in your ability to succeed.

- Peer Responses: This will follow a similar format compared to last presentation. Please note if you have to leave before your peer presents, I won't be able to schedule a make up.

No discussion or quiz this week: There's enough on your plate as it is, so I want your efforts focused on your speech.

Why It Matters

I would hope that it's evident by now, but I don't want to take chances. While it might not always be in the form of a formal, six minute presentation, you're going to be doing a great deal of persuasive speaking in your life. Even if it's not a formal speech, consider the contexts in which you'll need to persuade a group of people: Leaders in your place of work to support your team, provide resources, or take a particular course of action. Among your colleagues to adopt a new approach or strategy. It could even be among a friend group when it involves where to go for a vacation (or heck, even just lunch), or how to go about planning a special occasion.

The concepts here don't just apply to public speaking, but in every day communication. Still, in the event you do have to give an actual presentation, I want you to have some practice in it, even if it's long after you've left this class.

Objectives

We've made it to finals week! Congratulations! The objective this week is straightforward:

- Compare and contrast one's performance as a speaker from the start of the term to the end of the term.

One last video that's fitting for the end of our course is an old commencement presentation by the late Steve Jobs at Stanford University. It's fairly lengthy, so don't feel like you need to watch the whole thing immediately.

This presentation fits the "Special Occasions Speech" that we see in our textbook. The core of his message feels fitting for the end of a course like this. However, after going through this term, how would you rate his presentation? Do you think you might have evaluated this differently before you learned what you did over the course of the past few months?

Preparation

For this week, be sure to go back and review all of your assignments. I'm generally good about making sure scores are accurate and up to date, but if you see something that's ungraded (and was submitted on time) or has a mathematical error, then let me know.

Be sure not to wait until the last minute to gather your group of people to present to for your Tribute speech. It may be worthwhile for you to reach out to your peers and see if any of them are on campus and willing to volunteer.

Why It Matters

Why do you think this course matters?

## Closing The Loop - Public Speaking
Resource ID: g7ac136f3ae2ef7680c00a2c9df05967e
Source: wiki_content/closing-the-loop-public-speaking.html
Module placement(s): Finals Week

Congratulations! You've made it to the end of the course!

And now you're done giving speeches! Well. . . not quite: You'll be delivering presentations in some shape or form in your other classes, at your jobs, and perhaps even within your community. While they may not take the exact shape or form that they did in this class, the skills that you've developed here will undoubtedly serve you.

Next Steps - Where to go from here?

If you're interested in further refining your speaking skills, take a look at some of the other offerings in the Communication Studies department. Classes around Oral Interpretation, as well as debate will give you more first hand speaking experience. If you're looking for things outside of the traditional course, venues like Toastmasters or an improv class could help refine your skillset.

If you're not ready to commit to something like that, then at a minimum, feel more empowered to speak up in your courses, or be the person who takes a lead in the group project. Those environments tend to be low stakes than a larger presentation, and it's still giving you a chance to speak in front of a group.

Additional Resources and Practice

Have a wonderful break, and the best of luck to you in future speaking endeavors!

## Meeting RSI Regulations in your Plug-and-Play Course
Resource ID: g1deb4a8ad5c1b36a3ea69c9966e2179c
Source: wiki_content/meeting-rsi-regulations-in-your-plug-and-play-course.html
Module placement(s): [not placed in a module]

What is RSI?

Regular and Substantive Interaction, or RSI, refers to the requirement that instructors must regularly initiate and engage in academically meaningful interaction with students in online or distance-education courses. This language derives from the Education Department and is part of the regulations assessed by the Accrediting Commission for Community and Junior Colleges (ACCJC), the accrediting body for the California Community Colleges.

34 C.F.R. § 600.2

Distance education: Education that uses one or more of the technologies listed in paragraphs (2)(i) through (iv) of this definition to deliver instruction to students who are separated from the instructor or instructors and to support regular and substantive interaction between the students and the instructor or instructors, either synchronously or asynchronously.

( i ) The internet;

( ii ) One-way and two-way transmissions through open broadcast, closed circuit, cable, microwave, broadband lines, fiber optics, satellite, or wireless communications devices;

( iii ) Audio conference; or

( iv ) Other media used in a course in conjunction with any of the technologies listed in paragraphs (2)(i) through (iii) of this definition.

RSI is also an important part of quality control for online courses and is outlined in Section 3: Regular and Substantive Interaction (formerly Section B) in the most recent version of the CVC-@ONE course design rubric.

Section 3 Rubric Elements

- 3.1 Initial Course Contact

- 3.2 Instructor-Initiated Interaction

- 3.3 Student-Initiated Interaction

- 3.4 Facilitation of Student Interaction

Why is RSI important in an online course?

- Humanization: RSI humanizes online learning by ensuring students experience genuine instructor presence, empathy, and connection. As the subject-matter expert, it is your responsibility to provide students with your insights and feedback as students work through your course.

- Equity: RSI ensures these students receive equitable levels of instructor attention and academic support, regardless of whether they attend online or in person. Receiving consistent access to instructor support and engagement is a cornerstone of student success.

- Community: RSI fosters community by creating structured opportunities for dialogue, collaboration, and shared learning among students and instructors.

- Quality & Accountability: RSI upholds quality and accountability by aligning online teaching with federal and institutional standards that ensure meaningful, measurable educational interaction.

How do Plug-and-Play courses help you meet RSI?

RSI is ultimately a faculty-driven responsibility; being present, responsive, and providing meaningful feedback on assignments, quizzes, and discussions creates the most impactful learning experience for students. The content and activities in this course have been intentionally designed for instructors to have meaningful and regular interaction with their students , and for students to have meaningful interaction opportunities with their peers.

What has been provided to you to help facilitate RSI in this couse:

- Content and a Course Structure that meets CVC-@ONE course design standards (POCR)

- Course Syllabus

- Orientation Module

- Assignments

- Discussions

- Quizzes

You will still need to provide to meet RSI standards:

- Welcome Letter and/or Announcement

- Customized Orientation Module

- Weekly Announcements

- Consistent instructor presence (via weekly overview videos, short content-based lessons or demos in your preferred medium, etc.)

- Assignment/Submission Feedback (non-automated)

- Presence in Discussions as indicated in your "Participate in Discussions" page

- Timely Canvas Inbox Responses (or other methods of communication)

Links:
- Accrediting Commission for Community and Junior Colleges (ACCJC), -> https://accjc.org/

## The Virtual Equivalent: Communication
Resource ID: g5d5579d281ff254b1ace03625d9787a2
Source: wiki_content/the-virtual-equivalent-communication.html
Module placement(s): [not placed in a module]

In a traditional on-ground course, regular and substantive interaction (RSI) naturally occurs through in-person interactions, such as during class discussions, office hours, and spontaneous conversations. An online course requires intentional planning and consistent use of digital communication tools to ensure ongoing engagement, such as through discussion forums, announcements, messages, and timely feedback.

Setting the Scene

The California Code of Regulations stipulates that students are entitled to "regular and substantive interaction", but what exactly does this entail? Think of it this way: On a normal teaching day, you wouldn't go into your classroom, write all the notes for the day on the board, turn on a video, set out a test, and leave before your students even arrived, would you?

While there are some students who do prefer to learn independently, many of us are social beings. We need regular interaction with others to provide the optimal learning environment. And, it turns out, instructors are key factors in student success . In an online class, how do we replace face-to-face contact? The answer is actually pretty simple, we build it into the design of the course.

Instructor Communication and Engagement is So Important, It's the Law

Ed Code / Title V (click to view)

Distance Education Title 5 Regulations Revision (11/2022)

§ 55204. Instructor Contact

In addition to the requirements of section 55002 and any locally established requirements applicable to all courses, district governing boards shall ensure that:

-

- Any portion of a course conducted through distance education includes regular and substantive interaction between the instructor(s) and students, (and among students, if described in the course outline of record or distance education addendum), either synchronously or asynchronously, through group or individual meetings, orientation and review sessions, supplemental seminar or study sessions, field trips, library workshops, telephone contact, voice mail, e-mail, or other activities.

- “Substantive interaction” means engaging students in teaching, learning, and assessment, consistent with the content under discussion, and also includes at least two of the following:

- Providing direct instruction;

- Assessing or providing feedback on a student's coursework;

- Providing information or responding to questions about the content of a course or competency;

- Facilitating a group discussion regarding the content of a course or competency; or

- Other instructional activities approved by the institution's or program's accrediting agency.

- Regular interaction between a student and instructor(s) is ensured by, prior to the student's completion of a course or competency:

- Providing the opportunity for substantive interactions with the student on a predictable and scheduled basis commensurate with the length of time and the amount of content in the course or competency; and

- Monitoring the student's academic engagement and success and ensuring that an instructor is responsible for promptly and proactively engaging in substantive interaction with the student when needed on the basis of such monitoring, or upon request by the student.

- Regular and substantive interaction is an academic and professional matter pursuant to sections 53200 et seq.

- For purposes of calculating instructional time in the context of asynchronous distance education, a week of instructional time is any week in which:

- The institution makes available the instructional materials, other resources, and instructor support necessary for academic engagement and completion of course objectives; and

- The institution expects enrolled students to perform educational activities demonstrating academic engagement during the week.

ACCJC / Accrediting Body (click to view)

ACCJC / Accreditation Standards

The ACCJC builds on the language from the Ed Code (above) and uses a rubric to assess RSI in online courses during accreditation. Their rubric criteria for "highly developed" RSI, the highest rating, is as follows. You can also view the full rubric here: ACCJC Quality Continuum Rubric

Substantive Interaction is engaging students in teaching, learning, and assessment, consistent with the content under discussion, and also includes at least two of the following:

- Consistently provides prompt, personalized, and detailed feedback on student coursework and assignments, such as written comments, detailed rubrics, audio or video notes, and examples for improvement.

- Frequently provides substantive information, and announcements beyond reminders that discuss previous topics, trends in assignments, or that highlights key concepts. Frequently encourages participation and questions and responds in a timely manner with detailed information and ideas.

- Actively participates in discussions to pose questions, propose alternative viewpoints, connect ideas, and encourage struggling students.

Regular Interaction between a student and an instructor(s) is demonstrated by the two following criteria:

- Instructor engagement with students yields equitable student outcomes. Interactions are predictable and occur in accordance with the length and course content.

- Instructor frequently engages with students throughout the term to provide formative feedback and information based on monitoring student engagement and success. Instructor promptly and proactively responds to students in response to observed concerns or at the request of students, to provide necessary support.

Course Outline of Record (click to view)

Course Outline of Record

On your Course Outline of Record, which is your official curriculum guide for your course, there must be a Distance Ed (DE) addendum for the course to be offered in an online or hybrid format. Part of the DE addendum specifies how instructors will initiate and maintain communication with students. Here are just a few of those ways:

- Orientation at start of course - Orientation to occur during the first week of instruction and includes information on how to use the school’s learning management system, how to access course content, and submit assignments.

- Announcements/Inbox/Pronto - Once a week or more, as needed by the instructor, to communicate information on assignments, due dates, or other information that pertains to the course.

- Chat feature - Enabled as deemed necessary by the instructor to facilitate communication with students.

- Discussion Boards - Discussion boards used during the course to facilitate communication between students about course content.

- Telephone conversations - Telephone conversations with individual students as necessary to assist with coursework and/or provide additional information.

- Scheduled on-campus meetings - HYBRID ONLY: The class will meet on campus as specified by the schedule.

- Virtual Office Hours - Instructor will hold virtual office hours as allowed and as necessary to provide student access. HYBRID ONLY: some office hours should be scheduled on campus.

Roles and Goals for Online Communication

In the most basic sense, our goal is to comply with the law and guidelines set by Title V. Ideally, though, our goal is to provide a quality learning experience for our online students, and this includes a lot of instructor communication. That's where you, the instructor, come in. How do you plan to implement these guidelines for regular, substantive interaction? You'll definitely need a plan and clearly communicate that plan to your students. Remember the bare minimum.

The goal:

Communicating regularly and substantively AND "engaging students in teaching, learning, and assessment"

Your role:

- communicate with students regularly and substantively

- provide instructional materials, other resources, and instructor support necessary for students to complete course objectives and remain engaged

- create enough activities for students to demonstrate engagement each week

- monitor student success and engagement

- proactively communicate with students who seem disengaged or needing additional support

- interact with students on a predictable and regular basis

- Do at least two of these things regularly:

- Provide direct instruction;

- Assess or provide feedback on a student's coursework;

- Provide information or respond to questions about the content of a course or competency;

- Facilitate a group discussion regarding the content of a course or competency;

Examples and Ideas - Regular, Substantive Interaction in Practice

Before the course

- Emailing students a welcome letter

- Posting an announcement on the first day

- Plan to facilitate introductions through individual messages, a discussion board, or getting to know you survey

- Prerecording and including your own lectures to mimic direct instruction

- Reaching out to students who haven't logged in or participated on the first day

Throughout the Course

- Providing regular, individualized feedback on assignments.

- Hosting weekly virtual office hours or live sessions.

- Posting weekly announcements to guide students through course content.

- Sharing weekly update videos with FAQs you received about assignments that week

- Participating in and facilitating discussion forums.

- Sending personalized reminders or follow-ups to students falling behind.

- Including surveys for students to share feedback

Key Takeaway

The goal of Regular Substantive Interaction (RSI) is to ensure that students engage with a real, responsive instructor rather than feeling like they are receiving automated or impersonal instruction. Things like automated grading, feedback, and AI may make our jobs as instructors easier, but they are not a substitute for teaching, connecting, and communicating with students.

Additional Resources

For more information and ideas about Regular, Substantive Interaction in online courses, explore the links below

- "The Art of Connection: Building Effective Regular, Substantive Interaction" video playlist from @ONE; videos in this playlist are one hour each.

- Quick Reference Guide for RSI pdf from Oregon State University eCampus

- RSI Examples Google doc from Blue Mountain Community College

Click Next to move to the next page.

Instructions:
Self-Reflection: Speech #3 – Social Issues Presentation

Now that you’ve completed several presentations, I hope you’re starting to feel more confident in your public speaking skills. For this assignment, you’ll complete another self-reflection—this time focusing on your Social Issues Speech .

Use this opportunity to reflect not just on this most recent presentation, but also on how you've grown since your earlier speeches. You may draw comparisons to your previous performances as you respond to the following content areas.

Reflection Prompts

In your reflection, address the following areas with thought and detail:

-

Strengths: What do you feel you did especially well in this presentation? Was this an area you struggled with in earlier speeches?

-

Weaknesses: What aspects of this speech didn’t go as well? Are these recurring challenges, or new ones that came up?

-

Process: Did anything change in how you prepared for this presentation? What worked—or didn’t?

-

Speech Anxiety: How did you feel leading up to and during the speech? How did you manage any nervousness or communication apprehension?

-

Comparison: Compared to your previous presentations, what parts of this one felt easier—or more difficult?

-

Changes: Looking ahead to your final presentation, what would you like to do differently (if anything)?

Submission Requirements

Your reflection should:

-

Be 500–750 words

-

Be formatted as a coherent essay , not a list or short-answer response

-

Follow college-level writing expectations:

-

Proper grammar, punctuation, and paragraph structure

-

Double-spaced, 12-point font

-

See the Formatting Guidelines in the Course Orientation module for more details

You may organize your reflection in any order that makes sense to you. Using headings is fine, and a conversational tone is welcome—just be sure your content is clear, thoughtful, and well-structured .

Grading Criteria:

Your reflection will be assessed using the rubric below . Be sure to review it so you understand how your work will be evaluated.

Final Note:

It's natural to focus on areas you want to improve—but remember to also acknowledge your progress. If you showed up, prepared, and gave it your best effort, that’s already a win. I’m proud of the work you’re doing, and I look forward to hearing your thoughts.

Feel free to reach out if you need help getting started or have any questions!
