Instructions:
Speech #2 Self-Reflection: Core Values Presentation

Although we learn a great deal by simply doing, it’s just as important to reflect on our performance after a presentation. This assignment gives you the opportunity to critically evaluate your strengths and areas for improvement following your Core Values Speech .

If you recorded your speech, reviewing it before writing your reflection will make this process much more insightful.

Reflection Prompts

Your self-reflection should address both your performance and your process . Use the guiding questions below to help you develop a thoughtful and detailed response.

Performance & Improvement

-

What do you feel were your specific strengths during the presentation? Why?

-

What specific areas of weakness may have affected the effectiveness of your speech? Why?

-

What concrete steps can you take to improve your next presentation?

Process

-

Preparation: How did you prepare for this speech? Do you feel your preparation was effective?

-

Content: You know your material well, but your audience only saw a portion of it. Did you need to cut any parts that may have impacted your message or clarity?

-

Limitations: What challenges did you face (e.g., time, nerves, environment) that may have limited your ability to present the way you intended?

Submission Requirements

Your reflection should:

-

Be 500–750 words

-

Be formatted as a coherent essay (not a list or short-answer response)

-

Follow college-level writing standards:

-

Clearly indented paragraphs

-

Proper grammar, punctuation, and spelling

-

Double-spaced, 12 pt. font (see formatting guidelines in the Course Orientation module)

You may respond in any order, use headings if you like, or take a more conversational tone—as long as your reflection is clear, organized, and well developed.

Grading Criteria

This assignment will be graded using the rubric below . Please review it carefully to understand how your reflection will be evaluated.

💡 Final tip: While it's important to think critically about areas for growth, don’t forget to recognize your accomplishments and progress as well.

If you have any questions, feel free to reach out!

- assignment/hide_in_gradebook: false
- assignment/intra_group_peer_reviews: false
- assignment/only_visible_to_overrides: false
- assignment/post_to_sis: false
- assignment/moderated_grading: false
- assignment/grader_count: 0
- assignment/grader_comments_visible_to_graders: true
- assignment/anonymous_grading: false
- assignment/graders_anonymous_to_graders: false
- assignment/grader_names_visible_to_final_grader: true
- assignment/anonymous_instructor_annotations: false
- assignment/post_policy/post_manually: false

Instructions:
This week's reading illustrates the importance of knowing your audience and tailoring a speech's message, as well as structure to them. the text also reviewed the more formal ways in which you can do a demographic and psychological analysis needed to gauge audience attitudes around an issue. For the purposes of our presentation, your target audience consists with your peers.

Our goal for this exercise is to due a preliminary analysis of your peers and construct a survey to conduct your audience analysis. Done well, this tool can also be modified to be used in subsequent presentations. By conducting a self analysis and analyzing your peers, you'll gain better insights into how to tailor your message effectively. Let's take a look at the instructions.

Part 1: Demographic and Psychological Self Analysis

First, identify, to the extent that you're comfortable, your relevant demographic characteristics (a list will be included at the end of this page). Next, identify elements from the psychological analysis list. Finally, reflect on how these characteristics might influence your perspectives and communication style.

Part 2: Conduct a Class Analysis

For part 2, you'll be conducting a class analysis of your peers based on direct observation. By this point in the quarter, you've had the opportunity to see your classmates in a recorded presentation, during a live presentation, as well as their discussion posed. Based on these observations, draw some conclusions on their demographic and psychological factors.

Part 3: Designing a Survey Instrument

For the last part, you'll be designing a survey. I recommend using either Google Surveys or SurveyMonkey to create one. The criteria for the survey as follows

- Include at least 5 questions that gather information about your classmates' demographic characteristics

- Include at least three questions that address the psychological factors. These should be close-ended questions that use either yes/no answers, or a likert-type response.

- At least 2 questions should be open-ended questions based on psychological factors which invite longer responses.

You'll be deploying this survey next week, so you only need to share it with me for now so that you can get feedback.

Final Submission

Your submission for this assignment should include the following:

- The answer to your demographic and psychological factors, with a brief summary to the reflection questions (one paragraph maximum is fine)

- An assessment of your classmates' demographic and psychological factors (once again, a single paragraph is fine)

- A link to your survey

Demographic Characteristics

- Age (can indicate a precise number or range)

- Gender (male, female, nonbinary)

- Nationality

- Ethnicity

- Education

- Occupation

- Marital Status

- Income Level

- Household Size

- Parental Status

- Political Affiliation

- Religious Affiliation

Psychological Characteristics

- Attitudes (towards a particularly topic; e.g. 'how would you rate your attitudes towards. . )

- Values (personal or social; e.g. "what values do you hold most important?")

- Interests (hobbies; career goals; topics of curiosity)

- Motivations (long and short term goals; what motivates them -- finances, family, etc.)

- Openness to feedback

- General levels of stress and anxiety

Speech Make-Up Instructions

If you missed a scheduled live presentation and have been approved to complete a make-up, please follow the instructions below:

What You Need to Do:

-

Record your speech using Zoom, Canvas Studio, or another video recording tool.

-

Submit your recorded presentation and speech outline via this assignment page.

-

Your speech should follow the same guidelines and time requirements as the original assignment. Be sure to review the appropriate speech guidelines to ensure you are meeting expectations.

Before Submitting:

-

Make sure your video and audio are clear.

-

Ensure your outline is complete and follows the format required for the original assignment.

-

Double-check that your speech addresses the correct topic and follows all instructions.

Grading:

Your make-up speech will be graded using the same rubric as the original assignment. Scroll down to view the rubric for details.

Due Date:

Your make-up speech must be submitted no later than the deadline communicated to you via email or Canvas message.

If you have any questions or need support with your submission, feel free to reach out.

Instructions:
👥 Peer Evaluation: Advocacy Speech Presentation

Use the form below to complete your peer evaluation for two classmates during the Advocacy Speech presentations.

📌 Instructions:

-

Complete one evaluation form per classmate (2 total).

-

Submit your completed evaluations here in Canvas.

-

Send a copy of each evaluation to your classmate via Canvas Messages or email.

✅ Grading Note:
This assignment is graded based on completion . You will receive full credit for each evaluation if:

-

You answer all questions in both Part 1 and Part 2

-

You send a copy of the completed evaluation to your classmate

📊 Part 1: Likert-Style Evaluation (Questions 1–10)

Respond to each statement using the following scale:

-

Strongly Agree

-

Agree

-

Uncertain

-

Disagree

-

Strongly Disagree

-

The speech clearly introduced the social issue, cause, or organization.

-

The speaker connected the issue, cause, or organization with the audience.

-

Relevant information was quoted or paraphrased from a source with appropriate oral citations.

-

The speaker clearly described their own personal connection to the topic.

-

The presentation was organized and the information was easy to understand.

-

The speech had a logical flow with a strong introduction and effective conclusion.

-

The speaker’s oral delivery was clear and effective.

-

The speaker maintained reasonable eye contact and did not rely heavily on notes.

-

The speaker’s nonverbal delivery was effective.

-

The speaker appeared confident and well-prepared.

✍️ Part 2: Open-Ended Feedback (Questions 11–12)

Respond in a few complete sentences:

-

What are two things the speaker did well in their presentation?

-

What are two areas the speaker could improve on, and how might they improve them?

📌 Rubric

Please scroll down to review the rubric for this assignment. It outlines how your peer evaluations will be assessed for completeness and thoughtfulness.

If you have any questions, feel free to reach out. Thanks for offering constructive support to your classmates!

## Discussion #9 - The Fallacy Game
Discussion resource ID: g75223857af2aeec0860585581e85a387
Module placement(s): Week 14
Prompt:
Sometimes, one of the most effective ways to explain something is an example by negation. For this discussion, we're going to focus on intentionally making a bad argument so that we can learn how to avoid those mistakes moving on. Here's what you'll be doing.

Initial Post

- Select a topic to present on: You can revisit the topics from your previous speech or you can utilize a segment of your upcoming speech if you'd like. Either way, make sure that it's a socially relevant presentation.

- Next, review our resources on fallacies earlier in this module. There are a total of 10, but if you really want to go wild, you can draw from this larger resource: Fallacy Master List .

- Develop an introduction and main point for your topic, but with a twist: You should intentionally make a faulty argument incorporating one or more of the fallacies. This should be around 2 minutes.

- Take some time to practice, then, once you're confident, record a take and upload it to this thread.

The key here is going to be subtlety. You can certainly incorporate humor if it's suitable, but don't be too over the top.

Responses

- Review at least two of your peers. Ideally, the first one should be a person who has not received a response.

- Identify which fallacy you think they're employing. If you're really stumped on the terminology, describe what the weakness in their argument is.

- Offer a suggestion for how the argument can be strengthened, while providing evidence (i.e. a source) to help them better support the argument.

Your response is not required to be a recorded video, but you're welcome to do so if you'd like.

Have fun with your initial post, but the key here is going to be subtlety: You can certain incorporate humor if it's suitable, but don't be too over the top that it becomes a mockery, and even if you're utilizing fallacies such as ad hominem, please avoid offensive, rude or demeaning language towards a person or groups of people.

Besides, it's a bit more fun if you weave it in so expertly that you end up stumping your classmates.
Links:
- Fallacy Master List -> https://utminers.utep.edu/omwilliamson/ENGL1311/fallacies.htm

## Discussion #7 - Analyzing Advertisements
Discussion resource ID: g3bd28a25228233003498ece431e58fd3
Module placement(s): Week 11
Prompt:
Monroe’s Motivated Sequence is not only a useful way to structure a persuasive presentation, but also a format that many ads and commercial often follow without us realizing it. For instance, take a look at this old infomercial:

For this discussion, we'll be utilizing Monroe's motivated sequence to analyze a series of ads.

Initial Post

Select advertisements from two different areas (e.g. a political campaign ads; infomercials; restaurant advertisements; clothing advertisements; beauty product ads), then assess how the ad follows each step of Monroe’s motivated sequence (Attention, Need, Satisfaction, Visualization, and Action).

- A description of the ad and a link to said ad (please, when possible, select ads that have closed-captioning available)

- A listing of each part of Monroe’s Motivated Sequence and how the ad follows those criteria, as well as their effectiveness.

- Any flaws in the argument that stick out to you, if applicable.

Responses

Your response to your classmates (minimum 2) should include the following

- How convincing was the ad to you personally (would you want to buy the product for you or someone close to you? Would you support the cause)?

- What, in particular made it so effective (or not)?
Media and image descriptions:
- Iframe: https://www.youtube.com/embed/Psj1ii1wm9o?si=cww5z4qSOZaGBRn1

## Discussion #6 - Creating Effective Visual Aids
Discussion resource ID: gc474b36469d7a0868f11d45687e94a35
Module placement(s): Week 8
Prompt:
For this week's discussions, we'll be leveraging some different technologies to create a visual aid. At a minimum, you'll need the following:

- Slide / storyboard creation of your choice (e.g. Google Slides, Prezi, Power Point)

- Screencasting software that allows you to record your voice while showing your screen [ Screencastify - preferred as it is a free Chrome extension, Screenpal , Loom or even Canvas Studio]

If you have another tool you'd prefer to use, just give me a heads up: I'm glad to add it to the list for your peers' benefit. While I'm requiring a slide at a minimum, if you have extensive graphic design or video editing skills, you can utilize those (again, just run it by me first). Since we're relying on so many different technologies, I'm simply grading this on completion -- that being said, don't put off doing this until the day of, given the many moving parts.

Initial Post

Here are the steps for the initial post.

- Select an area of your upcoming presentation that you would like to focus on and would benefit from having a visual aid to explain

- Using your selected slide creator, create 1-2 slides [max] that explain the principle or idea you're trying to convey. While I embrace creativity, try to strike the balance between keeping it simple, but still well-designed.

- Once you're comfortable with your work, select one of the Screencasting software. Record a brief presentation talking us through what the slide is trying to explain.

- Once you're satisfied with the recording, upload your video.

Responses

- Select at least one classmate to respond to in Flipgrid and critique their slide. Let them know what you liked and found effective about their slide, as well as any ways that can improve it. Base your answers on the design principles from our readings and materials for this week.

If all goes well, then congratulations! You'll have a completed visual aid for your presentation.
Links:
- Screencastify -> https://www.screencastify.com/
- Screenpal -> https://screencast-o-matic.com/
- Loom -> https://www.useloom.com/

## Exercise #4 - Generative AI & Public Speaking
Discussion resource ID: g4a702f89881cb948004866b6ab24e6e8
Module placement(s): Week 7
Prompt:
This assignment involves using generative AI tools to enhance your public speaking skills. It is more involved than our standard exercises, so I strongly advise getting an early start. Be sure to review the following page: Prompt Engineering - The CREATE Model earlier in this module.

Part 1 - Utilizing Generative AI

-

Select a Speech Topic

- Choose a socially relevant issue for your speech. You can stick with one of the two topics you came up with in your proposal or do something else entirely.It does not have to be the topic you are considering for your final presentation, but it should be significant and engaging. If you're stuck, revisit some of the ideas we've discussed in class.

-

Generate a Speech Using AI

- Using a generative text tool of your choice (e.g., ChatGPT), ask it to write a 5-minute speech script on your chosen topic. Be sure to save this text.

-

Generate a Speech Using the CREATE Model

- Use the same topic, but this time utilize a prompt based on the CREATE model. Have the AI generate another 5-minute speech. Again, be sure to save this text.

-

Post Your Speeches :

- Upload the files (.doc, .docx, .pdf, or link to a Google Doc) to thediscussion thread. Include the prompts you used for each speech in your post. Congratulations! Your work is done for part 1.

Part II: Evaluation

In comparing the two submissions, answer the following questions:

- What did AI do well in creating the output?

- Where did AI fall short?

- How much of the output do you feel is useable?

- How was the experience of using AI to generate a speech entirely? What insights did you gain about the strengths and limitations of GenAI in public speaking?

While it's not required, you may also feel free to comment on any posts by your peers: This assignment is weighted as an applied exercise, but posted as a discussion to allow us to learn from one another.

## Exercise #6: Product Review
Discussion resource ID: g7376d0ae77a467c94163ee7fcc38dd19
Module placement(s): Week 12
Prompt:
Have you ever heard the statement "everyone's a critic?" With the advent of social media and streaming platforms, that statement has never been more true. For this exercise, you get to critique the critics! Here's what you'll be doing:

Step I: Locating the Review

Surf the social media or streaming platform of your choice (YouTube, Instagram, TikTok, etc.) and select any 2 product reviews to watch. The product in question can be anything of your choice: A game, a movie, a restaurant, a PC component, a fitness routine, or more -- just be sure to keep it suitably appropriate (please, no reviews of obscene material). Since you're doing multiple reviews here, you'll want to keep it to something under ten minutes for each one, ideally.

Step II: Assessing the Review

Assess each review with particular attention to how they utilize language to make their persuasive argument. Assess using the following criteria:

- Relevancy : Evaluate and describe how well the review manages to stay on topic and give information relevant to the product in question.

- Credibility : Describe anything in the review, or about the reviewer, that adds to or diminishes their credibility [pay particular choice to language or phrases they use].

- Accuracy : Based on your own experiences with the product (direct or not), or based on what other reviewers have said, how accurate does this reviewer's assessment seem? Why? Which words or phrases stand out?

- Bias : Describe anything you feel skews the review in a positive or negative fashion (e.g. language, personal details, or even their profile picture, certain phrases that slant the viewpoint).

- Delivery: How dynamic or engaging of a speaker are they over camera? What adds to / detracts from this?

Your final post should include the following components:

- A link to the video or post containing the reviews

- Your assessment of each review using the aforementioned criteria

As long as both of these components are included and complete, you'll receive full credit for this assignment.

## Discussion #8: Topic Proposal - Persuasive Speech
Discussion resource ID: g8a8ab6253f0130511576a897736e5d4a
Module placement(s): Week 12
Prompt:
Before beginning this discussion, take some time to review the guidelines for your final presentation: Speech #5 Guidelines: Advocacy Speech

For this discussion, you'll be doing some brainstorming as well as providing some ideas to your peers. Here are the specific guidelines.

Initial Post

Reflect on issues, causes, or organizations that are important to you. After thinking about this, share at least 2 ideas. Remember that you can adapt what you did for your previous presentation for your final one, so if you want to stay with that idea as one of them, you can. As with your previous topic proposal, if it is a specific organization, provide us a link to their website. In addition, answer the following questions:

- Authority: What do you bring to this presentation that might make you more or less influential in persuading an audience (consider common measures of credibility; age, experience, background knowledge, etc.)

- Barriers: What barriers do you perceive will make convincing your audience to support your position more difficult: For example, controversial topics, negative attitudes towards the topic, lack of knowledge, or common misunderstandings about the topic. You can draw upon the earlier audience analysis you performed during the term to inform this.

- Outcomes: What would your audience need to do after your presentation for you to consider it successful?

Responses

Select least 2 classmates and provide the following.

- Any additional insights to their perceptions around audience barriers, or an elaboration of what they've already posted.

- What it would take in a presentation to convince you to support their topic, or, if you're already in support, what would further motivate you to action?

- What part(s) of their proposed topic interest you as an audience member.

## Discussion #5 - CRAAP Checklist
Discussion resource ID: g2533a17e3257ae77fced9f46b170d8cf
Module placement(s): Week 7
Prompt:
In last week's discussion, you should have received at least two potentially credible sources from your classmates in relation to your topic. This week, we're going to test just how credible those sources are by using the CRAAP method. If you need a refresher and don't want to click back earlier pages in the module, a former classmate of mine created a wonderful guide: The CRAAP Test.

Also, to complete this exercise, you'll need to refer to the following worksheet: CRAAP Test Worksheet

Initial Post

- Review the sources you were given from Discussion #4; if you missed the discussion, did not receive any posts from your peers, or have changed your mind on your topic, then research two new sources.

- Evaluate these sources using the CRAAP Test Worksheet (you don't need to upload a completed worksheet).

- Post the score breakdown for each category for both sources.

- Respond to the following: Why do you believe that your source received the score it did (note areas in which is excelled or failed)?

Responses

Select at least 2 peers and include the following

- Utilizing the resources you've learned about through the information literacy videos under this week's overview, select one source for each classmate.

- Review the source using the CRAAP test worksheet. Make sure that your selected source scores above at least a 35 (40-50 is ideal).

- Post your source, in APA format to your classmate and indicate why you think this source will benefit them.

Now, if all goes well, you will have, with the help of your peers, at least 3 sources you can incorporate for your upcoming presentation.

## Exercise #2: Anxiety Management Plan
Discussion resource ID: g3db76d1d6df6bec30b4b80a4178f9476
Module placement(s): Week 2
Prompt:
In the book Speaking Up Without Freaking Out , Matt Abrahams covers the concept of an "Anxiety Management Plan" as a strategy to combat communication apprehension. Abrahams styles these Anxiety Management Plans (Or "AMPs") as a series of steps (typically three, but no more than 5) a person creates an acronym or mnemonic device from to make remembering it easier. For this assignment, you'll be creating your very own Anxiety Management Plan for the quarter. The plan should include the following:

- A minimum of 3 steps

- A description of what the step entails for you (i.e. how will you execute it)

- How you feel the steps will help you in managing your communication apprehension/speech anxiety.

This is posted as a discussion thread so that the rest of your classmates can see (and maybe gain inspiration from) your plans, but there's no requirement to respond: Unless you want to, of course.

For reference, I've included some sample plans from previous terms.

AMP #1 - B.R.A.V.E.

Anxiety Management Plan (AMP): B.R.A.V.E
1. B reathe slowly and deeply - using this technique will help me relax and focus before my presentation. In addition, will help me prevent making mistakes in the delivery of my speech.
2. R eplace less assertive phrases to confident phrase - using this technique will not only help me build confidence, it will also help to engage the audience; providing them with researched knowledge and credibility.
3. A udio record yourself and practice while listening - using this technique will allow me to correct any mistakes such as pace, grammar, repetitive remarks, and tone of voice.
4. V isualize - using this technique will help to calm my nerves by creating a positive, visual representation of my speech.
5. E ngage in focused practice - using this technique will help me with my organization skills.

AMP #2: L.E.A.R.N.

Overall, I feel that using my own AMP will benefit me in various ways as a speaker not only in front of my communications class but also in front of complete strangers.

- Learn the material : It is extremely important that I'm confident in the knowledge of my topic. This will greatly affect all other areas.

- Experiment with different structure : Sometimes, a simple tweak in the order in which I present information greatly helps with the flow and delivery.

- Act it out: I practice in front of a mirror to access body language and eye contact. It's mostly about the visual performance at this stage and less about the order of information.

- Rehearse: Once I'm satisfied with the visual performance and order, I rehearse many times over to fine-tune.

- Nerve control : Now it's time to relax. This is one of the most important things for me. Being relaxed will benefit me more than anything else. I can make adjustments if I need to when I'm relaxed and confident.
Discussion and grading settings:
- topicMeta @identifier: g821b625e5e69340470df62cf79aa3749
- topicMeta/topic_id: g3db76d1d6df6bec30b4b80a4178f9476
- topicMeta/title: Exercise #2: Anxiety Management Plan
- topicMeta/position: [empty]
- topicMeta/type: topic

## Exercise #1: Your Speaking Environment
Discussion resource ID: g403f01367cfbf9673103ee54c28c001b
Module placement(s): Week 1
Prompt:
For this exercise, you'll be setting up your primary speaking space for the term. Some of us may present from multiple locations depending on the day (for instance, I might either present from a space in my home or my office on De Anza's campus). Either way, when preparing for this exercise, try to designate at least one space you know you can present from on a semi-regular basis.

Prior to completing this assignment, it will also be helpful, if you haven't already, to review the page earlier in this module about preparing your virtual speaking space.

The Checklist

Take a look at your space and assess it against the following checklist using the following scale: 1: Low, 2: Fair 3: Good and 4 : Excellent

✅ Quiet environment
✅ Clean, distraction-free background
✅ Good lighting on your face
✅ Camera positioned at eye level
✅ Clear audio quality
✅ Strong internet connection
✅ Organized workspace

For instance, if I were to assess my office space at De Anza:

- Quiet Environment: Fair; mostly quiet, but depending on the time of the day, my colleagues or students might be passing through.

- Background: Excellent: The only thing behind me is a wall and a bookshelf.

- Lighting: Fair; the office lighting isn't the best, but in a pinch I can move my work station to have front-facing lighting

- Camera Position: Excellent: A good ergonomic set up

- Clear Audio Quality: Good: The workstation microphone works well, but I also have a pair of good-quality headphones with a mic.

- Internet Connection: Excellent: Ethernet connected to the campus internet. Our tax dollars at work!

- Organized Workspace: I keep my office relatively clutter-free and have a pretty large desk set up.

Photographic Evidence

Seeing is believing! Please share a photo of your space with us so we can provide some insights and strategies for improvement. Now, if you're genuinely uncomfortable with your classmates seeing this space (though arguably we will during the presentation) you can send it to me directly.

For reference, here's a picture of my office space at De Anza. Let it not be said that I demand anything of you that I wouldn't do myself.

Reflection

Finally, include your response to the following question:

How does your virtual presentation space reflect your professionalism? What changes can you make to enhance your setup for future presentations?

I'd like you to be thoughtful in this response, but please don't feel the need to make it an essay: A few sentences will suffice.

In sum, your submission should include the following:

- Your checklist assessment

- A photo of your space

- Your answer to the reflection question.

- Optional: Solicitation of any advice on your space ('do any of you have tips on lighting or things I should remove?)

While this falls into the "Applied Exercises" category I do have it set as a discussion so that we can learn from one another. A response is not required, but , if you have any advice or tips for your classmates about their space, please feel free to respond!

## Discussion #1: Playing the Name Game.
Discussion resource ID: gca2541912857cadbff2e4f5e4ba740e6
Module placement(s): Week 1
Prompt:
This discussion will require you to upload a recording. If you are unfamiliar with how to do this, please refer back to the following guide under the speech modules: Guide: Creating & Uploading Virtual Presentations

The Name Game (or "The Story of My Name")

Adapted with permission from Lisa Merritt, Louisiana Tech University

What's in a name? Many of us have been going by the names our parents or caregivers gifted us, while others of us made specific decisions (legally or otherwise) to go by a different name. In the case of the former, have you ever stopped to consider what thought (or lack of) that your loved ones gave into selecting the name? Or, if you've changed it, what considerations went into that name? This presentation asks you to dig further into that.

Presentation Goal: Inform your audience about yourself through discussing any part of your name (first, middle, surname, nickname).

Directions

First, research your name using the following sites:

- The American Name Society

- Ancestry.com

- Names.org

- The New York Public Library

You are welcome to use other resources not listed above if you happen to find something interesting.

Second, interview a family member (or other knowledgeable person), asking about the origin of your name: How did they select the name? Is there any significance beyond the etymology (found in the sites above) that provides additional information?

Third (optional): You may include a visual aid that further connects to the significance of your name.

Procedure & Requirements

- Time Limit: Aim for 2-3 minutes. It's fine if you go slightly under! However, in the interests of time, I will need to cut off speeches that go over.

- Supporting Materials: Verbally cite at least one of the sources listed above. Personal interviews can also be cited as a source.

- Organization: While this is an introduction, do take some time to organize your ideas. As this is a short presentation, you do not need an extensive outline (or one at all): But, be sure to have an introduction, a minimum of two main points, and a conclusion: Your future presentations will have a specific outline template to follow.

- Delivery: This is meant to showcase extemporaneous delivery. That means that it's ok to have note cards, but don't read directly from them.

Responses

Select a minimum of 2 presentations to watch and reply to by answering the following prompt:

- One positive thing you found in their presentation (this can be something you found interesting, funny, or otherwise stood out to you)

- One question you have after listening to their presentation (an additional point of elaboration or something they haven't covered).

Since this is an intro assignment, I will be grading mostly on participation. That means that it's ok to mess up a little or not be perfect! I'm simply grading on completion of both components.
Links:
- Guide: Creating & Uploading Virtual Presentations -> $WIKI_REFERENCE$/pages/gd9893c8a1d9cec0f2c9038ea2d785204

## Discussion #2 - Speech Topic Proposal
Discussion resource ID: g2be15fa9b2d902ea9753b858ffd9df88
Module placement(s): Week 3
Prompt:
For this week’s discussion, you will brainstorm and come up with two topics for your second speech in Week 5, then, indicate how these topics would be of value to your peers or why you think it’s worth it for them to listen to. In addition, you will need to reply to at least 2 of your classmates and indicate what you find interesting about their topic, as well as any suggestions to help them improve their topic (such as narrowing the focus, or adding a particular main point). Combined with the survey you complete this week, this should help as your first foray into audience analysis.

Here are the guidelines:

Initial Post

Propose a total of 2 topic ideas for the presentation. Each should include the following:

-

- A description of the topic (a one or two sentence summary of what you're planning to do)

- Why you think the topic is valuable to your peers (how does it affect them? Why might they find it relatable?)

- Why you want to do this particular topic.

Responses to Peers

Select 2 of your peers to respond to (though you're welcome to do more). At a minimum, respond to the following areas:

- Describe anything that you find engaging or interesting about their proposed topic idea.

- 1-2 ways they can refine the idea (e.g. including a specific subject; things to avoid when talking to it)

## Exercise #5 - Ethics in the Media
Discussion resource ID: gf07e055dc68cac0ee540334234014e9e
Module placement(s): Week 9
Prompt:
Exploring Ethics

Sometimes, in their haste to spread the message about a noble cause, some organizations resort to ethically questionable tactics to grab attention. One ill-timed example from a few years back came from Pepsi:

Another organization known for this is is PETA, who in their support for the humane treatment of animals, will frequently use shocking ad campaigns or live demonstrations to bring attention to these issues.

We're going to be exploring the concept of ethics further through a media lens. For this exercise, search for at least 2 information campaigns used by an organization that attempts to bring attention to social issues. You'll need to include the following in your submission:

- A link to your selected campaign (this can be an image, video, or webpage).

- Answers to the following questions

- What do you think the intent was? Why?

- Do you agree with the means they used to promote their message? Why or why not?

- In what ways could the message have been modified to be more ethical? Would it have diminished the effect of the message? Why?

This activity is presented as a discussion thread so that we can see everyone's response, but responses are optional. So long as you meet the criteria (posting two links/campaigns, answering the questions) you'll receive full credit for the exercise.
Media and image descriptions:
- Iframe: https://www.youtube.com/embed/uwvAgDCOdU4?si=qh1INLR52Ruxy4h-

8. QUIZZES, CHECK-INS, QUESTIONS, AND ANSWERS
========================================================================
## Quiz record: Quiz #3
Quiz ID: g10d061ce232c9cf01a73779ddae8133c
Module placement(s): Week 3
Instructions:
Quiz Instructions

The content of this quiz is based on Chapter 3 of It's About Them: Public Speaking in the 21st Century . Be sure you have carefully read and reviewed this chapter before starting.

- Number of Attempts: You are allowed two (2) attempts on this quiz. The higher of your two scores will be recorded as your final grade.

- Time Limit: This is a timed quiz . You will have 30 minutes to complete all questions in a single sitting. The timer will begin once you start the quiz and will continue to run until you submit your answers.

- Format: The quiz may include multiple-choice, true/false, and/or short-answer questions based on key concepts, examples, and terminology from the reading.

- Preparation: Review your notes, highlighted sections, and any class discussions related to the chapter. Understanding key terms, main ideas, and examples will help you succeed.

- Before You Begin:

- Ensure you have a stable internet connection .

- Find a quiet space free from distractions.

- Allow yourself the full 30 minutes to focus, as you cannot pause or restart an attempt once it has begun.

Your best score from the two attempts will be the one recorded in the gradebook. Take your time, read each question carefully, and answer to the best of your ability.

## Quiz record: Mid-Term Checkin
Quiz ID: g1534fb34f6a77ee112cc797c18e37786
Module placement(s): Week 8
Instructions:
This survey is a quick check to see how things are going for you at the midpoint. You have had time to experience the course and try out the skills, and there is still time for me to adjust and support you.

I will read every response and follow up as needed. The more specific you can be, the more helpful I can be.

What to expect

- Time to complete: about 8–10 minutes

- Question types: multiple-choice

- Credit: This is an ungraded survey

- Privacy: your responses are for me and do not affect your grade

What to comment on

Use examples where you can.

- Pace and workload: too fast, too slow, or about right

- Clarity of instructions and rubrics for speeches and assignments

- Usefulness of feedback and peer evaluations

- Your preparation process and speaking confidence so far

- Course materials and technology, including Zoom logistics

- What is helping you learn and what is getting in the way

- One thing to keep, one thing to change, and one question you still have

How to submit

- Click Take the Survey .

- Answer all questions.

- Click Submit and check for the confirmation message.

What happens next

I will review responses after the deadline, share a brief summary with the class, and make any adjustments that can improve your experience.

If you prefer to talk through feedback, reach out via Canvas Messages or visit office hours.

Instructions:
Quiz Instructions

The content of this quiz is based on Chapter 11 of It's About Them: Public Speaking in the 21st Century . Be sure you have carefully read and reviewed this chapter before starting.

- Number of Attempts: You are allowed two (2) attempts on this quiz. The higher of your two scores will be recorded as your final grade.

- Time Limit: This is a timed quiz . You will have 30 minutes to complete all questions in a single sitting. The timer will begin once you start the quiz and will continue to run until you submit your answers.

- Format: The quiz may include multiple-choice, true/false, and/or short-answer questions based on key concepts, examples, and terminology from the reading.

- Preparation: Review your notes, highlighted sections, and any class discussions related to the chapter. Understanding key terms, main ideas, and examples will help you succeed.

- Before You Begin:

- Ensure you have a stable internet connection .

- Find a quiet space free from distractions.

- Allow yourself the full 30 minutes to focus, as you cannot pause or restart an attempt once it has begun.

Your best score from the two attempts will be the one recorded in the gradebook. Take your time, read each question carefully, and answer to the best of your ability.

Instructions:
Quiz Instructions

The content of this quiz is based on Chapter 1 of It's About Them: Public Speaking in the 21st Century . Be sure you have carefully read and reviewed this chapter before starting.

- Number of Attempts: You are allowed two (2) attempts on this quiz. The higher of your two scores will be recorded as your final grade.

- Time Limit: This is a timed quiz . You will have 30 minutes to complete all questions in a single sitting. The timer will begin once you start the quiz and will continue to run until you submit your answers.

- Format: The quiz may include multiple-choice, true/false, and/or short-answer questions based on key concepts, examples, and terminology from the reading.

- Preparation: Review your notes, highlighted sections, and any class discussions related to the chapter. Understanding key terms, main ideas, and examples will help you succeed.

- Before You Begin:

- Ensure you have a stable internet connection .

- Find a quiet space free from distractions.

- Allow yourself the full 30 minutes to focus, as you cannot pause or restart an attempt once it has begun.

Your best score from the two attempts will be the one recorded in the gradebook. Take your time, read each question carefully, and answer to the best of your ability.

## Values Speech Rubric
Rubric ID: gc60c727be4e967edc377fde9f34e21f0
Points possible: 40.0
Read only: false; reusable: false; public: false
Criterion 1: Introduction (5.0 points)
  Description: Introduction contains a hook (statement or action to engage the audience); psychological orientation (statement of how the topic is relevant to the audience) and a preview of key main points
  Rating: Full Marks (5.0 points)
  Rating: No Marks (0.0 points)
Criterion 2: Thesis (4.0 points)
  Description: Contains a clear, framing statement articulating the speaker's main core values and how they have shaped them as a person.
  Rating: Full Marks (4.0 points)
  Rating: No Marks (0.0 points)
Criterion 3: Structure & Organization (2.0 points)
  Description: Main points are clearly stated and signposted; Speech utilizes clear organizational patterns throughout (e.g. Chronological; Spatial; Topical; Problem-Solution; Cause-Effect; Monroe's Motivated Sequence).
  Rating: Full Marks (2.0 points)
  Rating: No Marks (0.0 points)
Criterion 4: Transitions (3.0 points)
  Description: Speaker uses clear, connective statements to bridge between the introduction, main points, and conclusion.
  Rating: Full Marks (3.0 points)
  Rating: No Marks (0.0 points)
Criterion 5: Content (15.0 points)
  Description: Speaker clearly articulates and describes three core values, their importance, their origin and presents artifacts which represents each value.
  Rating: Full Marks (15.0 points)
  Rating: No Marks (0.0 points)
Criterion 6: Conclusion (4.0 points)
  Description: Conclusion summarizes key ideas (logical closure), restates the importance of the topic (psychological closure) and contains a clincher statement to close out the presentation.
  Rating: Full Marks (4.0 points)
  Rating: No Marks (0.0 points)
Criterion 7: Delivery (7.0 points)
  Description: Speaker executes tenets of effective delivery as they relate to a mediated environment, including elements of vocal delivery (conversational tone, rate, and minimal vocal fillers), physical delivery (appropriate eye contact with camera and minimal note reading) and environment (lack of distracting factors within the speaker's control: e.g. lighting, external noise, interruptions).
  Rating: Full Marks (7.0 points)
  Rating: No Marks (0.0 points)

## COMM1 Exercise Rubric
Rubric ID: g21d8370e317b9e2b416082e08689b83e
Points possible: 10.0
Read only: true; reusable: false; public: false
Criterion 1: Examples of Evidence (2.0 points)
  Rating: Excellent (2.0 points) — Provides the required number of examples, sources, or materials that clearly relate to the topic and meet assignment guidelines.
  Rating: Developing (1.0 points) — Provides fewer than the required examples, or examples are only loosely related to the topic.
  Rating: Needs Improvement (0.0 points) — No examples provided, or examples are unrelated to the topic.
Criterion 2: Purpose/Intent Analysis (2.0 points)
  Rating: Excellent (2.0 points) — Clearly explains the purpose or intent of each example with well-reasoned insight and justification.
  Rating: Developing (1.0 points) — Provides a basic or unclear explanation of purpose/intent; reasoning is minimal.
  Rating: Needs Improvement (0.0 points) — No explanation of purpose/intent or explanation is off-topic.
Criterion 3: Evaluation or Critical Analysis (2.0 points)
  Rating: Excellent (2.0 points) — Thoughtfully evaluates the example(s) using relevant criteria, providing clear reasoning and supporting details.
  Rating: Developing (1.0 points) — Evaluation is present but vague, lacks depth, or is missing supporting reasoning.
  Rating: Needs Improvement (0.0 points) — No evaluation provided.
Criterion 4: Suggestions or Alternatives (2.0 points)
  Rating: Excellent (2.0 points) — Offers specific, realistic suggestions or alternatives based on the evaluation, with discussion of potential outcomes or impact.
  Rating: Developing (1.0 points) — Suggestions are present but lack detail, realism, or discussion of outcomes.
  Rating: Needs Improvement (0.0 points) — No suggestions or alternatives provided.
Criterion 5: Clarity & Completeness (2.0 points)
  Rating: Excellent (2.0 points) — All parts of the exercise are addressed clearly, with complete sentences, correct grammar, and logical organization.
  Rating: Developing (1.0 points) — Most parts are addressed but with limited clarity, incomplete answers, or noticeable grammar issues.
  Rating: Needs Improvement (0.0 points) — Responses are incomplete, unclear, or disorganized.

10. ATTACHED DOCUMENT CONTENT
========================================================================

Discipline: Communication Studies
Sub-discipline:
General Course Title: Public Speaking
General Course Description:
Theory and techniques of public speaking in democratic society. Discovery, development, and criticism of ideas in public discourse through research, reasoning, organization, composition, presentation, and evaluation of various types of speeches including informative and persuasive speeches.
Any rationale or comment
Required Prerequisites
Required Co-Requisites: None
Advisories/Recommended Preparation
Course Content:
Principles of human communication
Theory and techniques of public speaking in society, including ethics, and the use of presentation aids
Critical analysis of public discourse
Types of public speaking
Listening skills
Elements of effective public speaking, including analysis of communication situation, ethics and diversity, audience, occasion, purpose, selection of subject matter, research, evidence evaluation, organization, presentation skills, and evaluation of communication effectiveness
Laboratory Activities: (if applicable)
Course Objectives: At the conclusion of this course, the student should be able to:
Explain the basic principles of human communication;
Analyze their communication situation, audience, occasion, and purpose; and selection of subject matter;
Formulate through research, analysis, and organization of material; presentation of the message including management of communication apprehension; and evaluation of the effectiveness of their communication;
Demonstrate that they are careful and critical thinkers and communicators, both as speakers and as listeners;
Explain their relationship and ethical responsibilities to others involved in the communication transaction.
Articulate the connection between communication and culture and respect diverse perspectives and the ways they influence communication.
Methods of Evaluation:
Faculty-supervised, faculty-evaluated oral presentations in front of a live audience; speech outlines and bibliographies; critiques of speeches; quizzes and tests.
Sample Textbooks, Manuals, or Other Support Materials
University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing. Stand Up, Speak Out: The Practice and Ethics of Public Speaking. (Latest edition). LibreTexts. (OER)
Barton and Tucker. Exploring Public Speaking. (Latest edition). LibreTexts. (OER)
Floyd, K. Public Speaking Matters. (Latest edition.) McGraw-Hill.
Lucas, S. The Art of Public Speaking. (Latest edition.) McGraw-Hill.
German, K. Principles of Public Speaking. (Latest edition.) Routledge.
FDRG Lead Signature:
Date: Rev. 12/20/10; 5-year review: 4/19/2022
[For Office Use Only]
Internal Tracking Number
Descriptor Guide Sheet
Discipline: The discipline has been determined and is entered.
Subdiscipline: You may decide that a sub-discipline will serve your discipline best. For example, biology faculty may or may not decide to identify subdivisions (cellular vs. organismic, or marine, or ecology/environmental). Discipline faculty will determine what best serves their needs.
General Course Title: Insert a course name in this field that is generally used and will be widely recognized. It need not be the actual course title at all colleges or universities but should describe the topic of the course.
Minimum Units: Indicate the minimum number of units expected of this course, based on semester configuration; we will later offer a conversion into quarter units.
Proposed Number: Use the numbering protocol to assign a tentative number to the course; like the sub-discipline or general course title, during your drafting stages, this number can be changed.
Proposed Suffix: If desirable, add an “L” after the number in the box to indicate a lab; or an “S” to indicate this course is part of a sequence.
Rationale or Comment: Use this space to provide explanation to the field about the number; during the drafting stage, you may also use this space to record a request for an additional suffix or modification of the numbering protocol.
Required Prerequisites or Co-Requisites: List any courses required to be completed prior to taking the listed course; if there is not agreement among segmental faculty about the prerequisites, you might consider describing a similar course without those prerequisites or listing only Advisories/Recommended Preparation (see below). A co-requisite does not mean in the CCCs what it may mean for the 4-year institutions.
Advisories/Recommended Preparation: These recommendations for courses, experiences, or preparation need not be validated; they can be good-faith and generally accepted recommendations from discipline faculty that further the students’ chances of success in this or subsequent courses.
Course Content: Count content should list all the expected and essential topics of the course. If this course is a lab/lecture combination, the Lab content should be spelled out separately.
Course Objectives: List the course objectives, competencies, or skills that the students should be able to demonstrate upon completion of the course. Community college faculty should be attentive to explicitly linking the objectives to the topics covered. If this course is a lab/lecture combination, again the learning objectives should be spelled out separately and be linked to the topics covered in the lab component of the course. Use additional sheets as needed.
Methods of Evaluation: List those methods you anticipate would be used to observe or measure the students’ achievement of course objectives (e.g., quizzes, exams, laboratory work, field journals, projects, research, demonstrations, etc.)
Textbooks: Recent (published within the past 5-6 years) college-level texts, materials, software packages can be suggested here. While texts used by individual institutions and even individual sections will vary, enter examples of representative work. If this is a lab course or a lab/lecture section, remember to include an example of a lab manual.
FDRG Lead’s Signature and Date: When the descriptor template has been finalized by the FDRG is in final form and is ready for posting, the Lead should send this completed and signed document to Katey Lewis at
HYPERLINK "mailto:Katey@asccc.org"
Katey@asccc.org
who will post the descriptor and solicit review and comment prior to finalizing the descriptor for the next phase of the C-ID Project.
Prerequisite or co-requisite course need to be validated at the CCC level in accordance with Title 5 regulations; co-requisites for CCCs are the linked courses that must be taken at the same time as the primary or target course.
Advisories or recommended preparation will not require validation but are recommendations to be considered by the student prior to enrolling.
