16.1 Online Public Speaking
Traditionally, public speaking has been understood as a face-to-face exchange between a designated speaker
and an audience. In fact, when you imagine a pub lic speaker, you likely picture a person standing on a stage
with a podium and speaking in front of a live audience.
However, new media and digital technologies have begun expanding both our access to public speakers and
our platforms to speak and reach new audienc es. YouTube—a global video sharing servic e—has more than
1.8 billion monthly users (Gilbert, 2018), and these are just pe ople who log in! If y ou’re like us, y ou’ve likely
watched hours of content published on YouTube, from instructional videos to political commentary. You may
even access videos on Instagram Liv e or F acebook. With access to these p latforms, speakers are no w able to
broadcast their insights and advocacies to a global audience.
Businesses, too, have begun using online public speaking. Webinars, video conferences, and digital speakers
have permeated professional industries, and it’s becoming increasingly important to consider best practices for
creating speeches and being in the audience for online public speeches.
The Covid-19 pandemic made ev en more pe ople familiar with online too ls for public speaking. When it
was too dangerous to gather in person, pe ople began using te chnologies such as Zoom and Mic rosoft Teams
to host virtual classes, me etings, and gatherings. E ven when it w as possible to me et face-to-face again, man y
organizations and w orkplaces continued to use online p latforms for meetings and presentations be cause it’s
easier for people to join a Zoom call from their office or home than it is to physically meet in one location.
16.1 ONLINE PUBLIC SPEAKING | 373

[PDF page 386]
Like any appro ach to ward pub lic speaking, online pub lic speaking off ers a v ariety of opportunities and
constraints. Below, we outline what digital public speaking is and how to prepare to speak online.
Online Public Speaking
Online public speaking—also kno wn as digital oratory—is a “thesis-driv en, vocal, embodied public address
that is housed within (online) new me dia platforms” (Lind, 2012, p. 164). Like all pub lic speeches, an online
speech should be well-prepared, organized, well-reasoned, and well-rehearsed. Purpose, synchronicity, and the
audience all play key roles in online public speaking.
Purpose
Online speaking opportunities are not c reated e qually, and each spe ech will ha ve a diff erent general
purpose—informing, persuading, or entertaining. Remember that just like other f orms of pub lic speaking,
digital oratory re quires a thesis statement, and the purpose of y our spe ech will dictate ho w y ou c raft the
information that y ou’re going to present. W ith ready ac cess to vide o te chnology that c an be transmitte d
through our p hones, it c an be tempting to log in and let our f ollowers into our liv es through a stream-of-
consciousness vlog, but that’s not the type of digital oratory that constitutes prepared public speaking. Instead,
prepare b y c onsidering y our purpose f or speaking and y our thesis, then organize y our spe ech around the
answer.
For e xample, y ou might be p articipating in—or leading—a liv e w ebinar via Zoom or re cording an
instructional vide o that e xplains ho w to use a new pie ce of te chnology and which y ou might up load to
YouTube. Each speech will have a different purpose and, in turn, diff erent expectations on what y ou should
include. Once you’ve identified the goal, use earlier chapters to begin crafting content.
Synchronicity
Synchronicity describes whether your digital oratory will be deliv ered live or recorded for people to use later;
if you’re presenting a speech live, you’re speaking synchronously, meaning your audience is experiencing it in
real-time. Some online pub lic speeches occur synchronously. For example, if y ou’re speaking to a non-profit
organization about a local food pantry project through Zoom and the members of the organization are tuning
in live to watch and hear your presentation, the speech is synchronous. In synchronous online speaking, many
of the same f ace-to-face speaking princip les app ly. Live presentations are ep hemeral, meaning they happen
once. In s ynchronous online speaking—unless it’s being re corded—you ha ve one chanc e to c reate a clear
message, so it’s imperative that your content and information are crafted for clear understanding.
Alternatively, you may speak as ynchronously, meaning that the spe ech may be re corded and w atched at a
different time. YouTube, for example, houses many asynchronous videos, allowing audiences to tune in and
374 | 16.1 ONLINE PUBLIC SPEAKING

[PDF page 387]
watch when their schedule allows it. With an asynchronous video, speakers may have additional time to record,
watch, and re-do if ne cessary. Similarly, audiences also have the ability to re-w atch your presentation or pause
the speech, if needed.
Each option provides different opportunities and constraints.
In synchronous speaking, you may be more comfortable adopting and applying face-to-face public speaking
strategies, including integrating liv e audienc e f eedback. It’s c ommon in s ynchronous online speaking f or
audiences to post questions or pro vide live feedback, allowing you to adjust y our content and fill in gaps. If
there is a technological mishap, however, you can’t correct it later. The mishap also happens in real time, and
those barriers can influence your credibility as a speaker.
In asynchronous speaking, you can control the content more easily be cause you can re-record the material
to fix an y technological errors. Ho wever, in as ynchronous speaking, y ou cannot get liv e feedback from y our
audience, so you may be unaware if there’s a key question or issue they need answered.
The Audience
New media has expanded the audience pool for public speaking. In traditional pub lic speaking, the audienc e
is often limite d to those individuals who sho w up f or the ev ent—the audience is e xplicit (the pe ople who
are physically present in the audienc e) or disc rete (or targete d audience). In online speaking, y ou may have a
discrete or dispersed audience (an audience whose members hear the spe ech in different times and locations).
These different audience types, along with synchronicity, alter how audience engagement can occur.
Consider our earlier example about presenting to a non-profit organization through Zoom. In this example,
it’s likely that y ou’re a ware of who the audienc e is, so y ou’re ab le to link y our c ontent to the disc rete (or
defined) audienc e. Ho wever, in other instanc es, y our audienc e ma y be disperse d and more difficult to
determine. If you become passionate about a local policy, for example, and decide to post a speech on YouTube,
the audienc e is disperse d be cause it’s unclear who will click the link. W ith a disperse d audienc e, it c an be
difficult to make spe cific references or calls to action be cause geographic locations may alter what individuals
are able to do.
With a disperse d audience, there’s also an inc reased risk that audienc e members w on’t view y our digital
speech. Digital communication has led to information overload – we’ve all experienced it. If you’re like us, you
might scan through Instagram stories, clicking past images or videos that don’t catch your attention. If you’re
posting a digital speech with a less-defined audience, the first few lines—the attention-getter—become crucial
to hook them into watching. Spend a little extra time crafting and rehearsing your attention-getter.
Being a M ember of t he A udience: You’ll likely be an audienc e member f or man y online pub lic
speeches—synchronous and asynchronous. Remember to take your position as an audience member seriously
and avoid negative comments or trolling behavior. Even if you don’t know the speaker, how you contribute to
the dialogue online (or ho w you communicate) still functions c onstitutively, so make sure the w orld-making
that you’re participating in is ethical.
16.1 ONLINE PUBLIC SPEAKING | 375

[PDF page 388]
As you can see, there are quite a f ew variations that define the c ontext of a digital spe ech: an inf ormative,
asynchronous spe ech with a disc rete audience; a persuasiv e s ynchronous spe ech with a disperse d audience.
The more information you have about these v ariations, the more y ou can be prepared to digitally speak with
confidence and clarity.
Rehearsing to Speak Online
Rehearsing to speak online c an feel a bit odd, espe cially when vide o software enters the mix. Y ou’ll be more
effective in rehearsal if you’re aware of the speaking context, including the categories mentioned in the previous
section: purpose, synchronicity, and audience. Knowing the context will and should inform how you rehearse
for a digital speech because you should always rehearse under the conditions that you’ll speak.
Pro-Tip: Rehearse under the conditions that you’ll speak.
Integrate the presentation strategies f or an online spe ech as y ou w ould f or other spe eches—including
the purposeful dev elopment of v erbal, nonverbal delivery, and presentation aids. There are a f ew additional
variables for delivering a speech digitally that we’ll track below.
Verbal Delivery
Verbal delivery is key in a digital speech—particularly webinars or web conferencing where your vocals overlay a
slideshow and your body isn’t visible to an audience. Verbal enunciation, punctuation, rate, and pauses become
key to maintaining y our audiences’ attention. “Energy” be comes a key w ord—an energetic v oice has v ariety
and interest to it.
Audio-recording yourself during rehearsal on your smartphone or other device is a good first step, followed
by thinking c ritically and honestly about whether y our voice is listless, flat, or lacks energy . Since we tend to
have a lower energy level when we sit, some experts suggest that web conference speakers stand to approximate
the real speaking e xperience. As w e ha ve mentione d repeate dly throughout this boo k, prep aring means
practicing your speech orally and physically, many times.
Sound and projection are two variables that can affect your verbal delivery in digital contexts. It’s important
to rehearse with an y technology—including a mic rophone—that will be present and in the p hysical context
that you’ll record the formal speech. If you have a microphone, you will need to alter your projection level. If
you don’t have a microphone, be aware of how the recording device will pick up sound—including your voice
and other noise around you.
At the beginning of the p andemic, when many of us w ere just learning ho w to use Zoom, w e learned the
hard way about what can be heard on camera if we weren’t careful when meetings were interrupted by barking
dogs, passing traffic outside, the voices of others who were not on camera, or the sounds of toilets flushing or
lawn crews working next door.
376 | 16.1 ONLINE PUBLIC SPEAKING

[PDF page 389]
Remember that extra noise is distracting: it can influence your credibility and the likelihood that an audience
will continue listening.
Nonverbal Delivery
When rehearsing your nonverbal delivery, ask, “What’s visible in the video?”
If your body is visible, you should rehearse with Chapter 9 in mind. As you rehearse, be conscious of where
the camera will be. Will there be just one? Will there be multiple cameras? How far away is the camera? In some
instances, audiences may have the ability to view y our speech from multip le vantage points. Being a ware of
where those cameras are—one or multiple—is key to rehearsing your eye contact and facial expressions.
Eye contact is still a key p art of a digital speech. While you can avoid staring directly into the camera for an
extended period of time, audienc es still want some form of engagement, and ey e contact allows you to make
that connection. If y ou are re cording the spe ech with or without a liv e audience, view the c amera as y our
“audience substitute.”
Background and Lighting
Your b ackground is also p art of y our vide o’s non verbal aesthetics. M ake sure that y ou c onsider ho w the
background might translate to y our audience. Is it mess y? Distracting? Is it a white b ackground? If so , you
should avoid wearing white and disappearing into the w alls. Are there windows behind you with bright light
streaming through, making you almost invisible? If so, can you close the blinds or curtains or move? Can you
set up a ring light to offset the light c oming through outside sourc es and enhanc e your appearance? Does
your videoconferencing software have filters that b lur out imperfections that might otherwise be heightene d
by visual photography?
Camera Positioning
Because your facial expressions and body language are also visib le in a digital spe ech, you will w ant to p ay
some attention to how the camera(s) is positioned. Don’t position the camera so closely that your head fills the
frame, or so far away that you are a tiny object in the frame. To look your best, position the camera so that you
are viewed from above from between 15° to 30° from ey e level. Filming yourself at eye-level might make your
facial features appear flat, while filming yourself from below eye level gives the impression that you are towering
over your audience. If y our camera is in y our laptop c omputer, try b alancing it on a small stack of boo ks to
achieve an optimal level.
Wardrobe, Hair, and Makeup
Wear appropriate clothing on all parts of your body. By now, everyone has heard at least one story of someone
16.1 ONLINE PUBLIC SPEAKING | 377

[PDF page 390]
in a Zoom me eting who stood up to rev eal that he w as wearing pajamas or sw eatpants below more f ormal
clothing on top, or worse. Dress as if the audienc e might se e your entire body to a void such embarrassment.
Also, make sure that your clothing looks good on camera in terms of color and lighting in your setting. Avoid
noisy jewelry such as earrings or bangles that jingle if you move. Style your hair the way you would if you were
meeting with this audienc e in person. Also , if you normally wear cosmetics when you meet with this type of
audience in person, wear them on camera as well. Your goal is to look just as you would in the same setting if
you were meeting face to face.
For more detaile d inf ormation, che ck out “ Six Tips f or Loo king Great in a Zoom M eeting,” Jeff erson
Graham (2020).
Dress Rehearsal
Rehearse under these c onditions and record your facial expressions to see how they are translating to others.
Is y our body language clear? Do some gestures or f acial e xpressions loo k e xaggerated? Can y ou adjust the
camera position or lighting? Are y our clothing and ac cessories distracting or do they make y ou fade into the
background? Do you look professional? A videoed rehearsal will provide you with answers to these questions.
Remember that eff ective rehearsal oc curs under the c onditions that y ou’ll speak. Your goal is to c reate an
aesthetic experience that honors the purpose of your speech, so being accountable to all nonverbal factors will
increase your ethos.
Additional Preparation Tips:
Make sure you will not be interrupted during the web conference.
Have notes and anything else you need at hand. While you can use a computer to display them, be conscious
of your audience’s ability to see you reading.
If y ou c an be se en, be se en—use the te chnology to y our adv antage so that y ou are not an entirely
disembodied voice talking over slides.
Presentation Aids
In some cases, an online speech will include presentation aids. It’s important to determine a) if the presentation
aid is necessary and b) if you’re able to provide that presentation aid in a different form.
First, are you certain you need a presentation aid? It c an be tempting to use a presentation aid f or a digital
speech to avoid being visible to the audience. After all, it’s common for digital presentation software to display
either a visual aid or y our body. Don’t use a visual aid to a void being seen because the audience will be much
more interested in y our embodied presentation. Se cond, do y ou know how to share y our visual aid via the
digital platform you will be speaking through? As with all other aspects of online public speaking, you need to
378 | 16.1 ONLINE PUBLIC SPEAKING

[PDF page 391]
practice beforehand so that you know what your audience will see as well as how to quickly switch from your
screen to the visual aid and back again.
If you deem that a presentation aid is abso lutely necessary (or re quired) also, ask: do I ne ed to pro vide it
live or in the re cording? If you’re presenting to a discrete audience and want to provide a graph or some data,
send the inf ormation in a report ahead of time. This will allo w your audience to f eel acquainted with the
information and can spare you from having an additional technological component.
Like any public speech, when speaking online, y ou are responsible for crafting an effective advocacy that is
composed of well-reasoned arguments that are delivered with purposeful aesthetic choices. Rehearse under the
conditions that you’ll speak. Be confident that you’re aware of
• the technology you will need,
• where it will be placed,
• which technology that you are responsible for running, and
• how your embodiment of information translates.
Sharing Audiovisual Recordings
If y ou are re cording an as ynchronous presentation to share with others, post it to a cloud first (such as
SoundCloud or YouTube) and send y our instructor the link. Audio visual recording files are too large to be
emailed easily.
Conclusion
Digital public speaking is ev olving. These tips and tactics should help not just a void the major problems but
also cross the finish line into an effective presentation.

GLOSSARY
“Breathe and Release”
a short-cut version of systematic de-sensitization appropriate for public speaking preparation
Ableism
the s ystem of beliefs and practic es that produc es a p hysical and mental standard that is proje cted as
normal for a human being and labels deviations from it abnormal, resulting in une qual treatment and
access to resources
Abstract Language
language that evokes many different visual images in the minds of your audience
Academic Books
books that are primarily written for other academics for informational and research purposes
Acculturated
learning and using a code that other group members will be able to recognize
Achievement-Oriented Leaders
strive for excellence and set challenging goals, constantly seeking improvement and exhibiting confidence
that group members can meet their high expectations
Action-Oriented Listeners
primarily interested in finding out what the speaker wants
Adjourning
this stage of group dev elopment occurs when a group disso lves because it has c ompleted its purpose or
goal, membership is de clining and support f or the group no longer e xists, or it is disso lved because of
some other internal or external cause

[PDF page 404]
After Dinner Speeches
a humorous speech that makes a serious point
Ageism
prejudice or discrimination against a particular age-group and especially the elderly
Alliteration
the repetition of initial consonant sounds in a sentence or passage
Analysis
making the connection between your support and your argument
Anaphora
the succession of sentences beginning with the same word or group of words
Antithesis
the juxtaposition of contrasting ideas in balanced or parallel words, phrases, or grammatical structures

style scholars in the v arious social scienc e fields (e.g., ps ychology, human c ommunication, business) are
more likely to use

how persons and groups should be referred to and addressed based on inclusiveness and context

set of logical premises leading to a clear conclusion

personal, social, or cultural identities that are placed on us by others

[PDF page 405]

the repetition of vowel sounds in a sentence or passage

the assumption that people today are superior to people who lived in earlier eras (Russell, 1991)

a psychological phenomenon where people confronted with conflicting information or viewpoints reach
a state of dissonance (generally the disagreement between conflicting thoughts and/or actions), which can
be very uncomfortable, and results in actions to get rid of the dissonance and maintain consonance
Cognitive Restructuring (CR)
an internal proc ess through which individuals c an deliberately adjust ho w they perc eive an action or

Connotative
the subjective or personal meaning the word evokes in people together or individually
Consensus Rule

to offer comfort in a time of grief

[PDF page 432]
APPENDIX A: CHECKLIST FOR
ACCESSIBILITY
This title has been reviewed to meet these accessibility practices:
Organizing Content
• Content is organized under headings and subheadings.
• Headings and subheadings are used sequentially (e.g., Heading 1, Heading 2).
Images
• Images that convey information include alternative text (alt text) descriptions of the image’s content or
function.
• Graphs, charts, and maps also include contextual or supporting details in the text surrounding the
image.
• Images do not rely on color to convey information.
• Images that are purely decorative do not have alt text descriptions. (Descriptive text is unnecessary if the
image doesn’t convey contextual content information).
Links
• The link text describes the destination of the link and does not use generic text such as “click here” or
“read more.”
• If a link will open or download a file (like a PDF or Excel file), a textual reference is included in the link
information (e.g., [PDF]).
• Links do not open in new windows or tabs.
• If a link must open in a new window or tab, a textual reference is included in the link information (e.g.,
[NewTab]).
• For citations and references, the title of the resource is hyperlinked, and the full URL is not hyperlinked.
420 | APPENDIX A: CHECKLIST FOR ACCESSIBILITY

[PDF page 433]
Tables
• Tables are used to structure information and not for layout.
• Tables include row and column headers.
• Row and column headers have the correct scope assigned.
• Tables include a caption.
• Tables avoid merged or split cells.
• Tables have adequate cell padding.
Multimedia
• All audio content includes a transcript. The transcript includes all speech content and relevant
descriptions of non-speech audio and speaker names/headings where necessary.
• Videos have captions of all speech content and relevant non-speech content that has been edited by a
human for accuracy.
• All videos with contextual visuals (graphs, charts, etc.) are described audibly in the video.
Formulas
• Equations written in plain text use proper symbols (i.e., −, ×, ÷).1
• For complex equations, one of the following is true:
◦ They were written using LaTeX and are rendered with MathJax (Pressbooks).
◦ They were written using Microsoft Word’s equation editor.
◦ They are presented as images with alternative text descriptions.
• Written equations are properly interpreted by text-to-speech tools.2
Font Size
• Font size is 12 point or higher for body text in Word and PDF documents.
• Font size is 9 point for footnotes or endnotes in Word and PDF documents.
1. For example, a hyphen (-) may look like a minus sign (−), but it will not be read out correctly by text-to-speech tools.
2. Written equations should prioritize semantic markup over visual markup so text-to-speech tools will read out an equation in a way that makes sense
to auditory learners. This applies to both equations written in LaTeX and equations written in Microsoft Word’s equation editor.
APPENDIX A: CHECKLIST FOR ACCESSIBILITY | 421

[PDF page 434]
• Font size can be enlarged by 200 percent in webbook or ebook formats without needing to scroll side to
side.
Learn more about Pressbooks’ commitment to Accessibility.
“Checklist for Accessibility” by BCcampus is licensed under CC BY 4.0.
422 | APPENDIX A: CHECKLIST FOR ACCESSIBILITY

## web_resources\Outline Samples\Advocacy Speech Outlines\AdvocacySpeechOutline_MedShare.docx
File size: 24913 bytes
Extracted DOCX text:
[word/document.xml]
Introduction
Attention Getter:
“The Ebola epidemic continues to cripple the economies of Guinea, Liberia, and Sierra Leone, and is projected to result in Negative or contracting growth in these countries next year as the work to eradicate the Virus.”
(http://www.worldbank.org/en/news/press-release/2014/12/02/ebola-world-bank-report-growth-shrinking-economic-impact-guinea-liberia-sierra-leone)
Psychological Orientation:
Everyone seems to continue on living comfortably. Not knowing of the dangers of a pandemic. The United States is well prepared with supplies, Doctors and Scientists. Ask yourself one question though. How long can we keep Ebola under control? How long before a natural disaster hits? (Security needs – Safety, Protection)
Logical Orientation:
Organizations like Medshare are trying to supply those in need with our unwanted medical supplies. If we do not get these supplies where they are needed, there will be a bigger risk of spread. (Casual Reasoning)
Body
Main Point: Some of the countries hit hardest with the Ebola Outbreak get a lot of aid from Medshare.
Subpoint:
A. Shipping medical supplies to these nations help a lot. A lot of medical supplies in third world countries are reused. They have no choice sometimes and the risk of spreading infection grows exponentially.
B. Medshare has shipped containers a total of 6 containers (Sierra Leone 4, Guinea 1, Liberia 1) in 2014. Demand is growing due to disease in those areas.
2. A. Medshare makes this possible with the recycling and collecting of useable medical supplies. Everything has to be checked and can’t be close to expiration or it will get stuck at customs.
B. Medshare helps any nation in need. Everyone is talking about Ebola now and the outcome of a Pandemic. Any nation in need can take advantage of Medshare’s medical supplies.
B. Subpoint:
1. a. Medshare helped teams provide supplies for the Philippines after Super Typhoon Haiyan 2013. “Within days of the storm, Medshare had equipped numerous medical teams traveling to the Philippines with over 22,000 pounds of high-quality medical supplies.”
(http://www.medshare.org/media/news/224)
b. They have sent more than 1000 containers worldwide since the beginning. They have been trying to average a container a week. They just get overwhelmed with the quantity they receive sometimes.
2. a. MedShare even sends Biomedical Engineers to provide training and maintenance on high tech equipment. They set up and make sure the equipment is running.
b. These engineers don’t just go and turn the machine on. They train workers to understand and use these biomedical devices correctly.
II. Main Point: MedShare has ”collected more than 100 million dollars worth of life saving equipment and saved in excess of 2 million cubic feet from area landfills.” (Esteem needs- Self image, Recognition)
(http://www.medshare.org/about-us)
Subpoint: MedShare helps people as it helps the environment.
A. Medshare operates 3 facilities place in large metropolitan areas. They receive a lot of material and sort it out accordingly. We help provide useable supplies, which in turn don’t end up in the dump. (Casual Reasoning)
b. They are able to ship millions of dollars of equipment with the help of 320 volunteers a month.
B. Subpoint: Medshare needs volunteers.
1. There are those who can sponsor a container (25,000 dollars) and others that can donate their time.
2. Medshare gets a lot of this work done with the help of volunteers. You can help people get medical supplies and keep the environment free of any extra waste. Dr. William Foege (Bill and Melinda gates foundation) said it best, “Medshare is a truly win-win situation.”
(http://www.medshare.org/about-us/testimonials)
Main Point: Keeping the environment cleaner. (Esteem needs – Self image)
Subpoint: California is becoming a green state.
If not for the sake of getting supplies to those in need, help the environment.
Volunteering for Medshare will score you double the Karma points.
(Values throughout outline are helping others and helping the environment)
Conclusion
Logical Closure:
Non Profit organizations like Medshare help in more ways than one. Their main focus is providing Medical supplies and equipment to those in need. As they provide that service they help keep millions of pounds off the waste yard.
Psychological Closure:
There are diseases out there that increase the demand of supplies. There are Natural Disasters that destroy with no mercy. Organizations like Medshare are around to help. Clean supplies help keep diseases from spreading. (Security needs – Safety)
Clincher:
If we volunteer our time or donate to Medshare we can be saving lives without knowing it. We can keep the environment clean while helping others. Volunteer your time, to help get this equipment to those who might need it, instead of the bottom of a ton of waste.
References
World Bank Website (2014). Ebola Report Article.
Retrieved from http://www.worldbank.org/en/news/press-release/2014/12/02/ebola-world-bank-report-growth-shrinking-economic-impact-guinea-liberia-sierra-leone
MedShare. (2014). Media – Recent news.
Retrieved from http://www.medshare.org/media/news/224
MedShare. (2009). About us – The Med Share Story.
Retrieved from http://www.medshare.org/about-us
Medshare. (2014). About us – Testimonials.
Retrieved from http://www.medshare.org/about-us/testimonials
Advocacy Speech Outline Rubric
Your outline will be assessed according to the rubric below. Please review these guidelines carefully, and be sure that, where applicable, the respective sections on your outline are labeled accordingly. If your outline deviates wildly from the required template (i.e. you submit an essay, not an outline) or it contains an excess of spelling and/or grammatical errors (over 5) you may be asked to resubmit the outline (at a reduced score).
Please copy and paste a copy of this rubric into the same file as your outline. Failure to do so will result in an automatic 10% deduction from your final score.
__________________________________________________________________________
/4
Outline utilizes full, single sentences (not paragraphs) on all components (intro & conclusion are exempt from this rule)
/3
Prior to the introduction, the outline includes the following: Topic, proper Organization Pattern, Specific Purpose, and Primary Audience Outcome.
/4
Outline includes a strong thesis statement stated as a declarative statement that emphasizes the central focus or idea of the speech
.
/3
Introduction includes: Attention Getter/Hook, Psychological Orientation (puts the topic into a relevant context), and Logical Orientation (establishes credibility and previews the main points)
/3
Conclusion includes: Logical Closure (summarizes the key points), Psychological Closure (connects back to the introduction and reminds the audience of relevance) and a Clincher
/2
Full-sentence transitions are used to connect each major main point
/3
Outline contains no major spelling or grammatical errors.
/3
Includes at least 3 different APA or MLA style references cited within the body
of the outline.
/2
A reference page in accordance with APA or MLA rules is included at the end of the
outline
/2
At least two dimensions of credibility (i.e. competence, concern, trustworthiness, dynamism) are labeled clearly in the body of the outline.
/2
At least two appeals to audience needs in accordance with Maslow’s hierarchy of need are
labeled clearly in the body of the outline.
/2
At least two appeals to audience values are labeled clearly in the body of the outline.
/2
At least two instances of valid reasoning (inductive, causal, deductive, or analogy) are labeled clearly in the body of the outline.
/35

## web_resources\Outline Samples\Advocacy Speech Outlines\AdvocacySpeechOutline_SecondHarvest.docx.doc
File size: 52736 bytes
Safe best-effort legacy DOC printable strings (formatting and some text may not be recoverable):
ÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿì¥Á
PERSUSASIVE WORKSHEET
INTRODUCTION
ATTENTION GETTER: Food, always goes to waste in our pantries, fridges, and on our plates. The food we waste could help our friends, family or neighbors in need. During the Holiday Season families are in more need and pressure to provide food and presents for their families.
PSYCHOLOGICAL ORIENTATION (relate topic to this audience):
HYPERLINK "http://www.shfb.org/volunteer"
http://www.shfb.org/volunteer
is a quick 13 second video clip of a girl named Kate and her family in need and how you can help?
How many of you go to Starbucks buy a fat-free quad shot no foam latte for $5? Well that $5 can provide can provide 10 holiday meals to a family in need.
LOGICAL ORIENTATION (state thesis, then preview main points): SHFB helps families, children, soup kitchen, seniors, disabled people, and shelters in need during the holiday season and all throughout the year. 3 main reasons how you can help; Donate your time (volunteer), money, or host a food/fund drive this season or all year round.
MAIN POINT (state as a single declarative sentence): Donating is the best way to pay it forward.
SUBPOINT: How to and how much of your money.
a. Every $1 you give will provide 4 healthy meals for the people in need.
b. 95¢ of every $1 you donate goes directly to programs that feeds people in need.
Transition: Help out by volunteering.
MAIN POINT (state as a single declarative sentence): If you don’t want to donate money you can help by donating your time by volunteering.
SUBPOINT: many ways to help by volunteering.
Sorting food at one of the many warehouses is a popular way to help.
Pack and sort produce and other food for distribution.
Perfect for individuals or groups whether you come once a week or once a year.
2. Distribute Food in the Community
a. Manly with Family Harvest a monthly distribution to provide food and educational programs to low-income families with minor children
b. Manly with Brown Bag is a self-help program that gives low-income seniors a weekly distribute a bag of grocery’s
c. Best for individuals or small groups willing to commit to weekly or monthly support.
3. Ongoing volunteers.
a. To help with everything from data entry to educating about nutrition at one of the 3 main cites.
Transition: Donate with food, or host a food drive.
HYPERLINK "http://www.thefoodbank.org/calendar-of-events/"
http://www.thefoodbank.org/calendar-of-events/
for Santa Cruz and
HYPERLINK "http://www.shfb.org/events"
http://www.shfb.org/events
for Santa Clara and San Mateo Counties.
MAIN POINT (state as a single declarative sentence): Post your drive on Facebook, tweet a few hunger stats, and e-mail some people.
SUBPOINT: Host a drive it’s easy and fun for everyone!
1. Host a drive for your company, school, congregation, club, or neighborhood. You can also host a drive among your family and friends
a. high-traffic areas where the barrels and signage will be noticeable and convenient for donors
b. One barrel holds an average of 125 pounds of food.
2. How long do they run for?
a. they can go as long as 2 hours to 2 months, some donors collect food and money year round. Average drive is about 2 to 3 weeks.
3. How many people does it help?
a. 20 people give just $25, that’s $500. You’ve raised enough to provide a family of 4 enough food for 6 months.
b. Shop and pick up a little extra to drop off in the barrels on your way out at your local Safeway’s, Food Maxx, Nob Hill, and Save-Mart grocery stores. Safeway has $10 pre-packed bags of food available for purchase in the stores daily until December 29th.
LOGICAL CLOSURE (restate main points and thesis):
In conclusion SHFB is here to help when you are ready to donate your time, money, and how to host a food and fund drive. This is why and how SHFB is one of the largest non-profit local hunger
PSYCHOLOGICAL CLOSURE (relate importance and relevance to this audience): The main points/values I want you the audience to take from this persuasive speech is that you can always have fun by paying it forward by helping someone in need this holiday season and throughout the year.
CLINCHER (end with a bang, not a whimper):
Boardwalk Bowl Strike Out Hunger: Asks you to bring just one can of food to Santa Cruz Bowl from November 1st through December 31st and have one free bowling game on them.
“Hunger is actually the worst weapon of mass destruction. It claims millions of victims each year." - Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva, was the 35th President of Brazil
Cited References:
HYPERLINK "http://www.shfb.org/"
http://www.shfb.org/
HYPERLINK "http://www.shfb.org/Page.aspx?pid=886"
http://www.shfb.org/Page.aspx?pid=886
Advocacy Speech Outline Rubric
__________________________________________________________________________
Outline utilizes full, single sentences (not paragraphs) on all components (intro & conclusion are exempt from this rule)
Prior to the introduction, the outline includes the following: Topic, proper Organization Pattern, Specific Purpose, and Primary Audience Outcome.
Outline includes a strong thesis statement stated as a declarative statement that emphasizes the central focus or idea of the speech
Introduction includes: Attention Getter/Hook, Psychological Orientation (puts the topic into a relevant context), and Logical Orientation (establishes credibility and previews the main points)
Conclusion includes: Logical Closure (summarizes the key points), Psychological Closure (connects back to the introduction and reminds the audience of relevance) and a Clincher
Full-sentence transitions are used to connect each major main point
Outline contains no major spelling or grammatical errors.
Includes at least 3 different APA or MLA style references cited within the body
of the outline.
A reference page in accordance with APA or MLA rules is included at the end of the
At least two dimensions of credibility (i.e. competence, concern, trustworthiness, dynamism) are labeled clearly in the body of the outline.
At least two appeals to audience needs in accordance with Maslow’s hierarchy of need are
labeled clearly in the body of the outline.
At least two appeals to audience values are labeled clearly in the body of the outline.
At least two instances of valid reasoning (inductive, causal, deductive, or analogy) are labeled clearly in the body of the outline.
TOPIC: to persuade the audience to donate to The Second Harvest Food Bank
ORGANIZATIONAL PATTERN: Persuasive
SPECIFIC PURPOSE: of this speech is to persuade the audience to donate either their time, money, or a can a food.
PRIMARY AUDIENCE OUTCOME: why they should donate to SHFB
THESIS STATEMENT: 3 main reasons why you should donate or support the Second Harvest Food Bank of California. What kind of donation can you help with? Donate your time as in volunteering, by giving a little bit of money, and hosting a food drive or bringing food.

þÿÿÿþÿÿÿþÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿR

þÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿ

## web_resources\Outline Samples\Advocacy Speech Outlines\PersuasiveOutline_TakingPhilosophy.docx
File size: 23187 bytes
Extracted DOCX text:
[word/document.xml]
Topic: Why you should take philosophy classes at De Anza
Organizational Pattern: What? – So What? – Now What?
Purpose: To persuade the audience to take at least one philosophy class before leaving
Outcome: By the end of the presentation, everyone should feel compelled to sign up for a philosophy class
Thesis: Besides fulfilling GE requisites, taking a philosophy class provides the means for serious introspection to evaluate one’s own personal belief, then to affirm them or revise them in order to live a morally wholesome life.
Introduction
Hook: So there I am, 7:30 AM in the first class of my first quarter here at De Anza. I enrolled into this class called Critical Thinking and first thing the professor says the moment he walks in is, “Good morning class, I’m Antono Ramirez, you can call me Tono, and holy fuck is it early” At first, the class was pretty interesting from just the entertainment value the professor offered into the class. By the end of the quarter, I was hooked. I went on to signing up for every class he offered every quarter. I personally think it’s pretty rare to say that this professor has inspired me seriously take philosophy serious.
Psychological Orientation: “As much as we think philosophy is detached from our daily lives, whenever we state an assertion or doubt someone else’s, we are practicing philosophy. Albeit very rudimentary and seemingly dismissive aspect, however the whole entire foundation of philosophy ever since the beginning of rational thinking is about taking a proposition and convincing an audience that it is true and the premises valid.
Logical Orientation: “For those who are not interested into divulging into morality due to the school’s overall left leaning slant, rest assured the professor I will recommend to you guys does a pretty good job keeping neutrality. However, if that still does not convince you, you can fulfill many requirements of your IGETC with just philosophy classes
As for my own personal experience with philosophy classes, I have almost taken every single one of the core philosophy classes. I will be taking the last one next quarter. Thanks to philosophy, I have decided to take another year at De Anza in order to fulfill the rest of my GEs and intermediate film classes. (experience)
Body
Lets start with a little exercise.
Take a look at these quotes
“It means to say that very strong gun control advocates may not get everything they want. And people who think they should have a missile launcher in their backyard as a Constitutional right may not have that” (Huffington Post, Bernie Sanders)
“I would leave the question of marriage to the states, I would leave the question of adoption to the states. That’s the way it has been for two centuries of our nation’s history until five unelected judges in an illegitimate and wrong decision decided to seize the authority over marriage and wrongfully tear down the marriage laws of all 50 states” (Washington Post, Ted Cruz)
“California’s sanctuary policies are illegal and unconstitutional and put the safety and security of our entire nation at risk. Thousands of dangerous & violent criminal aliens are released as a result of sanctuary policies, set free to prey on innocent Americans. THIS MUST STOP!” (Twitter, Donald J. Trump)
These classic techniques used by politicians on both parties.
This in it of itself is not fallacious, just shallow with no argument to be made, however when someone quotes these politicians or draw conclusions from there, then it becomes fallacious. Hence the blind leading the blind (analogy)
Transition: I am assuming nobody wants to live a life with the wrong beliefs
So what does this have to do with philosophy at all? Maslow: Self- Esteem
In this current state of society where philosophy classes are not mandated at all, just taking a basic course on how to construct an argument already puts you so much further than the average student.
Current state of politics, bi-partisan, deeply entrenched in party and not truth.
Political figures are practically being sponsored by their respective parties to spout emotional statements 2. This is in turn is allows people to hold unjustified beliefs (causual)
Many of you guys have most likely argued with somebody over something you feel deeply inclined with.
What if those beliefs are wrong?
Can you justify them in a rational procedure rather than drawing from emotion?
To argue with emotion is no different than what Trump has been doing.
Whether or not you think he’s fit as president, emotional arguments does not equal good arguments (appealing to fear)
Transition: Lets now move onto my final point
Taking a philosophy class is less about being able a pretentious pseudo philosopher but rather learning life skills.
By taking philosophy classes, you are forced to reconcile with your own personal beliefs.
Not going to tell you what is right or wrong.
The point is for you to take the classes and figure out for yourself.
I personally would not have continued
Tono Ramirez sparked interest
Highly recommend for you also to take his classes as well
Take Critical Thinking and Writing for Area 1b req
Take Knowledge and Reality and Ethics for humanities
Conclusion Maslow Self-Actualization
I cannot affirm to you all that I am now some sort of transcended human being.
I have more questions now and have hardly any answers to any of the original question I initially had.
I also understand the fact that philosophy community has made virtually zero progress in terms of being able to answer the most sought questions such as “is there a God” or “what is the meaning of life” may deter you away from approaching philosophy. (concern)
I hope I have at least garnered your interest to take a philosophy class due to
Examples of modern politics straying away from truth
Being concerned with your own personal beliefs
With a professor who has inspired me so much I am taking another year
Philosophy should not be thought of a physical activity in which you brood over a niche topic and have a heated debate.
Rather think of it as a state of mind in which you constantly evaluate your own way of thinking.
Clincher: I cannot guarantee some sort of enlightenment at all or an elevated state of being from taking philosophy classes. If anything you should be glad I am not saying that because that is exactly the type of things a cult would say. All I can implore to you is that I can guarantee every one of you guys want to live a good life. We want to seek comfort within the satisfaction of knowing we are not committing immoralities. Try taking a philosophy class, it may guide you toward the right way.
Work Cited
Works Cited
Fix, Team. “The Fox News GOP Debate Transcript, Annotated.” The Washington Post, WP Company, 3 Mar. 2016, www.washingtonpost.com/news/the-fix/wp/2016/03/03/the-fox-news-gop-debate-transcript-annotated/?utm_term=.8d2efee6af02.
Lavender, Paige. “Bernie Sanders: Hillary Clinton Has 'Misstated' My Comments On Gun Control, Shouting.” The Huffington Post, TheHuffingtonPost.com, 7 Nov. 2015, www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/bernie-sanders-hillary-clinton-shouting_us_563d5010e4b0b24aee4a8049.
Trump, Donald (realDonaldTrump). “California’s sanctuary policies are illegal and unconstitutional and put the safety and security of our entire nation at risk. Thousands of dangerous & violent criminal aliens are released as a result of sanctuary policies, set free to prey on innocent Americans. THIS MUST STOP!” 13 March 2018, Tweet

## web_resources\Outline Samples\Outline_Template_COMM1.doc

Safe best-effort legacy DOC printable strings (formatting and some text may not be recoverable):
ÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿì¥Á
OUTLINE TEMPLATE – COMM 1
Using this Template
This template follows the basic outline structure I am expecting to see for your major presentations (Speeches #2, #3, and #5). The blue text (such as in this sections) contains descriptions of what you should fill in the area with and can be deleted as you fill out this outline.
The body of this template has 3 main points with three levels of subordination within it. You can feel free to either add additional main and subpoints as necessary or even condense it. That said, these presentation should, at a bare minimum, have 2 main points with
If you’re used to creating outlines and know the standard, accepted format (Roman numerals for main points; capital letters for the first level of subordination; etc.) then you can create your own outline from scratch: Just be sure to check the rubric to ensure you hit all of the criteria.
Lastly, but most importantly: Later speeches have additional requirements to be included within the outline (i.e. in text citations, reference pages, noting audience appeals). Make sure to review the guidelines, as well as the sample outline on Canvas
[Perhaps the easiest part of this outline, unless you’re sleep deprived or dealing with an identity crisis]
[The topic of your speech; feel free to use a creative title!]
SPECIFIC PURPOSE:
[In 1 or 2 sentences, articulate what you aim to accomplish]
AUDIENCE OUTCOME: [What should your audience do, think, or feel after the speech?]
INTRODUCTION
ATTENTION GETTER: [Craft a statement that immediately hooks the audience; or describe what actions you will do to gain their attention]
PSYCHOLOGICAL ORIENTATION:
[In a sentence or two, state how this speech is relevant to your audience]
THESIS STATEMENT: [State the central idea of your speech as a declarative sentence]
LOGICAL ORIENTATION: [Preview the main points of your statement]:
Stick to 2-5 main points
Use complete sentences for the main points (I, II, III etc.) and the first level (A, B, C etc.) of subordination
MAIN POINT (state as a single declarative sentence):
Transition: [Insert a full-sentence transition here]
LOGICAL CLOSURE: [Use a few sentences to summarize your main points and restate your thesis here]
PSYCHOLOGICAL CLOSURE: [Re-emphasize the importance and relevance of the speech to your audience. If you are doing a persuasive presentation, incorporate a call to action]
CLINCHER: [Include a statement or action that brings your speech to an end, such as a memorable quote, catchy statement, or challenge. Barring that, a simple ‘thank you for your time; this concludes my speech’ can work]
Include any references that were cited in your presentation here in APA format.
The Online Writing Lab at Purdue has instructions on how to format your reference page here:
HYPERLINK "https://owl.purdue.edu/owl/research_and_citation/apa_style/apa_formatting_and_style_guide/reference_list_basic_rules.html"
https://owl.purdue.edu/owl/research_and_citation/apa_style/apa_formatting_and_style_guide/reference_list_basic_rules.html

þÿÿÿþÿÿÿþÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿÿR

## web_resources\Outline Samples\Social Issues Speech Outlines\Sample Informative Outline - NAMI.docx
File size: 21743 bytes
Extracted DOCX text:
[word/document.xml]
TOPIC: National Alliance on Mental Illness (NAMI) and its mission to change public perspective on mental illness and provide support for the mentally ill and their family and friends.
ORGANIZATIONAL PATTERN: Topical.
SPECIFIC PURPOSE: The purpose of this informative speech is to identify and describe a non-profit organization, NAMI, that I believe supports an importance cause.
PRIMARY AUDIENCE OUTCOME: My primary audience objective is to familiarize my audience with the existence and purpose of the National Alliance on Mental Illness.
THESIS STATEMENT (state central idea, the essence of the speech): The National Alliance on Mental Illness is a pioneer for change in the diagnosis, treatment and successful management of mental illness.
ATTENTION GETTER: Mental illness does not discriminate. People of all ages, races, genders, education and incomes are afflicted with mental illness. It is estimated that as many as 1 in 4 people suffer from mental illness (National Institutes of Health, National Institute of Mental Health, n.d.). It is stifling statistics like this that prompted the formation of the National Alliance on Mental Illness, NAMI, a grassroots organization striving to increase understanding of mental illness.
PSYCHOLOGICAL ORIENTATION (relate topic to this audience): How many of you have seen the commercials on t.v. about depression, anxiety, bipolar and other illnesses? Mental illnesses are not caused by weakness of mind, negative personality traits or lack of parenting skills. Because of this common misconception, people who suffer from mental illness often feel ashamed to seek help; and even trained, educated professionals often fail to make proper diagnoses and treatment recommendations.
LOGICAL ORIENTATION (state thesis, then preview main points): Like other medical illnesses, awareness, proper identification, adequate lifelong treatment are the key to managing symptoms of mental illness and coping with the disease. NAMI works with families, communities, health care organizations and even the government to promote education, support and advocacy to improve treatment outcomes and quality of life for people with mental illness and their loved ones.
BODY
I.MAIN POINT: The National Alliance on Mental Illness is a nonprofit advocacy group for mental health.
A. SUBPOINT: NAMI is a grassroots organization, formed from ordinary people in the
community who saw the need for serious change in mental health care. They accomplish this through:
Support.
Education.
Awareness.
Advocacy.
Research.
B.SUBPOINT: Founded in 1979, NAMI has become the largest group of its type and has established itself as a formidable proponent of mental health awareness. They sponsor events such as:
Mental Illness Awareness Week.
NAMI Walks.
C.SUBPOINT: It is NAMI’s mission to provide hope and help to people with mental illness, their families and their communities.
In their mission statement, “NAMI recognizes that the key concepts of recovery, resiliency and support are essential to improving the wellness and quality of life of all persons affected by mental illness” (NAMI, n.d.)
NAMI challenges the idea that people with mental illness cannot live normal or fulfilling lives because of their disease.
Promoting access to services, treatment, support and research is one of the primary goals of NAMI.
Transition: According to Linda Rosenberg in her article A Silent Public Health Crisis (Rosenberg, 2011), no other medical illnesses “are as widespread or contribute as much to the burden of illness in the U.S. as do mental illnesses.”
.
II.MAIN POINT: Mental illness is a medical condition and it is a major public health issue in the United States.
A.SUBPOINT: There are biological and genetic causes of mental illness.
B.SUBPOINT: People with mental illness have disproportionately higher rates of unemployment, homelessness and comorbidities.
C. SUBPOINT: It is predicted that mental illness and substance abuse disorders will be the primary cause of disability in the world by 2010 (HSS.org, n.d.).
Transition: The good news is mental illness is treatable.
III. MAIN POINT: NAMI asserts that awareness and access to treatment are essential to successful
management of mental illness.
A. SUBPOINT: Historically, many people suffering from mental illness received inadequate treatment for their condition secondary to discrimination.
After a 20-year-long campaign, the Mental Health Parity and Addiction Equity Act was passed in 2008, forbidding discriminatory practices with regard to insurance coverage of mental health services HHS.gov, n.d.).
In November of 2013, NAMI celebrated some final changes to this law including mandates for equal coverage for mental health and medical health benefits.
B.SUBPOINT: Medical management of mental illness is absolutely necessary and influences the treatment outcomes and time to recovery.
C.SUBPOINT: The National Advisory Mental Health Council (Rosenberg, 2011) reports treatment success for some of the most prevalent mental illness to range from 65% to 80%.
Transition: A woman wrote about her brother’s schizophrenia and the powerful impact of stigma on his ability to be treated effectively, “stigma is hopelessness, resulting in helplessness” (Byrne, 2000).
IV. MAIN POINT: NAMI sites stigma as one of the greatest obstacles to overcome with regard to mental health care.
A. SUBPOINT: Regardless of party, NAMI works with the local, state and federal government to draft legislation to address discrimination towards persons with mental illness caused by stigma.
B.SUBPOINT: Non-profit organizations with a goal of increasing awareness and improving quality of life for the mentally ill are becoming more abundant as the number of individuals diagnosed with mental illness grows exponentially.
NAMI has many local and state affiliates, as well as groups focusing on the family, friends and caretakers of mentally ill individuals.
Esteemed Actress Glenn Close has co-founded the organization BringChange2Mind with her sister who was diagnosed with bipolar disorder and her nephew with schizophrenia. She worked with Producer Ron Howard to film a PSA in NYC in hopes to make a strong message about the prevalence of mental illness (BringChange2Mind, 2014).
C.SUBPOINT: In their publication, The Advocate, NAMI conducts an interview with Halle Berry highlighting her role in the soon to be released film, Frankie and Alice. Berry portrays a woman with mental illness and the illustrates the impact the illness has on her life and the lives of those around her (NAMI, 2014).
CONCLUSION
LOGICAL CLOSURE (restate main points and thesis): Because millions of Americans’ lives are devastated by mental illness, “NAMI works every day to save every life” (National Alliance on Mental Illness, n.d.).
PSYCHOLOGICAL CLOSURE (relate importance and relevance to this audience): Next time you see an advertisement for depression or some other mental illness, listen carefully to the symptoms. Chances are you or someone you love suffers from a mental disorder. Persons with mental illness are not alone.
CLINCHER (end with a bang, not a whimper): Remember, mental illness does not define the person and it is not a life sentence. If you are diagnosed in your lifetime with a mental illness, will you live with it or die from it? You do have a choice. Exercise your right to choose!

## web_resources\Outline Samples\Social Issues Speech Outlines\Sample Informative Outline - Ronald McDonald House.docx
