[word/document.xml]
TOPIC: The topic of this informative speech is the Ronald McDonald House Charity.ORGANIZATIONAL PATTERN: The organizational pattern is definitions, description, and considerations.SPECIFIC PURPOSE: This specific purpose of this speech is to provide information regarding what the Ronald McDonald House is and what it does for people across the United States.PRIMARY AUDIENCE OUTCOME: My desired outcome is to make the audience more informed about the Ronald McDonald House Charity.THESIS STATEMENT (state central idea, the essence of the speech): The Ronald McDonald House provides stability and resources for families everywhere. TOPIC: The topic of this informative speech is the Ronald McDonald House Charity.ORGANIZATIONAL PATTERN: The organizational pattern is definitions, description, and considerations.SPECIFIC PURPOSE: This specific purpose of this speech is to provide information regarding what the Ronald McDonald House is and what it does for people across the United States.PRIMARY AUDIENCE OUTCOME: My desired outcome is to make the audience more informed about the Ronald McDonald House Charity.THESIS STATEMENT (state central idea, the essence of the speech): The Ronald McDonald House provides stability and resources for families everywhere. OUTLINE WORKSHEET
TOPIC: The topic of this informative speech is the Ronald McDonald House Charity.
ORGANIZATIONAL PATTERN: The organizational pattern is definitions, description, and considerations.
SPECIFIC PURPOSE: This specific purpose of this speech is to provide information regarding what the Ronald McDonald House is and what it does for people across the United States.
PRIMARY AUDIENCE OUTCOME: My desired outcome is to make the audience more informed about the Ronald McDonald House Charity.
THESIS STATEMENT (state central idea, the essence of the speech): The Ronald McDonald House provides stability and resources for families everywhere.
TOPIC: The topic of this informative speech is the Ronald McDonald House Charity.
ORGANIZATIONAL PATTERN: The organizational pattern is definitions, description, and considerations.
SPECIFIC PURPOSE: This specific purpose of this speech is to provide information regarding what the Ronald McDonald House is and what it does for people across the United States.
PRIMARY AUDIENCE OUTCOME: My desired outcome is to make the audience more informed about the Ronald McDonald House Charity.
THESIS STATEMENT (state central idea, the essence of the speech): The Ronald McDonald House provides stability and resources for families everywhere.
INTRODUCTION
ATTENTION GETTER:
“It’s something we see every day – children healing faster because they’re surrounded by their families. And while RMHC may not be able to make the medicine taste better or erase the pain of a much-needed treatment, they can help lessen the burden for more than seven million families each and every year” (http://www.rmhc.org/about-us).
PSYCHOLOGICAL ORIENTATION (relate topic to this audience): How many of you know what the Ronald McDonald House Charity is?
LOGICAL ORIENTATION (state thesis, then preview main points): The Ronald McDonald House provides stability and resources for families everywhere, whether it be providing housing that’s near a hospitalized child, the expense of staying together in another city, or even getting basic medical or dental care.
BODY
2-5 MAIN POINTS PREFERREDUSE ONLY COMPLETE SENTENCES
I.MAIN POINT (state as a single declarative sentence): To understand what the Ronald McDonald House Charity does, you should know how it got started.
A. SUBPOINT: The first Ronald McDonald House was created in 1974.
1.
a. Fred Hill, a Philidelpia Eagles football player, and his wife Fran found themselves sleeping in the waiting room of a hospital night after night as their 3-year-old daughter Kim was being treated for Leukemia.
b. They knew there had to be a solution for families like them who had to travel to get special and extended care for their children.
2.
a. Together with the help of Jim Murray, the Eagles GM, Dr. Audrey Evans, and Don Tuckerman from the local McDonalds advertising agency, their dream became a reality.
b. Tuckerman and local McDonald’s Regional Manager Ed Rensi launched the St. Patrick’s Day Green milkshake, known now as the Shamrock Shake, promotion. (testamonial) (McDonald’s Corporation).
B.SUBPOINT:
1.
a. Funds raised from this went to purchasing an old house located near the hospital.
b. In 1974, the first Ronald McDonald house was built. (statistic)
2.
a. By 1979, 10 more Houses opened. (statistic)
b.By 1984, local communities founded 60 more Houses (statistic)
3.
a. Today, more than 250 Ronald McDonald House programs in 26 countries support families around the world. (statistc) (http://www.philarmh.org/who-we-are/history/). (McDonald's Corporation)
II.MAIN POINT (state as a single declarative sentence): The Ronald McDonald House Charities provide housing for people that need to stay near thier loved ones during difficult times.
A. SUBPOINT: There are 336 Ronald McDonald Houses (statistic)
1.
a. Many families travel far from home and spend several weeks or months to get treatment for their seriously ill or injured children.
b. A Ronald McDonald House is that “home-away-from-home” for families so they can stay close by their hospitalized child at little or no cost.
2.
a. These Houses are built on the idea that nothing else should matter when a family is focused on the health of their child – not where they can afford to stay or where they will get their next meal.
b. RMHC believes that when a child is hospitalized the love and support of family is as powerful as the strongest medicine prescribed. (McDonald’s Corporation).
B.SUBPOINT: At every House, families can enjoy:
1.
a. Home-cooked meals any time of the day.
b. Private bedrooms for sleep and privacy.
c. Playrooms for children.
C.SUBPOINT: Depending on the House, these special services may also be available:
1.
a. Special suites for children with suppressed immune systems
b. Recreational activities for children who need to be entertained for the day or distracted.
c. Sibling support services for siblings who need help coping with the situation at hand.
Transition: There are also Family Rooms in some hospitals that families can use to unwind in.
III.MAIN POINT (state as a single declarative sentence): 197 Ronald McDonald Family rooms (statistic)
A. SUBPOINT:
1.
a. When families walk into a Ronald McDonald Family Room, the idea is for them to forget they’re in a hospital.
b. Ronald McDonald Family Rooms offer a place to rest and regroup right at the hospital, just moments away from their sick child.
2.
a. They’ve found that staying close allows parents to better communicate with their children’s medical team.
B.SUBPOINT: some of the services found in RM Family Rooms include:
1.
a. A kitchen area for cooking meals.
b. Showering facilities for family members who need them.
c. Rooms to sleep in for naps or overnight stays.
d. Laundry facilities in case the stay is an extended one.
e. Internet access for kids with homework or parents who need it.
f. A quiet room in case people need somewhere to sit and think, or just some time to themselves.
CONCLUSION
LOGICAL CLOSURE (restate main points and thesis): The Ronald McDonald House provides stability and resources for families everywhere, whether it be providing housing that’s near a hospitalized child, the expense of staying together in another city, or even getting basic medical or dental care.
PSYCHOLOGICAL CLOSURE (relate importance and relevance to this audience):
Brenda Miller, executive director of the Ronald McDonald House in Des Moines Iowa said it best when she called it “a safe haven, it’s a refuge, it’s somewhere they can come and share their stories with other people that are going through similar instances. Their stories might be a little different, but they are all going through the same thing and they can pull strength from each other, and I think that in itself has got to be healing to the families on some level – to just be able to recharge physically, mentally and emotionally before going back to the hospital to endure what they need to endure.” (http://www.rmhdesmoines.org/news/).
CLINCHER (end with a bang, not a whimper):
The generosity of volunteers and donors make it all possible. In return, families either stay at no cost or are asked to make a donation up to $25 per day, depending on the House. The RMHC Global Policy is that families are never turned away; if it’s not possible to pay, the fee is waived.
If anyone is interested in making a donation or getting more detailed information, I can give you the website after class. Thank you!
References
McDonald’s Corporation. (2011). About Us. Ronald McDonald House Charities. Retrieved from http://www.rmhc.org/about-us
McDonald's Corporation. (2011). Welcome to The First House That Love Built. Philadelphia Ronald McDonald House. Retrieved from http://www.philarmh.org/who-we-are/history/
McDonald’s Corporation. (2011). What We Do. Ronald McDonald House Charities. Retrieved from http://www.rmhc.org/what-we-do
Simoens, M. (April 18, 2009). McDonald House is a ‘home away from home.’ Retrieved from http://www.rmhdesmoines.org/news/

[word/comments.xml]
Nitpicky, I know, but you are supposed to use hanging indents and double spacing within each citation.

## web_resources\Outline Samples\Social Issues Speech Outlines\Sample Informative Outline - Veggielution.docx
File size: 24486 bytes
Extracted DOCX text:
[word/document.xml]
(Placeholder1)TOPIC: VeggieloutionORGANIZATIONAL PATTERN (Chronological, Spatial, Topical, Problem Solution, Cause-Effect): TopicalSPECIFIC PURPOSE: To share information about VeggieloutionPRIMARY AUDIENCE OUTCOME: To understand how important Veggieloution is for the members of our communityTHESIS STATEMENT (state central idea, the essence of the speech): Veggieloution is a great start to spread awareness regarding the necessity of sustainable food sources.TOPIC: VeggieloutionORGANIZATIONAL PATTERN (Chronological, Spatial, Topical, Problem Solution, Cause-Effect): TopicalSPECIFIC PURPOSE: To share information about VeggieloutionPRIMARY AUDIENCE OUTCOME: To understand how important Veggieloution is for the members of our communityTHESIS STATEMENT (state central idea, the essence of the speech): Veggieloution is a great start to spread awareness regarding the necessity of sustainable food sources.
TOPIC: Veggieloution
ORGANIZATIONAL PATTERN (Chronological, Spatial, Topical, Problem Solution, Cause-Effect): Topical
SPECIFIC PURPOSE: To share information about Veggieloution
PRIMARY AUDIENCE OUTCOME: To understand how important Veggieloution is for the members of our community
THESIS STATEMENT (state central idea, the essence of the speech): Veggieloution is a great start to spread awareness regarding the necessity of sustainable food sources.
TOPIC: Veggieloution
ORGANIZATIONAL PATTERN (Chronological, Spatial, Topical, Problem Solution, Cause-Effect): Topical
SPECIFIC PURPOSE: To share information about Veggieloution
PRIMARY AUDIENCE OUTCOME: To understand how important Veggieloution is for the members of our community
THESIS STATEMENT (state central idea, the essence of the speech): Veggieloution is a great start to spread awareness regarding the necessity of sustainable food sources.
INTRODUCTION
ATTENTION GETTER: What is sustainable food? According to sustainabletable.org (2014), Sustainable food is basically the production of food, fiber, plants, and animal products produced using farming techniques that protect our environment, public health, human communities, & animal welfare.
PSYCHOLOGICAL ORIENTATION (relate topic to this audience): By a show of hands, who knows which fruits and vegetables are currently in season?
LOGICAL ORIENTATION (state thesis, then preview main points): With the overabundance of fast food restaurants and local grocery stores, it is only natural that we tend to lose our relationship with our natural environment. Veggieloution is a great organization that is geared toward strengthening the bond we should have with our food sources.
BODY
I.MAIN POINT (state as a single declarative sentence): The organization I would like to inform you of is Veggieloution.
A. Veggieloution is a community farm located in San Jose, CA.
1. The organization was founded in 2008 by SJSU students who wanted to garden but, could not due to their living in apartments with no open space.
a. The main purpose was to provide an area where members of the community could garden.
b. Working together side by side and eating a meal prepared with farm grown veggies after a morning of volunteering brings people of the community together.
2. The farm offers engaging activities for all age groups
a. With help from local parent advocacy groups, and donations; a variety of gardens have been planted at local schools.
b. Veggieloution volunteers, as well as, CSA volunteers help to maintain the school gardens & provide lessons on sustainable agriculture to students.
B. Veggieloutions mission is to empower youth and adults of the community with diverse backgrounds to create a sustainable food system in San Jose, CA.
1. Low income families are more likely to resort to fast food because it is highly advertised as a cheaper alternative and sometimes it is the only option due to income restraints.
a. According to the Business Insider (2011), In the US, McDonald’s serves 75 burgers per 1 second. McDonald’s, feeds 68 million people per day… that is 1% of world’s population.
b. In my opinion, these stats are a cause for concern and a contributing factor to the obesity epidemic the United States faces today.
2. The community farm started by Veggieloution provides access to healthy, organic, local food for volunteers as well as, the local community.
a. Veggieloution provides free vegetables for volunteers after each 2 hour work session.
b. It also provides free or low cost produce to low income families in the local area.
Transition: The ability to contribute to grow food you can eat, builds the relationship we have with our food sources. Strong bonds with the food we eat, will promote the value of sustainable eating.
II. MAIN POINT (state as a single declarative sentence): In 2011, the Emma Prusch Foundation gave Veggieloution 6 acres of land to expand the farm.
A. With the newly acquired land, Veggieloution was able to expand the farm and programs helped to start the first full growing season.
1. In 2013 56,133 pounds of organic vegetables were harvested at Veggieloution.
a. Veggie Vouchers were given to families with children who are at risk of getting Diabetes, or diet related diseases.
b. 20,568 pounds of vegetables were distributed to the community free of charge.
B. With the help of donations and land, education programs for adults and youth have been created.
1. With the expansion of land, Veggieloutions first half acre plot was turned into a youth garden.
a. During farm hours, families can bring children to the youth garden where they are educated on the importance of gardens and sustainable food.
b. While at the youth garden, kids are given the opportunity to work in the garden as well as feed the free range chickens.
2. Adult cooking classes are also offered
a. The classes teach how to promote a healthy lifestyle, how to read labels, and spread awareness of the availability of local food throughout the community.
b. Cooking classes are also provided to encourage mothers to serve healthy meals to their children.
Transition: Teaching people how to prepare local produce will help change the culture of how we eat.
III.MAIN POINT (state as a single declarative sentence): What Veggieloution hopes to achieve in the future.
A. SUBPOINT: The organization’s goal for the future is to enable people to learn tools that will help them live and maintain a healthy lifestyle.
1. According to sustainabletable.org (2014), eating sustainably provides many health benefits.
a. Eating organic produce and meat decreases your exposure to pesticides, antibiotic-resistant bacteria, and food additives.
b. By protecting yourself from harmful substances, you are also supporting a more environmentally friendly food system.
CONCLUSION
LOGICAL CLOSURE (restate main points and thesis): Organizations like Veggieloution are essential to improve the health of our community and environment.
PSYCHOLOGICAL CLOSURE (relate importance and relevance to this audience): Industrial agriculture is unhealthy-for our environment, our climate, our bodies, and our rural communities.
CLINCHER (end with a bang, not a whimper): We must band together and make insightful decisions when deciding what to eat.
Reference List
UCS Food and Environment Program. (2012). Toward Healthy Food and Farms: How Science-Based Policies Can Transform Agriculture. Union of Concerned Scientists. Retrieved from http://www.ucsusa.org/food_and_agriculture/
Lubin, Gus and Mamta Badkar. (11-25-11). 15 Facts About McDonald's That Will Blow Your Mind. Business Insider. Retrieved from http://www.businessinsider.com/facts-about-mcdonalds-blow-your-mind-2011
Brighid, Dawn. (2014). Sustainable Agriculture-The Basics. Grace Communications Foundation, 246. Retrieved from http://www.sustainabletable.org/246/sustainable-agriculture-the-basics
Veggielouton. (2014) What We Do. Retrieved from veggielution.org/what-we-do/

[word/comments.xml]
The formatting of the reference page is off just a bit. Everything is supposed to be in alphabetical order, use double spacing within each individual citation, and *not* be centered.

## web_resources\Outline Samples\Values Speech Outlines\Core Values Outline Sample 3.pdf
File size: 72647 bytes
PDF pages: 3
Metadata /Title: OUTLINE WORKSHEET
Metadata /Author: Julia Madden
Metadata /Creator: Microsoft® Word 2019
Metadata /CreationDate: D:20241203165657-08'00'
Metadata /ModDate: D:20241203165657-08'00'
Metadata /Producer: Microsoft® Word 2019

[PDF page 1]
TOPIC: What’s at my core

SPECIFIC PURPOSE: To share what my core values are and why they are important

AUDIENCE OUTCOME: I hope for the audience, my classmates, to better understand me.
Additionally, I hope that they agree with at least some of what I have to say and personify
some of my ideas.

INTRODUCTION

ATTENTION GETTER: Humans are social creatures. It’s in our nature to build
meaningful relationships with others. Through these relationships we discover who we are.
How someone chooses to treat those who are important to them can be illuminating.

PSYCHOLOGICAL ORIENTATION:
It’s cliché for a reason, but actions speak louder than words.

THESIS STATEMENT: Wholesome and meaningful relationships are built on
dependability, trust, and respect.

LOGICAL ORIENTATION:

Dependability, trust, and respect
BODY

I. These three values are interconnected (all symbolized by a chain)

A. Dependability - you can depend on a good chain to hold. I’ve spent a lot of time on
forklifts in and around trucks with different types of ramps, and the failsafe for
when things start to move away from each other is a set of chains. At work, my
safety often depends on a chain. Thinking of this chain as a team of people,
each person depends on the other people in order for the entire chain to
function as it’s supposed to.

1. The quality of being trustworthy and reliable
a. Here in the definition is the word trust
b. You can’t be dependable if you can’t be trusted
c. If trust is broken, you can no longer be depended on for the truth.
2. Being dependable requires respect
a. You need to respect time, yours and others’
i. Think of work relationships (employer/employee)
b. You need to respect the needs of others to be dependable
i. If you’re selfish you can’t recognize when others’ needs are
more important than yours, and can’t be depended on for support

[PDF page 2]
B. Trust - I know I can depend on the chain, I trust it. Again, if these were people I
know I can trust them to be a link in the chain, just as I am.

1. Trust is something that is earned.
a. It requires some measure of dependability to tell the truth, or act in a
way that does not betray a relationship (like stealing from a workplace). It takes time to build.
b. It also requires you to respect the other member(s) of your
relationship. Stealing from work is disrespectful to your employer, just like lying is
disrespectful to the other person. Lying only serves yourself, protecting yourself from facing
the consequences of the truth, whatever it may be.
2. It is extremely difficult to earn back trust once it is broken.
a. There is no formula of dependable truth per amount of time to earn
trust back. Depending on what the betrayal was, it may never be earned back.
b. Often when a lie comes out, multiple other lies that were used to
cover up the original lie also surface. This just happened to me, and after thinking about all of
the lies that were told and the way that they were told, I’m not sure I can ever believe a word
that comes out of the mouth of the person that lied to me.

C. Respect

1. You don’t have to look up to someone to respect them. – If we’re all links in
the chain, none of us are all that significant. Still, I can respect you by doing my job in the
chain.
a. Being honest with someone is a form of respect.
b. Being a dependable person in a relationship is a form of respect.
2. Respect in relationships is given as much as it is earned.
a. You have to approach relationships willing to be respectful. Being
trustworthy and dependable are the two fastest ways to build a meaningful relationship.
b. Being respectful towards another person will earn you trust and
respect in turn.

CONCLUSION

LOGICAL CLOSURE: I have felt this way about relationships for a long time, but this recent
betrayal by someone close to me has caused me to do a lot of reflecting. It’s clear to me after
having my trust broken, that I can no longer depend on this individual, and that they don’t
respect me. Without a doubt these three values are interdependent.

PSYCHOLOGICAL CLOSURE: If you value a relationship, approach it with respect. Be
honest, trustworthy, and dependable and you will be rewarded with quality relationships.

CLINCHER: I hope all of you take this to heart, because I don’t want anyone to have to feel
the way I did when I discovered I was being lied to. Also, I planned on talking about different

[PDF page 3]
values. I was definitely going to talk about optimism and humor, which might have been more
fun, and maybe less depressing. So, be dependable, trustworthy, and respectful to your
relationships and you might just be rewarded more entertaining speeches.

## web_resources\Outline Samples\Values Speech Outlines\Sample Outline 1 - The Perfect Apple.pdf
File size: 80624 bytes
PDF pages: 4
Metadata /Title: OUTLINE WORKSHEET
Metadata /Author: Julia Madden
Metadata /Creator: Microsoft® Word 2019
Metadata /CreationDate: D:20241203165505-08'00'
Metadata /ModDate: D:20241203165505-08'00'
Metadata /Producer: Microsoft® Word 2019

[PDF page 1]
TOPIC: Harvesting the Perfect Apple

SPECIFIC PURPOSE: The goal of this presentation is to give the audience a peek into
what core values mean to me. I aim to share some of my inner most treasures, my core
values…

AUDIENCE OUTCOME: In this speech I hope the audience connects to some of the values
I consider to be almost part of my genetic makeup. The idea is that the audience will be
inspired by my values and search their own hearts for their deepest core values. I hope that in
sharing how important I believe Compassion, honesty and Service are to me that the audience
will feel evoked to become more honest, compassionate and consider getting involved in the
service of others.

INTRODUCTION

ATTENTION GETTER: There is literally nothing better than a fresh, crisp juicy apple. In
fact, most times I find myself scraping all the meat off of this amazing fruit until there is
literally almost nothing left. I scrape all the way down to the innermost core until the piece of
fruit that used to be a plump round shining apple, is nothing more than a small stem and bits
of core. The apple is literally dead, and I don’t stop until I get to seeds, right when I’ve
completely pulverized the apple to death the very thing that gives the apple new life makes its
way out of the apple and I know I must stop eating. The seeds…..

PSYCHOLOGICAL ORIENTATION: At the very core of our beings is where you can
find the very things that are so important to us that they are essential to our survival. The
things that permeate from us as you get to the very center of what we are made of.

THESIS STATEMENT: Core values are passed down from family, they are gained with
wisdom, they are accumulated culturally, and harvested throughout the development and
growth of life.

LOGICAL ORIENTATION: At my core you will find traits such as, compassion, service
and honesty spilling over with importance. These traits I feel I must produce and live by for
my own survival, they are literally as important to my well-being as my heartbeat.

BODY

I. MAIN POINT: For me compassion is the situations of other people that break your
heart and cause you to feel the need to do something to contribute help in these situations. By
definition in Webster’s dictionary compassion is sympathetic consciousness of others distress
together with a desire to alleviate it.

[PDF page 2]
A. SUBPOINT: Extreme poverty and lack of resources in other countries drives a
giant hole in my heart making me feel responsible for cultivating change and
using my resources to help people that suffer from lack of food, education and
opportunity. I also endure extreme empathy for mentally ill and or addicted
and ever so rapidly growing homeless population in our very own valley.

1. Company which I support children with sponsorship: Compassion
International
a. Benon- 18 graduated Uganda
b. Victoria- 9 Nicaragua
c. Steven- 7 Nicaragua
2. Inclement Weather Shelter
a. housing 8 men in the Silicon Valley every night
b. food, showers and safe shelter

Transition: Compassion for me ties right into my next core value which is service.

II. MAIN POINT: Service to me is giving freely of yourself to benefit others. Making
yourself a servant for others benefit.

A. SUBPOINT: Service requires giving of yourself and humbling yourself to give
back to others. Serving homeless and people in other countries is amazing and
honorable but being a steward of service in all we do is a a pivotal trait that
would shift humanity.

1. Serving others on the freeway
a. letting people in
b. eliminating road rage
2. serving by manors
a. opening doors
b being aware of others existence

B. SUBPOINT: Serving can start in your own home and workplace

1. Honoring your family
a. doing dishes that aren’t yours
b. making dinner and treating people with love
2. Showing love and appreciation to coworkers or employees
a. acknowledges a job well done
b. plan a good dead for a friend

C. SUBPOINT: Finding places that you can devote time to giving back to others

1. Serving at church
a. Ushering team that greets people and makes new people feel
welcome
b. outreach ministry that goes into our community and looks for people
in need of a church community that can support and love them exactly where they are at.

[PDF page 3]
2. plug in to an organization and give some of your time
a. soup kitchen
b. coat closet

Transition: When we have given back and we feel our service has been offered we can hold
our head high and feel like we are making an impact on our world and issues that break our
heart. We take charge of tackling hard issues and give freely of ourselves. Giving of myself
only comes when I am strong enough mentally and physically to execute these tasks. I’ve
taken care of my mental and physical health in order to serve others better. One of the ways
that I can stay mentally healthy is not having junk bog me down such as being dishonest.

III. MAIN POINT: Honest strengthens my core and makes it so I can move freely because
I never have to think of truths. My truths are facts and I don’t have to waiver. People can
count on me and I have built a solid repour.

A. SUBPOINT Lying and being dishonest only robs others of their ability to
choose their own path and makes the perpetrator stumble.

1. a partner that isn’t upright about who they are
a. opposite partner is stuck with someone they wouldn’t chose do to
being lied to.
b. people self-worth is shattered by others lies and deceit.
2. a child who lies about where they are going to be
a. puts themselves in danger
b. breaks trust of a parent

CONCLUSION

LOGICAL CLOSURE: We want to harvest qualities in our core that create growth. When we
are honest and upright not only are we clear minded and sure footed, but people can trust in
us. When we have strength in our character, we can give to others freely and help assist in
other growth. Giving in this capacity pays back ten-fold

PSYCHOLOGICAL CLOSURE: I hope these qualities that I find at my deepest depths are
also things you can connect with and resonate with you so that we can come together and
empower each other to do better by humans and ourselves by having clout and substance.
When everything else in the world seems like it’s falling apart, we still have the ability to
have our own values.

CLINCHER: Martin Luther says it best “Even if I knew that tomorrow the world would go to
pieces, I would still plant my apple tree.”

[PDF page 4]
References

Dictionary by Merriam-Webster: America’s most trusted online dictionary (n.d) Retrieved
January 20, 20 AD from https://www.merriam-webster.com/Compassion

Martin Luther Quote. (n.d.) Retrieved January 20, 20AD from
https://www.azquotes.com/180782?red=apples

## web_resources\Outline Samples\Values Speech Outlines\Values Outline Sample 2.pdf
File size: 97983 bytes
PDF pages: 5
Metadata /Author: Brandon
Metadata /Creator: Microsoft® Word 2019
Metadata /CreationDate: D:20241203165603-08'00'
Metadata /ModDate: D:20241203165603-08'00'
Metadata /Producer: Microsoft® Word 2019

[PDF page 1]
TOPIC: What Shaped My Values

SPECIFIC PURPOSE: The main purpose is to invite my audience to see what shaped me as
a person and how I latched onto the values I did to both survive hard times and thrive in spite
of it.

AUDIENCE OUTCOME: The audience should look at this as both informative and
hopefully inspiring. My speech allows everyone to take a brief stroll in my shoes, they are
invited to see my viewpoints and life experiences. Ideally I’d want them to feel inspired, to
always push forward and never give up, even when life throws you judgment, hardships, and
seemingly impossible situations.

INTRODUCTION

ATTENTION GETTER: Who would have guessed between the ages of nine and fifteen I
would learn some of life's biggest lessons that would carry me as a person and define my
values.

PSYCHOLOGICAL ORIENTATION: I feel like everyone has gone through trials in life,
perhaps it will make someone feel not so alone, give someone some motivation, or just be a
bit of food for thought.

THESIS STATEMENT: Understanding what is important to us as individuals and the roads
we took to get there, are fundamental in knowing who we are and where we stand in the
world.

LOGICAL ORIENTATION: The main points of my presentation will give you a short
glimpse of how I adopted my values. I gained them through events that shaped me to be open
minded in spite of close minded influence, independant when I needed to be strong for others,
and persevere even when life got turbulent.

BODY

I. My first core value is open mindedness, it will be represented by a thistle I got from
Oregon.

A. Why did I choose this object?

1. It always reminds me how I was treated in a different state.
a. Moved to Oregon had to deal with racism and discrimination

[PDF page 2]
b. hard to make friends there due to my race
c.Pregnant in high school

B. Why is this value important to me?

1.Gainded perspective and tolerance
a.never treat others poorly
b.no judgement

2.willing to try new and different things in life
a.anything from food to parenting advice

C. How does this value relate to me or shape my life?

1.Made me a better problem solver
a.able to see different perspectives

2.Made me a better mother
a.understand my kids wants/needs
b.creative rule enforcement

Transition: This next value was key to growing up and being dependable

II. For my second core value I chose is independence, which will be represented by my
one way ticket from Oregon back to California.

A. Why did I choose this object?

1.It reminds me why I came back to california
a.to give my daughter the best life

2.It reminded me be self reliant
a.get a job
b.learn how to adult

B. Why is this value important to me?

1.Helps me make strong and decisive decisions
a.Be in charge of a family
b.Make medical decisions

2.Made me comfortable being on my own

[PDF page 3]
a.Huge family to just my daughter and I

C. How does this value relate to me or shape my life?

1.I create my own happiness
a.security
b.finding my voice

2.I never live in someone else's shadow
a.broke free of others
b.figured out what I wanted

Transition: And lastly this value drove all my ambitions

III. Perseverance is my final core value, this will be represented by my High School
Diploma.

A. Why did I choose this object?

1.Its a symbol of what I accomplished by never giving up
a.Achieved my HS diploma
b.enrolled in college

2.I wanted to prove how important dreams are to my kids
a.always stressed the importance of education
b.told them we could be anything

B. Why is this value important to me?

1.It keeps me focused on important goals
a.kept me forward thinking

2.Its Important for my drive to succeed in college
a. Get into nursing school

C. How does this value relate to me or shape my life

1.Its still shaping my life
a.school
b.own my own property independent of anyone else
2.Helping me to keep going
a. Even though Im sick

[PDF page 4]
CONCLUSION

LOGICAL CLOSURE: There are many qualities and values that define me, these three stand
out the strongest. Understanding what is important to us as individuals and the roads we took
to get there, are fundamental in knowing who we are and were we stand in the world.

PSYCHOLOGICAL CLOSURE: I believe that everyone has it in them to possess these three
qualities, I hope in explaining how they helped me, you might see how they can help you
accomplish personal goals in your own lives or be a little more understanding and open.

CLINCHER: Thank you for your time, this concludes my speech

[PDF page 5]
References
Selig, M. (2020). 6 Ways to Discover and Choose Your Core Values. Retrieved from
https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/blog/changepower/201811/6-ways-discover-and-
choose-your-core-values
